LAND SALE.
AT eleven o'clock on Wednesday the 14th
of June, the following portions of Crown Land
will be offered for sale by public auction, at
the Colonial Treasury, Sydney, at the upset
price affixed to each lot respectively. Deposit
10 per cent.
COUNTRY LOTS.
ROXBURGH. - 1. 40 acres, parish of Thorns¬
hope, near Mount Lambie, commencing on
Deadman's Creek, about half a mile south
from a measured portion of 109 acres, on the
north side of the Bathurst Road. 2. 44 acres,
same place. 3. 30 acres, parish of Falnash,
commencing at a stake on Meadow Flat
Creek, Upset price 20s. per acre.
ARGYLE.- 4. 40 acres at Jerralong, near
Windellama, commencing at a marked gum¬
tree on a line bearing south 56½ degrees east
240 chains from the confluence of Budjong
Creek with Nadjingomar Creek. 5. 60 acres
near Windellama Creek, commencing at a
marked gum-tree 23 chains north of the north-
east corner of a measured portion of 200 acres
on the north side of Windellama Creek. 7.
40 acres, near Windellama, commencing at
Nadlingomar Creek, near its confluence with
Budjong Creek. 8. 42 acres, same place. 9.
44 acres, same place. 10. 40 acres, same
place. Upset price 20s. per acre.
CAMDEN.- 6. 106 acres, at Dapto, Illawarra.
Upset price, 20s. per acre.
SUBURBAN ALLOTMENTS.
MACQUARIE.- 1. 2. 5 acres each, Nos. 1 and
2 of section No. 47, at Port Macquarie. 3, 4.
4 acres and 2 roods each, Nos. 3 and 4 of sec¬
tion 47. Upset price, £5 per acre.
BATHURST. - 5. 16 acres, parish of Bathurst,
No. 1. 6,7. 9 acres each, same place, Nos.
2 and 3. 8. 18 acres, same place, No. 4. 9.
16 acres, same place, No. 5. 10,11. 9 acres
each, Nos. 6, and 7. 12. 18 acres, same
place, No 8. Upset price, £2 per acre.
NORTHUMBERLAND.- 13,14. 50 acres each,
parish of Stockrington, Nos. 1 and 2 15-18.
52 acres each, same place, Nos. 3 and 6. Upset
price , £1 10s. per acre.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
BERRIMA.
DISTRICT COUNCILS, - This institution appears
to have fallen into disrepute, for on the last no¬
mination day, the Warden, after remaining in
Court for some hours to receive the nomination
of candidates for office, had to close the Court
without hearing or seeing a single elector come
forward. The vacancies will, therefore, (as
last year), be filled up by His Excellency's no¬
mination.
ELECTORAL LIST For 1850-1. - The magis¬
trates, last week, revised the list of electors, at
the end of which business there were found to
be one hundred names on the roll, being an in¬
crease of one name since last revision day.
MINERALS. - The mania for mines appear to
have affected some of the worthy folk in this
neighbourhood-we were lately shown a piece
of copper smelted from some ore said to have
been found near Berrima, and if the quantity is
only equal to the quality we saw, there can be
no doubt of the richness of the mineral. We
were not let into the secret of the locality the
ore was found in, but we expect shortly to hear
further news.
HAWKERS. - Some of this class of dealers
have adopted a plan which they fancy shelters
them from any fear of the law. They are in
the habit of proceeding from town to town,
stopping a week or so in each place, and rent¬
ing a room in one of the inns, by this means
they contend they are placed on the same foot¬
ing with the legitimate traders or shopkeepers.
One of them lately attempted the trick here, but
did not stop, in consequence, we believe, of the
Chief Constable warning him that an informa¬
tion would be lodged against him. If the
Chief Constables throughout the colony would
act similarly, an end would be put to this
system, which, independently of the facility it
affords for disposing of stolen property, is a
source of great pecuniary injury to country
storekeepers in general. Publicans do not
appear cognizant of the fact that they are liable
to a fine by allowing any portion of their
licensed premises to be thus occupied - Verbum
Sap.
POLICE FORCE. - We understand that the
magistrates here have taken up the question of
" want of sufficient police force," which ap¬
pears to be so generally agitated throughout
the colony at the present time. If so there can
be no doubt but that the superior knowledge
which they possess of the district will ensure
immediate attention from the Executive to
whatever report they may have made on the
question-considering the apathy that is gene¬
rally displayed in matters of importance by the
people of this district. The magistrates de¬
serve the thanks of the inhabitants for the
spontaneous movement they have made in this
matter.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE GOLD FIELDS
TUENA CREEK
(From the Bathurst Free Press)
Our intelligence from Tuena Creek is of a very
encouraging character. Although fewer in
number the diggers are certainly more pro¬
sperous than at any previous time, and we
believe a larger number of licenses than on
any former occasion have been obtained by
Mr. Commissioner Green during, his recent
trip. No more convincing proof of their
general good luck can be afforded than in the
fact that their licenses were cheerfully paid.
In every case where common industry is
exercised good wages are obtained by the
Tuena diggers, but large earnings are by no
means uncommon. Many parties are making
two to four ounces of gold per day. Mr. Lee's
party have been making sometimes three and
frequently four ounces per day on Sheep
Station Point, and others are equally success¬
ful.
Since Tuena was made a Police station
matters have proceeded much more peaceably
than was formerly the case. Although not
suppressed, sly-grog-selling is conducted in a
more stealthy manner, and public decency a
-little more respected. Mr. Fox, chief consta¬
ble of Carcoar, accompanied by one or two of
his subordinates, visited the place a short time
ago, and squared accounts with a few of the
illicit tapsters.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MORETON BAY.
WE have the Moreton Bay Courier of the 3rd instant.
The following are extracts therefrom :
VALUE OF PROPERTY - We are informed that Messrs.
Mort and Co. have sold the Rose Vale Station,
together with 15,000 sheep, the property of Mr. John
Brewster, J.P., to Messrs. Rolleston and Leslie, for
the sum of £9000.
NEW BANK IN BRISBANE. - We have the best ground
for belief that the branch of the Joint Stock Banking
Company, some time since talked of as about to be
established here, will be in operation forthwith - per¬
haps next month.
CHURCH OF ENGLAND SCHOOL. - We understand the
Rev. Mr Ye tma is making efforts to raise funds for
the erection of a new school-house for the use of the
children belonging to St. John's congregation. The
present building is almost in a state of dilapidation,
and a sum of £400 will be required, according to the
estimate made for the new building
BRISBANE HOSPITAL AND ITS LIBRARY.- State of
the Hospital for the month of April. - Remaining in
Hospital on the 1st of the month, 29 ; since admitted,
10; discharged during the month. 17. As present in
Hospital, 23, - of these 17 are paupers, and 6 are pay
patients. - The Committee, by means of a vote of a
small amount, have established the nucleus of a li¬
brary, for the use of the patients. The books, we
learn, arrived last steamer from Messrs Waugh and
Cox, who, in consideration of the object for which
they were intended, very kindly made a considerable
reduction in their price. The works are well adapted
to their intended purpose, being of a light amusing des¬
cription ; they include the works of the Messrs. Cham¬
bers', Hogg's Instructor, Dickens' Household Words,
&c., &c. The Treasurer, Mr. W. A. Duncan, to whom
the books were consigned, in forwarding them to the
Hospital, made a handsome and valuable addition to
the stock from his own library, promising to make an
additional contribution, should the patients appreciate
the efforts that are thus being made to amuse and
instruct them.
STREET IMPROVEMENTS.- We observe that a con¬
siderable quantity of stones, the carting of which was
sometime since contracted for, for the repair of the
streets, has already been broken at the gaol. This, we
believe, is the first instance of these said-to-be hard
labour men, being profitably employed ; and with the
many inconveniences and difficulties in the way great
progress seems to be making. By our last gaol report
it appears there are not a dozen hard labour men con¬
fined at present, and considering that these have to
first wheel the stones in, in the rough state, from out¬
side the building, and again to take them out by the
same means when broken, they are passing them
through their hands at a rate which shows that the
gaol officials are not neglecting their duty. The broken
stones seem to contain an extraordinary quantity of
pulverised matter. It is evident they are produced
from surface stones : indeed this is apparent from the
rough stones in the bulk. Why these should be
selected for, macadamising purposes it is difficult to
say; as it is well known that they have lost their
durable hard flinty nature, by long exposure to the
action of the atmosphere. We understand that the
entire sum of £400, voted for labour, repairing the
streets, &c., will be spent in producing the materials
for the streets. No doubt this is a judicious step, as it
is not likely that means will fail to apply the material
when prepared. The sum named will, at the contract
price, produce 2000 tons of stones - less the incidental
expenses. This quantity of material should mac¬
adamise the greater portion of our streets.
DRAYTON, April 23.-The elections are now over,
and it is a happiness to the inhabitants ; . before they
came there was nothing but canvassing and tormenting
the electors day after day, in favour of one or other of
the candidates. The two settlements are going ahead
rapidly. In the course of two or three years, Ipswich
will be nothing in comparison to it. At the Swamp,
in particular, houses are erecting week after week ;
there is a great cry out for land there. One gentleman
residing at the Swamp is selling small portions of his
estate as high as £100 per acre. Respecting the old
town of Drayton, or West Drayton as it will be most
likely called, it is not dying away as people formerly
thought. Here we have several new buildings erected
lately, and now as it is ascertained beyond a doubt
that there can be a good supply of water got at that
place people that have purchased there, consider their
properties 100 per cent, more in value within the last
eight weeks. There has been a well sunk on some
town land belonging to Mr. William Handcock.
When the spring was opened at the depth of 56 feet,
the water rose 28 feet, and continues at this height. It
is as fine drinking water as can be found in the colony.
Remarkable to relate, during the sinking, there was
not a stone found, it was all a solid mass of decayed
timber, in some places resembling coal. This well is
in the centre of the town of Drayton.

MUNICIPAL COUNCIL
A Meeting of the Municipal Council of Sydney will be held at the
Town Hall, this day, at 3 o'clock in the afternoon, for considering
the following
NOTICES OF MOTION.
By Alderman WILLIAMS : That the Report of the Finance Com¬
mittee, received this day, recommending the payment of sundry
accounts, be adopted.
By Alderman WILLIAMS : That this Council do now proceed to
open and determine on the undermentioned tenders, viz. : ¬
Curbing and guttering Bathurst-street, between George and
Sussex streets ; supply of gravel from Domain ; supply of blue
stone metal for the year 1858 ; construction of thirty-eight gullies
in Lower George-street ; supply of nine street watering carts ;
fencing along line of water main from Botany to Sydney ; supply
of piles for Water Works during 1858.
By Alderman THURLOW : That the Memorial of Messrs. Robert
Towns and Co., presented to the Council on the 21st December, be
now taken into consideration, and the prayer thereof be granted,
or such other order be made therein as this Council deems equit¬
able and proper to be done in the matter of the said petition.
By Alderman OATLEY : That Alderman Thornton be, and he is
hereby appointed as, a member of the, Finance and By-Laws Com¬
mittee.
By Alderman ROBERTS : That the following resolution passed at
a meeting of the Aldermen for the City of Sydney, on the 31st De¬
cember last, be recorded in the minute-book of this Council, viz. :
That this Council desires to record their approval of the efficient and
satisfactory manner in which George Thornton, Esq., has dis¬
charged the many important duties attaching to the office of
Mayor for the City of Sydney, during the year 1857.
By Alderman MURPHY : That a supply of water be granted to
the residents on the southern side of Cleveland-street, Chippen¬
dale, from the main lately laid in that locality, at the usual charge
of five shillings per room.
By Alderman MURPHY : That it be an instruction to the Im¬
provement Committee to consider the propriety of an application
being made to the Government to contribute some portion of the
expense of continuing the culvert at the entrance to the Railway
Station, from its present eastern termination to the entrance to
the Goods Station in Devonshire-street.
By Alderman NORTHWOOD : That the Improvement Committee
be instructed to report upon the works necessary for the extension
of the Market Wharf into deep water, and for otherwise improving
that property; and to furnish plan, specification for, and estimate
of the cost of carrying out such works.
By Alderman THORNTON : That the Resolutions of Council of
date the 4th May and 9th November last, referring the subject of
the construction of Public Baths to the Improvement Committee
for their report, be, and they are hereby, rescinded ; and that a
Special Committee consisting of six members be appointed by
ballot, for the purpose of considering the urgent necessity for the
immediate formation of a convenient bathing place or places in the
Domain.
By Alderman WILLIAMS : That the Report of the Finance Com¬
mittee received by the Council on the 31st December, recommend¬
ing the purchase of a cargo of Gabo Island granite, be adopted.
By Alderman WILLIAMS : That the report of the Finance Com¬
mittee, received by the Council on the 31st December, on petition
from the labourers at Botany Water Works, praying that they
might be allowed two days during the Christmas holidays, and
that their wages in the meantime should not be stopped, be
approved.
By Alderman SUTHERLAND : That application be made to the
Government to introduce a bill to extend the operations under the
Sydney Paving Act to Parramatta-street, and that portion of
King-street not included in the said Act.
By Alderman MURPHY : That Alderman Northwood be ap¬
pointed a member of the Water and Lighting Committee.
By Alderman MURPHY : That the Finance Committee be in¬
structed to enquire into the circumstances under which the Pyr¬
mont Bridge Company have taken possession of a portion of the
Market Wharf for the abutment theron of the bridge ; and to
report to the Council with a view to some compensation being ob¬
tained for the city, as provided by the Pyrmont Bridge Company's
Act.
By Alderman WILLIAMS : That the sureties proposed by Messrs.
Lane and Co., Shoobert and Sinclair, for the due performance of
their contracts for the supply of general stores, supply of coals and
works in Erskine-street, be accepted.
LYCEUM THEATRE. - Among the amusements of this city the
San Francisco Minstrels continue to take the lead. The careful
selection of tho programme, the almost nightly change of songs,
dances, and burlesques, together with the superiority of the enter¬
tainment over rival amusements, are causes which account for
the popularity of this company. The benefit of Mr. Demerest,
on the evening of Monday last, was well patronised, and also Mr.
Burbank's, who appealed to his friends on Saturday night. Mr.
D. F. Boley takes his benefit this evening.
PRINCE OF WALES THEATRE. - On Monday evening last, Mons.
W. Kholer, the Wizard of the North, commenced an engagement,
assisted by a stout young lady in a white muslin dress, which
would have touched the boards of the stage, had the garment been
extended some twelve or fourteen inches. The performances of
this artiste were of a peculiar character. Madame Flora, who is
called the Fire Queen, appeared to partake of the nature of a
salamander. She took off her shoes and danced upon a sheet of
hot iron; and with a large spoon ate flames of fire, with an avidity
and appetite as great as that which attends the demolition of a
lolly by a ravenous infant. Mons. Kholer's performances, if not
very original, were certainly clever, and his skill in the art of
legerdemain is considerable.

GOLD COMMISSIONER'S OFFICE
Stoney Creek, 1st July, 1858
Sir,—I do myself the honour to submit my half¬
yearly report on the district under my charge.
2. During the past month I have visited the gold¬
fields at Ophir, Burrendong, Merindee, and the Lower
Meroo, for the purpose of issuing miners' rights and
business licenses. At the first named locality there
are about sixty miners working in the bed of the
Summerhill and Ophir Creeks, many of whom during
the late dry weather were well repaid for their la¬
bour. Some parties are now engaged in testing, by
deep sinking, the surrounding ranges. At Burrendong
there are about 200 miners, the majority of whom
are engaged on a quartz vein lately opened in that
neighbourhood. A few only have been successful; for
this branch of gold mining is too laborious, and re¬
quires an amount of capital which the ordinary digger
is enable to command.
Merindee and the Lower Meroo, lately attached to
this district, contain a population of about 400 miners,
of whom one-third are Chinese; most of them are at
present opening up new ground on the Meroo — with
what degree of success I have been unable as yet to
ascertain.
The Ironbark and Stoney Creek are still, as stated in
my last report, principally occupied by Chinese, They
have erected three stores, and obtained large supplies
from Sydney, from which I think it probable that a
considerable addition to their number may shortly
be expected. No difficulty has as yet arisen between
them and the European miners, although in some in¬
stances complaints have been made of their tendency
to waste water. Their general conduct has been
remarkably peaceable and orderly. At the quartz
reef, several parties are still sinking, with every pros¬
pect of success; and I have already received five
applications for leases of 100 yards of this and other
veins in the vicinity.
3. I estimate the mining and business population of
this district at about 1200, of whom 400 are Chinese.
There have been issued since the 1st January, 491
miners' rights, and 43 business licenses; the disparity
of these numbers with that of the estimated population
is accounted for by the fact that nearly all the Chinese
having passed through other gold-fields were provided
with miners' rights on their arrival here, and
that many of the licenses of the European population
will not be due until later in the year. The amount of
gold forwarded by escort during the half-year was
4127 ozs. 13 dwts. 7 grs.; the small quantity sent is
chiefly attributable to the very dry weather during
the summer, which prevented a large portion of this
gold-field from being profitably worked; and also from
the fact, that the gold obtained at Ophir, Menindee, and
the Lower Meroo, does not pass through the Stoney
Creek escort—the former being sent to Orange, and
the latter to Louisa Creek.
I have. &c.,
WHITTINGDALE JOHNSON,
Acting Assistant Gold Commissioner.
The Commissioner in charge of the Western Gold¬
fields, Sofala.
Louisa Creek, 3rd July, 1858.
Sir, - I do myself the honour to report on the Meroo
and Tambaroora Gold-fields, for the past quarter.
I have issued, for the three mouths ending the 30th
of June—miners' rights, 2397; traders' licenses, 89.
The amount of gold transmitted by escort during
the same period was 19,209 ozs. 15 dwts. 6 grs., to
which may be added the amount left at this station by
the Mudgee escort, viz., 3465 ozs. 7 dwts. 3 grs., all
of which is the produce of this district; making a
total of 22,675 ozs. 2 dwts. 8 grs.
By the above returns it will be seen that the gold¬
fields still continue in a very flourishing condition,
and from their permanent character and extent, there
can be little doubt that they will continue so.
The Chinese population continue quiet, and, with
the exception of giving great trouble in the collection
of their license fees, are well conducted.
The European miners are generally contented, and
doing well. They bear very little good-will towards
the Chinese, but I apprehend no serious disturbance
among them at present.
The men under my command, with one exception,
have been steady and well behaved.
The horses, though poor, are still healthy, and,
barring one, sound.
I have, &c.,
W. BROWNE,
Assistant Gold Commissioner.
The Commissioner in charge of the Western Gold¬
fields, Sofala.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CAMDEN.
A correspondent, dating Camden, Monday, writes
as follows :- "This flood is fully four feet higher than
the last, and in consequence we have taken shelter
in the upper floor. The destruction of property is
immense. Furniture may be seen floating about in
all directions ; haystacks, beehives, water casks, pigs,
and poultry are also drifting about. Many houses in
the lower parts of the town have been completely
under water, and one or two have fallen down. The
water was five feet in Mr. Baisden's house, and has
spoiled seventy thousand bricks, just ready for the
kiln. We have not had communication with Sydney
since Friday.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
To the Editor of the Herald.
Sir, - As you have, in so honest and consistent a manner,
upheld the cause of justice and Christian humanity in
behalf of the poor oppressed Chinese at Lambing Flat and
the neighbourhood, I hope you will kindly allow space for
these few lines. I have waited for some abler pen than
mine to take the subject in hand, as well as to see if the
accounts were really exaggerated, but as I find with grief
and horror by to-day's Herald, and from your own reporter,
that they were by no means so - unhappily, rather under¬
stated, I wish to enquire whether a public meeting should
not at once be convened (which I should hope a sense of
duty will compel our worthy Mayor to call), so as to wash
our hands, as far as we can, from this foul blot on our
beloved country, by thus testifying our abhorrence of the
heartrending cruelties perpetrated on the unoffending and
industrious Chinese ; also to try to raise some support and
succour for these poor victims during this inclement
weather. This is surely a subject on which to awaken
Government (if, strictly speaking, we have one), as well as
to arouse all professing Christians and philanthropists
especially all Christian ministers - to follow your excellent
example, and speak plainly out on the subject.
I remain, sir, yours very respectfully,
J. P.
July 9th.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
TO THE EDITOR OF THE HERALD.
SIR,—The following is a cure for the worm-disease in
sheep:—As the worms are generated by a deteriorated
state of the blood, owing to feeding for many months
in abundant succulent herbage, and, therefore, na¬
turally lying down the greater part of the day and
night, chewing the cud, and not having sufficient
exercise to keep the blood healthy ;—the cure is—to
put them on a very bare run, or large paddock, when
they must walk about to get sufficient feed. Thus
they will be exercised the greater part of the twenty
four hours, and they soon become healthy and lively.
On the South Downs of Sussex, in England, the
sheep are the pictures of health, also on the moun¬
tains of Wales, &c, &.c., where the grass is scant
—where they bound over the precipices like sprites—
so healthy are they.
It is important to understand the first principle or
cause of the evolution of worms into the intestines or
fat of animals, as well as of animalculae in inanimate
matter—as cheese, water, &c.—by some named spon¬
taneous generation. It will appear that the internal
parts of many animals have the germs of animalculae
life always in them, and they are evolved when those
animals become in a certain bad state of health—as is
the case with human beings.
In inanimate matter we see spontaneous generation
in deteriorated, boiled, or distilled, water, which, at
first, is free from insect life, but becomes loaded with
it when it becomes deteriorated. So also in the
case of mites in cheese.
It may be said to be very mysterious, but it is not
more mysterious than many facts relative to the uni¬
verse, &c, &c.
I am, Sir, your obedient servant,
W. H. PEPPERCORNE.
152, Dowling-street, July 17.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ADELAIDE.
WEDNESDAY.
Henry Taylor, emigration agent delivered a
lecture to the Trades Union last night.
The Hamley Bridge to Balaclava Railway Bill has
been thrown out by the Legislative Council.
The Cricket Association has resolved to spend
£400 in improving the oval prior to the arrival of
the English cricketers.
The wheat market is hardening ; sales have been
effected at 5s. Flour in demand.
Henry Giles, bookmaker has been arrested for
shooting a blackfellow at Wilcannia ; bail has been
refused.
Babbage pleaded guilty to a charge of forgery on
the Bank of Adelaide.
The Legislative Council passed the Tariff Bill by
a majority of six.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
WATER POLICE COURT.
WEDNESDAY.
Before the Water Police Magistrate with Messrs. Brown,
Edwards, Manning, and others.
Seven inebriates were each fined 5s., and one was fined
10s., all with the customary alternatives.
William Jeffrey, french polisher, pleaded guilty to steal¬
ing a pair of trousers and a vest valued at £2, the property
of Benjamin Dunning, and was sent to gaol for a month,
with hard labour. He was also charged with stealing a coat,
a trousers, a vest an overcoat, a handkerchief and a pair
of braces, valued at £6 15s., and the property of Patrick
Hayes, a fellow lodger; and a prima facie case being
established against him, he was committed for trial. It was
proved that the prisoner pawned the goods.
Margaret Gardner, on remand from Murrurrundi, was
charged with bigamy. It was alleged that she married
Stephen Ransome, her former husband, William Gardner,
being alive at the time. The prisoner in no way combatted
the allegations, and signed her name on the "statement of
the accused" as "Margaret Gardner." Documentary
evidence was produced of the prisoner having,
under the name of Margaret Lindsay, been married
at Balmain, on May 25th, 1874, to William
Gardner, and of her being married under the name of
Lindsay to Stephen Ransome at Murrurrundi on August
21st, 1877. Elizabeth Henry Dick, a single woman, re¬
siding at Balmain, said she knew prisoner before her mar¬
riage and was present at her marriage at Balmain to
William Gardner, who she saw was present in Court.
G. B. Brodie, registrar of births, deaths, and marriages, at
Murrurrundi deposed positively to having married the
defendant to Ransome, who was a labourer. After this the
prisoner was committed for trial, and allowed bail in the
sum of £100
There were forty-three cases on the summons sheets. N.
S. Smith, for permitting music in his licensed house, was
fined 10s and 5s. 6d. costs. William Cook, for
selling liquor during prohibited hours, was fined 10s. and
5s. 6d. costs. Robert Patterson, for using obscene lan¬
guage, was fined 20s. and 4s. 10d. costs. Thomas
Ironfield and William Wright were each fined 10s.
and 4s. 10d. costs, for being drunk. Mary Casey,
for being drunk and disorderly, was fined 20s. and
4s. 10d. costs. For interrupting the free passage
of Hunter-street, Michael O'Connor and John M'Gill
were each fined 1s. and 2s. 6d. costs. Mary Marshall
was fined 15s. and 5s. costs for being riotous and throwing
stones, and Thomas Hanley for being riotous was fined 10s.
and 4s. 8d. costs; John Skehan for leaving his horse and
van unattended, was fined 6s. and 2s. 6d. costs, Jeremiah
Twoomey for illegally removing sand the property of
the Willoughby Corporation, was fined 40s. and 4s.
8d. costs. Sixteen persons were fined for allowing
stock to stray in public places. Jacob Schafer
for abandoning his public-house, had his license cancelled.
David Wishart was fined 20s., and 2s. 6d. costs, for assault¬
ing a constable. For being riotous, James Burns was fined
5s., and 2s. 6d. costs; and David Wishart, James Mackin,
William Stewart, John Matthews, and Henry Ludies, were
each fined 10s., and 4s. 4d. costs.

POLICE.
At the CENTRAL POLICE COURT, yesterday Mr. Bu¬
chanan, S.M., presided in the Charge Division, and Mr.
Abbott, S.M., disposed of the business in the Summons
Court. Mary Ann Gleeson, a child 6½ years of age, found
living with women of ill-fame, was sent to the Industrial
School, Biloela. Joseph Hymer, a lad 11 years old, found
wandering about the streets without any ostensible lawful
occupation, was sent to the Industrial School on the Vernon,
his father being ordered to pay 6s. per week towards his
support. Charles Johnson was fined £1, or seven days'
imprisonment, for being drunk while in charge of a horse in
Mitchell-street; £5, or three months, for making use of
obscene language in Campbell-street, Glebe ; and 14 days'
imprisonment (additional) for assaulting a constable in the
execution of his duty. A number of minor offences were
dealt with.
At the WATER POLICE COURT, yesterday, Mr. Addison,
S.M., disposed of the business in the Charge Court; and
Mr. Johnson, S.M., dealt with the cases in the Summons
Court. A number of persons were fined for drunkenness.
Emma M'Carthy, 24, charged with having been drunk and
disorderly in Cambridge-street, was fined 40s., in default
seven days' imprisonment. Thomas Hickey, 30, seaman,
charged with having been drunk and disorderly in Argyle¬
street, was fined 40s., in default seven days' imprisonment.
Charles Armstrong, 69, charged with having behaved in
an indecent manner in the Domain, was fined 40s., in de¬
fault one month's imprisonment. He was also fined 40s.,
in default one month's imprisonment for having
used indecent language. John, Creeney, 22, seaman,
belonging to the ship Orontes, was charged with having
been absent from his vessel without leave. He was
convicted, and was ordered to be imprisoned for seven days.
John M'Millan, 28, seaman, belonging to the ship Carlisle
Castle, was charged with having been absent from his
vessel without leave. He was ordered to be imprisoned for
seven days. A meeting of the Licensing Court was held at
noon, the bench being occupied by Messrs. Addison and
Buchanan, S.Ms., and the Hon. John Macintosh. A number
of transfers, colonial wine, auctioneers', and music licenses
were granted.
At the NEWTOWN POLICE COURT yesterday, before Mr.
G. O'Malley Clarke, S.M., in the matter Schell v.
Whittaker, the plaintiff, a butcher at Enmore, sued defen¬
dant, his servant, for damages (£10), for riding over one
Thomas Pickvance at night, when collecting orders. Pick¬
vance, in September last, recovered £6, with costs, against
Schell; and Schell now sued Whittaker for that amount.
Verdict for plaintiff, £6 12s., and 18s. 6d. costs.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MUNICIPAL COUNCIL.
RYDE.
The fortnightly meeting of the Ryde Municipal Council
was held on Friday evening, the Mayor (Mr. H. W.
Watts) in the chair. Aldermen Jackson, Small, Best,
Forsyth, Edwards, and Gallard also were present. Report:
A report was received from the finance committee, who
certified for the payment of accounts amounting to £85.
The report was adopted. Correspondence: The following
communications were received; From Jane Darvall, in¬
forming the council that the residents of Eastwood and
Pennant Hills are desirous of having the Government
road from Eastwood to the Parramatta-road opened
as soon as possible. The council were urged to give
the matter their attention. The letter was referred to the
Department of Mines. From Messrs. Campbell, Synnott,
and Co , offering to supply the council with blue metal at
12s 6d per yard, delivered at Ryde. At the same
time the firm intimated their willingness to contract
for tar-paving for side walks. The letter was received
From the Treasury, Sydney, requesting that, with a view
to determine the amount of endowment to which the muni¬
cipality is entitled, the Mayor would furnish the depart¬
ment with a certificate, signed by himself and treasurer,
showing the amount received for rates and subscriptions for
the periods August 4, 1885, to November 10, 1885, and
November 11, 1885, to February 1, 1886. It was
decided that a return of rates and subscriptions re¬
ceived from November 11, 1885, to February 1,
1886, be forwarded to the department. A com¬
munication was also read and received from Mr.
M. A. Cleary, suggesting that the night of the meeting
b changed from Friday to Wednesday. Motions: Alder¬
man Small moved, - "That the works committee examine
the bridge in Bridge-road, and report upon the matter of
the necessary repairs required." The motion was
seconded by Alderman Gallard, and carried. It was
moved by Alderman Small, seconded by Alderman
Jackson, and carried, - "That the works committee
examine Paul-street, and report upon the necessary works
required to be carried out in the street." Alderman For¬
syth moved, - "That a month's notice be given to Mr. P.
R. Young that the council dispense with his services in the
capacity of inspector of nuisances." The Mayor seconded
the motion. Alderman Jackson moved an amend¬
ment, "That the inspector of nuisances be called
upon to show cause why he should not be dismissed."
The amendment was seconded by Alderman Best, and
carried. Alderman Forsyth moved - "That the council
clerk be instructed to draw the attention of the Hunter's
Hill Municipal Council to the bad state of the water-tables
on their side of the Gladesville-road, and, further, to request
that the same be cleaned out to avoid injury to the road."
Alderman Best seconded the motion, which was carried.
Tenders: The following tenders were accepted: For 500
cubic yards of metal, to be delivered in the Central Ward
- Michael Toole, at 5s. 6d. per yard; for 120 cubic yards
of metal to be delivered in the East Ward - Thomas Duck,
at 4s. 6d. per cubic yard. Miscellaneous: It was resolved
that the works committee meet at the bridge, in Bridge¬
road, at 5 p.m. on the 10th instant. The council then rose.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE ILLAWARRA LINE.
The numerous popular resorts along the Illawarra line
were patronised by a large number of pleasure seekers.
Special provision was made by the railway authorities to
meet the requirements of the passenger traffic along the
line. During the day a number of special trains were run
at frequent intervals between Sydney and Hurstville, and
also, for the convenience of those who wished to go to
Waterfall and Loftus. Some of the morning
trains were well crowded, but those that ran during
the afternoon carried the greater number of passengers.
As on former occasions, some hundreds of persons journeyed
to Rockdale and took the tram for Cook Park or Lady
Robinson's Beach. The tram was kept running to meet
every train throughout the day, and was well filled with
passengers on each trip. At Cook Park a number of
special attractions were provided, and good care was taken
by those catering for the amusement of the public to ensure
the comfort and enjoyment of visitors. Numbers of per¬
sons found a considerable amount of pleasure in pro¬
menading up and down the beach, whilst scores of
well-organised family parties found camping places
a little further back amongst the trees. Numerous
games were provided in the neighbourhood of the tram
terminus for those who cared to indulge in them. Fruit
stalls were plentiful, tea and coffee were to be had in any
quantity, whilst the New Brighton Hotel, afforded ample
opportunity to those who preferred stronger beverages.
The New Brighton gardens and dancing pavilion
were freely patronised by the young people, and
dancing was kept up with considerable vigour
throughout the afternoon. The number of per¬
sons who visited Como and George's River was fully
equal to that of former holidays, and, as usual, there was
no lack of enjoyment at this beautiful watering place. It
would indeed be difficult to find a more charming retreat
than that afforded by the picturesque surroundings of
Como, and it is not surprising, therefore, that so many people
should journey thither on every holiday in the year. Boating
on George's River is still in the full tide of popularity, and
will no doubt remain so for many a day to come. Numbers
of those who visited Como spent hours upon the water,
whilst some spent the time in hill-climbing and in rambling
up and down, picking ferns, some of which are of a delicate
and beautiful kind. Several family parties found no little
pleasure in spreading the cloth and unpacking the hamper
underneath the trees. An hotel known as the Woronora
Hotel, which marks another stage in the progress of the
place, has just been opened at Como, and will, no doubt,
prove a great convenience to visitors on holidays and other
occasions. A number of holiday folk journeyed to Suther¬
land, National Park, Loftus, Waterfall, and other places
along the line.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
A WOMAN FOUND DEAD.
A magisterial inquiry was commenced at the South
Sydney morgue yesterday morning by Mr. W. T. Pinhey,
J.P., regarding the death of a woman named Mary Dawes,
who was found dead in an unoccupied house situated No. 2,
Fitzroy-terrace, Pitt-street, Redfern, on Tuesday last. It
appears that she was a widow, 45 years of
age, and of intemperate habits. On Saturday
night last the deceased and a man were seen
by a single woman named Hannah Eyre, who resides at
No. 6, Fitzroy-terrace, Pitt-street, to enter No. 2 of the
same terrace, which is at present unoccupied. Dr.
Milford made a post-mortem examination of the body,
and formed the opinion that the deceased had been dead five
or six days. There was a bruise about the size of a florin
on the scalp, which was the only mark of violence present.
The cause of death, in his opinion, was compression of the
brain. His Worship adjourned the inquiry until 9 o'clock
to-morrow morning for the attendance of a witness.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
COUNTRY NEWS.
¬
[BY TELEGRAPH.
FROM OUR CORRESPONDENTS.)
BRAIDWOOD, FRIDAY.
A complimentary banquet was given last night to
Mr. J. T. Murphy, previous to his leaving the district.
About 40 leading citizens were present. Mr. R.
Hassall being in the chair. The health of the guest
was drunk with much enthusiasm.
BYROCK, FRIDAY.
The Rev. J. B. Gribble, who has travelled across the
country from the Barwon mission station, arrived here
last night with a party of blacks; he goes on to Dubbo
to-day to gather up the natives in that district. There
are 85 aborigines settled at the mission.
COBAR, FRIDAY.
Business at the local Government office for the past
year shows a marked increase over that of the pre¬
ceding year. At the local police court 491 cases were
dealt with. The revenue received, exclusive of lands,
was £6494 12s. 7d., being an increase over the pre¬
ceding year of £3504 9s. 5d. At the warden's office
(mining), 42 gold applications, embracing 391 acres,
and 195 mineral applications, covering 1726 acres, were
received, the revenue received being £3334 2s. In
every department the increase has been most pro¬
nounced.
FORBES, FRIDAY. .
The revenue of Forbes Land Office for the year 1888
was nearly £20,000.
GUNDAGAI, FRIDAY.
The receipts at the Crown Lands Office, Gundagai,
for the quarter ending December were £1989 1s. 8d.
INVERELL, FRIDAY.
The revenue for the year at the Lands Office was
£16,364 13s. 2d.
Mr. J. H. Michell, hotelkeeper, and a very old resi¬
dent of Tingha, died to-day.
NARRANDERA, FRIDAY.
In response to a large requisition, the Mayor has
convened a public meeting for this evening to consider
what steps shall be taken to procure the railway exten¬
sion to the Lachlan. The proposed line would open up
a large area of agricultural land, that would be readily
selected once it was made accessible.
NEWCASTLE, FRIDAY.
This morning Constable Anderson, of Adamstown,
apprehended a man mimed Thos. Robertson on a charge
of forgery at Armidale. He was brought up at the local
police court to-day and remanded to Armidale.
WELLINGTON, FRIDAY.
At yesterday's annual meeting for the election of a
hospital committee, medical officer, &c., only the secre¬
tary put in an appearance, and he, after waiting
an hour, adjourned the meeting until next
week. It is rumoured that Dr. Rygate does
not intend to renew his connection with the hos¬
pital, which has existed for upwards of 20 years. The
treasurer has resigned, and it is thought that the insti¬
tution will have to be closed. Interest in all public
matters is at a very low ebb here.
WINDSOR, FRIDAY.
A very old resident of Windsor, Mrs. Ann Collins,
96 years of age, was found dead in bed yesterday. An
inquest was held to-day, and a verdict of death from
natural causes was returned.
At Pitt Town, Mr. S. Cox hass struck water at a
depth of 24 feet on his farm. It is believed that all
the farmers in the vicinity could be similarly success¬
ful.
Little surprise is expressed here at the resignation of
the Government. Mr. Bowman's friends are expecting
him to visit the electorate shortly.
WARDELL, FRIDAY.
John M'Lean and his old trainer, Charles Tyler,
arrived here yesterday, and were met on the wharf by a
number of friends. In the evening they were ban¬
queted at the Star Hotel. The town was decorated
with bunting, red, white, and blue predominating.
The Wardell brass band played through the town until
a late hour.
A young girl named Annie Palmer met with a serious
accident to-day. While taking a pot off the fire her
clothes ignited. Her hands and body were severely
burned. She is under the care of Dr. Tomlins, and
now lies in a critical condition.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
LICENSING MEETING.
A special licensing meeting for the purpose of
dealing with the application for renewals of publicans'
licenses in the metropolitan district, was held at the
Water Police Court yesterday, when Mr. A. M. Fisher
and Mr. John Macintosh, L.M.'s., were present.
There are in all 858 public houses in the metro¬
politan district, and there were over 300 ap¬
plications for renewals of publicans' licenses.
Eight of the applications were objected to ;
the remainder were granted. Transfers of pub¬
licans' licenses were granted as follows:- From
Sebastian Hodge (deceased) to Emily H. Hodge
(widow), for the Commercial Hotel, King and
Castlereagh streets; Elizabeth Haddon to E. J.
Hughes, Glen Hotel, Liverpool - street ; John
Monat to Michael Bourke, Empire Hotel,
Sussex-Street ; Thomas Foster Knox (trading as
Dalgety and Co., Limited) was granted a spirit mer¬
chant's license. A packet license was issued to H.
Bennison. The spirit merchant's license of Robert
Cunynghame (trading as Cunynghame and Co.,
Limited, Bond-street) was transferred to Vaughan,
Rogers, and Co. A number of music permits, booth,
hawkers', and pawnbrokers' licenses were also issued.
The application of Charles H. E. Lindeman for a
renewal of publican's license for the Ropemaker's
Arms, George-street North, was opposed by Sub-Inspector
Atwill on the ground that certain alterations
had been effected in the property for which the license
was originally granted which tended to render the
place unfit for an hotel. Mr. Slattery, for the appli¬
cant, contended that Lindeman's house possessed all the
accommodation required by the Act. The Bench ex¬
pressed a desire to visit the premises in question, and
the further hearing of the matter was postponed for a
week. John Cooper, licensee of the Metropolitan Hotel,
George-street, applied for a renewal of his license. The
application was opposed by Sub-Inspector Atwill on
the ground that the applicant was a man of intemperate
habits. After hearing evidence, the Bench said they
had no desire to take Cooper's license from him, but
hoped this would be a warning to him in future. The
application was granted. The other applications for
renewals which have been objected to by the police will
he heard at future sittings of the Licensing Court.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
DEATH OF MR. JOHN KITCHEN.
[BY TELEGRAPH.]
(FROM OUR CORRESPONDENT.)
MELBOURNE, Thursday.
Mr. John Kitchen, for many years senior partner
in the firm of John Kitchen and Sons, soap and
stearine candle manufacturers, died yesterday at
Kew. Mr. Kitchen arrived in the colony in 1853. In
the following year he and his sons established a soap
manufactory at Emerald Hill. About 1860 they
moved to Sandridge and started the factory which
has since grown into very large proportions. Mr.
John Kitchen retired from the firm about 15 years
ago. Mr Kitchen had attained the age of 92, and
until a few years ago enjoyed splendid health.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE GOLD DEPOSITS OF PERU.
Sir Charles Mansfield, the British Minister at Lima,
has recently sent to the Foreign Office an elaborate re¬
port on the auriferous deposits of Peru, drawn from the
report of the Lima School of Mining. In the moun¬
tain ranges of Peru, in the vicinity of the sea¬
board, whenever the rocks are of a crystal¬
line character, gold is found in veins of
quartz which have been intruded into the
granite and syenite. In this belt almost all the spurs
of the Andes are of those formations, and the auriferous
quartz is almost invariably accompanied by oxide of
iron and mica. The proportion of oxide of iron varies
considerably. Every possible form is met with, from
white quartz permeated with small ferruginous spots to
a reddish rock so charged with oxide of iron that
it forms almost the whole of the auriferous mineral,
the quartz appearing, as it were, in an accidental
manner. The quartz in the auriferous minerals of the
coast varies considerably in appearance, being found
crystallised in prisms, in semi-crystallised grains,
agglomerated with and united by oxide of iron, or in
amorphous masses more or loss compact, or friable,
with the appearance of scoria. In this district the
auriferous quartz is often associated with other
minerals, such as a white talcose substance, smooth to
the touch, with a silky, almost silvery polish, with
flakes of carbonate of lime of a laminar structure, and
limonite of a resinous appearance. Lastly, on the coast
of Peru gold may sometimes be discerned in copper
minerals, as well as in those mixed with chalk, copper
pyrites, malachite, alacamite, and silicate of copper, in
the upland districts, where the formations exhibit the
character of aqueous deposits, veins of gold
are not only found in crystalline earths, but
also in metamorphic rocks, such as quartzites and slaty
schist, intruding themselves into the sedimentary and
eruptive formations. In these veins the gold is some¬
times in a pure state, as well as in pyrites, sulphuret
of iron, or accompanied by other metallic sulphurets
more or less auriferous, copper pyrites, panabasa,
bournonite, galena, jamesonite, &c. Gold in the
mountain range is found in veins and threads, and in
the alluvial districts of the same in flakes and grains.
In the Cordillera Oriental, inthe district called the
Montana, gold is usually found in quartz veins in¬
jected into talc and clay slate by upheaval of crystal¬
line rocks. The quartz which accompanies the gold
in this district is white, and occasionally exhibits
marks of oxide of iron, but the latter is never found
in the same abundance as in the auriferous minerals
of the seaboard. In this part of Peru there are im¬
portant beds of auriferous soil, and it is from here
that the greater number of nuggets have been ex¬
tracted. The report then passes in review all the dis¬
tricts of the country where, as far as is known at
present, the existence of gold has been authenticated
following the departments in geographical succession
from north to south. The information is given in
great detail, and refers to all the mines, worked and
worked, and gold deposits known to exist in Peru,
with details respecting the quantity of the metal
obtainable. This portion of the report occupies 26 pages.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
The Caledonians journeyed down to Helensburgh on
Saturday last to encounter the Helensburgh Rovers,
and a one-sided game resulted in a win for the
Caledonians by 7 goals to nil. The visitors were after¬
wards entertained to supper, and spent a very enjoyable
evening. The Caledonians, under skipper Gleghorn,
comprised Logan, Aiken, Ferguson, Todd, Greig,
Mathieson, Philips, Irvine, and Murray, and they were
accompanied by a number of friends.
The Parkgrove Club (playing under Southern British
Association rules) journeyed to Balgownie, near Wol¬
longong, on Queen's Birthday, to try conclusions with
the local club. The visitors were met at
the station by several members of the club.
The match formed part of a programme
of sports which were well patronised. The Parkgrove
captain having won the toss, decided to play for the
first half with wind and sun in his favour. The ball
having been set in motion, was carried down to the
locals' goal, but was well returned, and some nice play
in mid-field resulted. Several times the visitors had
excellent chances of scoring, but owing to their bad
shooting and the splendid play of the Balgownie backs,
their efforts were in vain.' The game throughout the
first half was of a very even nature, and the teams
were well matched. Half-time was called without
either side having scored, although strenuous efforts
were made on the part of both teams. For some time
after the ball was sent rolling again the play was very
equal, but the ball having been run down the field by
Connors and Thompson, the Parkgrove had the luck to
score out of a scrimmage in front of the Balgownie
goal. This put the local team on its mettle, and for
some time the visitors' citadel was in great danger,
being only saved by the splendid play of the goal¬
keeper, Dunstan. From this till the call of time the
game waxed fast and furious, and the local players
were having the better of it when the whistle sounded
for cessation of hostilities, the scores being-Park¬
grove, 1 goal: Balgownie, nil. The visitors were
royally entertained after the match, everything possible
being done for their comfort and enjoyment. The re¬
mainder of the stay was occupied by a visit to the
famed Bulli Pass and excursions round the neighbour¬
hood, which were thoroughly enjoyed.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
NORTH SYDNEY RAILWAY.
PUBLIC MEETING AT ST. LEONARDS.
A public meeting of the residents and ratepayers of
North Shore was held last evening at the Masonic
Hall, St. Leonards, to discuss and consider the ad¬
visableness of assisting and co-operating with the
North Sydney, Manly, and Pittwater Railway Exten¬
sion League. The attendance, which numbered about
80, was presided over by the Mayor of St. Leonards,
Mr. F. Punch. Two of the members for the district,
Messrs. Burns and Cullen, were present.
The Chairman explained that the railway to be
considered was one which would run over the populous
district of North Shore. He thought that if the North
Shore people showed the Government by fair and
reasonable arguments that the railway would be a good
and paying one, it would cause the project
to be put before the Public Works Committee.
Again, if the line were constructed, it would not
only be a paying concern, but would increase the value
of thousands of acres land owned by the Government to
an enormous extent. He regretted that the evening
was a wet one. Had it been fine many more would
have been present ; still, he knew that those who were
away would enter heart and soul into the movement.
Among the apologies which he had received
was one from the Hon. E. Barton, M.L.C.
Mr. F. A. A. Wilson moved,—"That this meeting
approves of the action of the North Sydney, Manly,
and Pittwater Railway Extension League in endeavour¬
ing to secure the establishment of railway communica¬
tion between North Sydney, Manly, and Pittwater."
He contended that there was a necessity for the rail¬
way, and that the line would open up a vast amount of
good country.
Mr. D. Thomson seconded the resolution, and said
it had his heartiest sympathy. In spite of the difficul¬
ties of access the district of the Hawkesbury was even
now going ahead, and only required proper communi¬
cation to make it a residential centre. If the Govern¬
ment would take steps to secure now the ground which
would be required for the railway, it would save
the country a great deal of money. The Government
would, proportionately, get the railway for nothing
if it made the line now instead of waiting for years
until the route to be taken should be settled upon by a
number of residents. It did not need any foresight to
see that the district must go ahead, and that there
would in time be as large a city on this side of the
harbour as there was now on the other.
Mr. Burns, who supported the resolution, said that
he desired to manifest by his presence, rather than by
making a speech his sympathy with the cause. The
policy of the residents was to gather information and
get the Government to ask Parliament to send the pro¬
posal on to the Public Works Committee. He also
briefly commended the project in similar terms to those
in which he recently advocated it at Manly, and added
that he had ascertained that the population of the three
principal boroughs on the North Shore had increased
129 per cent. in the last eight years.
Mr. Cullen said he considered that the people of
Manly and Pittwater were entitled to the railway
on the ground of the present importance of
those places. He would give some figures to
show why the railway should be constructed.
He found that the population of North Shore and
Manly was 22,500. The steamer passenger traffic
between Sydney and Manly amounted to about 350,000
or 400,000 passengers per annum. Between Manly and
Newport during the summer months of the year, the
number of passengers was about 6000, while the number
of coach journeys for the same period was about 4000.
Between Manly and North Shore, although up to a
few months ago the communication was of a most
forbidding character, there were 5800 passen¬
gers in six months. For the six months
after the new punt was put on the passengers
numbered 9300.These were significant facts.
The unalienated acreage of Government Crown lands
at Manly Cove, Narrabeen, and Broken Bay was about
36,500 acres, and at least half of this would be
materially increased in value the moment the line was
made. In conclusion, he pointed to the rapidly
increased settlement in the localities served by the
suburban portion of the Illawarra line immediately
that line was constructed.
The resolution, on being put, was carried unani¬
mously.
Mr. W. T. Muston moved,—"That the following
gentlemen be appointed to represent this district at the
meetings of the league :—Mr. F. Punch, Mayor of St.
Leonards; Mr. J. Mountford, Mayor of East St.
Leonards; Mr. M. McMahon, Mayor of Victoria; Mr.
W. T. Muston, Mayor of Willoughby; Messrs.
Joseph Waterhouse, G. T. Clarke, J. R. Carey, A.
Muston, F. A. A. Wilson, J. Monday, and W. C.
Woolcott."
Mr. M. McMahon, in seconding the resolution, urged
that the Government should construct the line on
sanitary grounds, the country which would be opened
up would yield healthy homes for the population. Some
of the most lovely country in the world was to be found
at Pittwater. No other such country near Sydney
could be opened to the public. The proposed railway
should not cost more than £10,000 per mile, and there
would be about 20 miles of it.
The resolution was unanimously carried.
On the motion of Mr. T. S. M'Cormick, a subscription
list to provide the sinews of war was opened, and, at
the request of the Mayor of Manly, the chairman was
accorded a vote of thanks.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE CONCILIATION AND ARBITRA¬
TION ACT.
TO THE EDITOR OF THE HERALD
Sir, - The weakness of the Trade Disputes Con¬
ciliation and Arbitration Act, at the very point
where it should be strong, is demonstrated to us, and
clearly so, by the present strike at Broken Hill, for
were the Act in operation at the present moment it
could not be made use of, as one side to the dispute
refuses to submit to arbitration. The same thing
will happen, it appears to me, in all trade disputes of
any magnitude. Then the question arises whether
as we have admitted the principle of legislative in¬
terference in trade disputes, we should compel one
side to a dispute to submit to a desire expressed by
the other side for arbitration, as could be done by
admitting into the above Act the principle of com¬
pulsion. In passing this Act the Legislature of the
colony have given their sanction to the principle of
Parliamentary interference in trade disputes, and
may it not be as well to entrench that principle in its
strongest position by making the Act compulsory?
There can be little doubt but what the Act will be
taken advantage of only when there is little to be
lost or gained by either side to a dispute, but when
it would be of the greatest use to the whole com¬
munity it will probably be ignored. Thus we have
our Statute Book loaded with another Act weak in
its operation, and can we not with justice trace the
cause of this to too great a desire for new laws,
regardless of their effectiveness? It is as if we
were to say let as have new legislation, in fact
any legislative measures so long as they are new.
The usefulness - or, rather, the ineffectiveness - of
this Act gives us the impression that legislative
measures are being placed on the Statute Book before
the people have had time to maturely consider the
principles embodied in them, and it is question
whether we should not protest against the colony
being put to the expense of passing laws which we
found inoperative, where they are mostly desired to
be of use. I am &c.,
July 7. SAMUEL BROOKS.
No other medicine in so reliable as Ayer's Cherry Pectral
for the cure of coughs, colds and all derangements of the
respiratory organs. It relieves the asthmatic and con¬
sumptive, even in advanced stages of disease - [ADVT.]

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
GOVERNMENT GAZETTE.
The following announcements appeared in the
Government Gazette issued yesterday :¬
NUISANCES. - The Nuisances Prevention Act is to
come into operation at Murrumburrah in May next,
and in Greta in April next.
MUNICIPAL. - The provisions of the Municipalities
Act are extended to the Borough of Cootamundra.
APPOINTMENTS, &c. - First Lieutenant W. S.
Millard to be captain of the Young Corps, 1st Regi¬
ment of Infantry; Mr J. E. Wilson to be first
lieutenant in the Liverpool Company, Mounted In¬
fantry Regiment; Mr C. E. Eglese to be first lieu¬
tenant in the Illawarra Troop, New South Wales
Regiment of Cavalry; Mr J. Newman to be second
lieutenant in the Campbelltown Company, Mounted
Infantry Regiment; Mr L. D. Bull to be second
lieutenant in the Liverpool Company, Mounted
Infantry Regiment; Mr J Houston to be second
lieutenant in the Parramatta Troop, New South
Wales Regiment of Cavalry; Mr T. W. Cohen to
be district registrar in bankruptcy at Woodburn, to
act in that capacity at such times as Mr E. Jones,
Lismore, is unable to undertake the duty. The following
persons are appointed chief engineers and
masters in the dredge service of the Department of
Public Works, viz. :- Messrs J. C. Ruddle, D.
Mitchell, P. Fletcher, J. Dymock. Mr A. Carroll
to be post and telegraph master at George-street
West; Mr F. Ahrens to be post and telegraph
master at Mogil Mogil; Mr D. S. Strickland to be
postal assistant and telegraph operator at Moree.
The following are promoted to the positions of tele¬
graph operators at the undermentioned places:–
Messrs A. Fox, Broken Hill; E. Phillips, Tam¬
worth; W. H. Southwick, Forbes; R. C. Bowler,
Bathurst ; R. Hamilton, Wagga Wagga, Mr,
Wm. J. Cain to be telegraph operator at Bourke.
RETIREMENTS. - The following have retired from
the Civil Service: - Mr E. Burton,examiner of
titles, Registrar-General's Department: Mr D. A.
Garvan, stationmaster, Macdonaldtown, Department
of Railways; Mr F. R. Cole, preventive officer,
Wollongong, Department of Customs; Mr T. Gould¬
rake, ganger, Department of Railways ; Mr J.
Barron, labourer, Ordinance and Barrack Department.
TENDERS ACCEPTED. - The following tenders have
been accepted by the Department of Public Works ;
-Walton Pell, erection of bridge, Gara River, road
Armidale to Kangaroo Hills ; H. Vale and Sons,
supply of ironwork for footway, Corowa Bridge.

METROPOLITAN DISTRICT COURT.
FRIDAY.
(Before his Honor Judge GIBSON.)
BROOMHEAD V. PARRY.
This was a claim by John William Broomhead, of
Elizabeth-street, Balmain, against Griffith Parry, of
Crescent-street, Balmain, for £17 5s, amount of a
dishonoured promissory note. A verdict was given
for plaintiff for the amount claimed.
MARSHALL V. WHATMORE.
John A. Marshall, trading as Marshall and Co.,
butchers, of Alfred-street, sued W. W. Whatmore,
of Bellevue-street, for goods sold and delivered. A
verdict was given for £11 16s 3d, the amount claimed.
IZZARD AND CO. v MOIR.
This was a claim by Robert Izzard, John O'Neil,
and George Goldsmith, of Wyong, against A. Moir,
of Bowra Wharf, Sydney, for £10 11s 1d, goods sold
and delivered. Verdict for the plaintiffs for the
amount claimed.
KAVANAGH V. NICE.
J. Kavanagh, of 114 Castlereagh-street, sued
Joseph Nice, of 70 King-street, for £28 4s 5d for
goods sold and delivered. A verdict was given for
the plaintiff for the amount claimed.
SIMMS V. ROBEY.
Henry B. Simms trading as Henry Simms and
Co , importer, of York-street, Sydney, sued Henry
Robey, of Cleveland-street, Surry Hills, for £25 10s,
amount due on a dishonoured promissory note. A
verdict was given for the plaintiff for the amount
claimed.
FIELD V. WRIGHT.
Henry Field, soap manufacturer, of Oxford-
street, Paddington, claimed £17 13s 7d from David
Wright, dealer, of Marion-street Leichhardt, for
goods sold and delivered. A verdict was given for
plaintiff for the amount claimed.
SAYWELL V. ALLEN.
Thomas Saywell, carrying on business as the South
Clifton Coal-mining Company, sued Thomas Allen,
of Druitt-street, Sydney, timber merchant, for £15
18s, goods sold and delivered. A verdict was given
for the amount claimed.
GREEN V, CHAUNCEY.
Emanuel Green, of 2 Glebe-street, Paddington,
sued H. R. Chauncey, of 158 Pitt-street, Sydney, for
£23 6s, money paid by plaintiff to the defendant for
payment by him to the defendant of lands on the
purchase of conditional purchase 88-28 at Windsor.
A verdict was given for the plaintiff for the amount
claimed.
The Court then adjourned.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
TRANSIT COMMISSION.
A meeting of the Transit Commission was
held at the offices, Phillip-street, yesterday morn¬
ing. There were present the Mayor of Sydney
(Sir W. P. Manning) in the chair, Messrs. E Fos¬
bery (Inspector-General of Police), and G. Adams.
A letter was received from J. G. Malouf, the
owner of the Strawberry Hills 'buses stating that
he intended withdrawing his vehicles from the re¬
cently establshed route from Circular Quay, via
Foveaux, Campbell, Devonshire, and other streets
in Surry Hills, owing to the lack of patronage
accorded them.
The manager of the Sydney Omnibus Company.
Mr. O. A. Rayson, and Mr. Jno. Woods, one of
the directors, attended the meeting and complained
of the incorrect character of the report submitted to
the last meeting of the board as to the number of
their 'buses which plied to the Randwick Racecourse
on Anniversary Day. The report stated that 15
'buses were run, nearly all of which were un¬
licensed. The deputation denied that so many of
the vehicles were unlicensed, but Mr Rayson ad¬
mitted that some were. The board did not consider
that the report was shown to be incorrect in any
material point, and adhered to the decision previously
arrived at, that the same practice must not be fol¬
lowed in future.
With reference to the claim of Mrs. Darragh for
the refund of a sum of money found by her son in
an omnibus and handed over to the commission, the
Attorney-General forwarded a further communica¬
tion. He said there was some doubt as to how the
money should he dealt with, and the benefit of the
doubt should be given to Mrs. Darragh. The board's
solicitor expressed the opinion that by the
Act the registrar should not pay the
money to any one but the true owner. It
was decided to advise Mrs. Darragh to summon
the registrar, and allow a magistrate to decide the
matter. If he were not prepared to do so, as there
seemed some doubt as to the meaning of the section,
the applicant should ask him to state a case for the
Supreme Court.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE CASE OF JOHN COPP.
ATTEMPTS TO SOLVE THE MYSTERY.
THE BODY TO BE EXHUMED.
Yesterday morning the Minister for Justice (Mr.
A. J. Gould) received his final report in regard to
the case of John Copp, including one from the Chief
Medical Adviser to the Government After having
read the reports, Mr Gould decided to issue orders
for the exhumation of Copp's body, and for the
holding of a post-mortem examination of it. In¬
structions were accordingly sent to the City Coroner,
and arrangements were made-for the body
to be exhumed at an early hour this morning,
in the presence of Dr Rennie (Government
Pathologist), Dr. Goode, and Mr Hamlet
(Government Analyst). The Inspector-General of
Police his also been communicated with to secure the
co-operation of the police in regard to the exhuma¬
tion of the body, and also in regard to making any
further inquiries that may tend to throw light on the
circumstances connected with Copp's death.
The body was buried in the Church of England
portion of the Rookwood Cemetery on the 3rd
December, 1891. The number of the grave is 920,
section CC. For some time the police have been in¬
vestigating the matter, and Detective Hinds has been
instructed to supervise the work of exhumation. When
the work has been completed, the body will be
brought to the South Sydney Morgue, where Drs.
Rennie and Goode will perform an autopsy. Por¬
tions of the body are then to be submitted to the
Government Analyst (Mr Hamlet). The exhumation
is to be begun, as stated, at an early hour this morn¬
ing, and it is anticipated that the arrangements will
be speedily carried out. Upon the result of the
autopsy and of the analysis any future action by the
police in the matter will largely depend.
Copp, it will be remembered, was in Thornton's
Half-way House, Botany on the night previous to
his death. The following morning he was found in
an unconscious state by two boys on a piece of land
now included in Rosebery Park. The boys directed
the attention of a constable named Manuell to Copp,
and the constable conveyed him to Prince Alfred
Hospital at 6. 30 a.m. He died in the hospital two
hours after admission. The entry-book at the in¬
stitution describes the deceased is having been in a
state of coma when admitted but it does
not give any further particulars. There
were no marks of violence on Coup's body,
but Dr Purser, of the institution, declined to give
a certificate that death had resulted from natural
canses unless a post-mortem examination were
made. Drs Hunter and Munro, however, gave a
certificate but it had been represented to the
doctors that an autopsy would be distasteful to
members of Copp's family. It was also known that
Copp suffered from epileptic fits - a fact which
strengthened the impression of his death from
natural causes. He is spoken of at being a man of
sober habits and good behaviour.
It is impossible to say what proceedings will be the
result of the action of the authorities in issuing the
order for exhumation. It is certain that there are
circumstances connected with the matter that call
for investigation and those who are entrusted with
the case are fully seized of this but the great diffi¬
culty in the matter is, even if it be ascertained that
Copp did not meet his death by natural causes or by
accident, to connect anyone with the affair. If the
result of the analysis of the body prove that Copp
met his death by being poisoned, then the detectives
will continue their investigations.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PROSPECT AND SHERWOOD.
A meeting was held on 17th February. There were
present - the Mayor (Alderman W. Noller), and
Aldermen Hollier, Gleadall, Jamieson, Muston,
Miller, Carpenter, Jones, and Gifford. A communi¬
cation was received stating that His Excellency the
Governor had appointed Messrs. Gifford and Car¬
penter as aldermen to fill the vacancies not filled by
the ratepayers. Alderman Jones objected to Alder¬
man Carpenter's appointment. A letter was re¬
ceived from the Colonial Treasury, stating that £15
had been voted for Sherwood Park. It was thought
that the council was entitled to a larger grant. It
was decided to try and get a grant for a second park
in the municipality, and a concert was suggested to
raise funds to expend thereon. The annual com¬
mittees were then elected.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
STATEMENT BY MR. CATER.
Mr. Cater, of the Bellambi Colliery, returned to
Sydney yesterday morning, and gave the particulars
to a " Herald " representative. He said : " When
the vessel struck there were about 100 people on the
beach ; but no attempt was made to save life as
there was no apparatus available, and boats could
not live in the heavy sea. None of the crew were
successful in reaching the shore, but one man,
hanging on a plank, drifted within a few yards of
the shore, when he was immediately washed out to
sea again." About 18 months ago the Amy was
driven ashore near Cronulla Beach.
The Amy was owned by Messrs. Warburton and
Sons, Sydney. The first person to observe the vessel
appears to have been Mr. M'Cauley, a son of a
farmer, after whom the beach was named. He was
engaged in rounding up some cows, when he was
amazed to see the Amy's masts looming up but a
short distance away. The wind was blowing a gale
from the E.S.E., with thick rain, and as the ill¬
fated vessel canted over in the surf the waves swept
her fore and aft. No help could possibly have been
rendered from the shore, so tempestuous was the
weather and sea, as there were no means
of communicating with the crew. The brig
had her main and fore lower topsails set.
Very little was seen of the eight persons on board.
Captain M'Gee, the master, was noticed by an
onlooker in the rigging. Of the two boats on the
brig one was launched, but no sooner was she
lowered than she was swamped, and the sole
occupant of the boat, who, it is surmised, was the
winchman, was swept away. The man secured a
plank, and after being tossed about in the very heavy
sea disappeared. Some of the others were dis¬
cerned in the rigging and on the bowsprit. Had a
life-saving apparatus or lines been available it is
considered that some lives would have been saved.
The master was well known here, and was about
55 years of age and a very careful and experienced
mariner. The others lost were a mate, cook, winch¬
man, and four sailors.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
SALVATION ARMY.
A united officers and soldiers' meeting will be
held in the Salvation Army Temple, Goulburn-street,
next week, at which the question of General Booth's
visit to the colony will be considered. Lieutenant
Colonel Hoskin will preside, assisted by Major Taylor
(the colony secretary), and Majors Graham and
Wossberg (Sydney divisional officers).
General Booth has been compelled to postpone his
visit to Tasmania and New Zealand on account of
serious indisposition, His medical advisers insist on
this course being taken. It is hoped he will be
sufficiently recovered to undertake his New South
Wales campaign, which he purposes commencing
early in April
In connection with General Booth's projected visit
to Sydney the Railway Commissioners have granted
return tickets at single fares from all stations on
production of a Salvation Army certificate duly
signed. A similar concession has been made by the
North Coast S N Company to those travelling
saloon to the general's meetings.
To celebrate General Booth's seventieth birthday
it has been decided by the Salvation Army to endea¬
vour to gain some 7000 new soldiers besides other
important work undertaken on the plan of advance,
to be consummated by the end of the century.
Ensign Minnie Rowell, editress of the " Young
Soldier," the Salvation Army junior soldiers' paper,
who has been recently indisposed, is on furlough in
New South Wales. She is progressing favourably,
although it is considered it will be some time ere she
is fit for work again.

ENTHUSIASTIC MEETING AT IPSWICH.
BRISBANE, Thursday.
A successful federation meeting was held at Ipswich
yesterday. A branch of the Queensland Federation
League was formed. Mr Philp, Colonial Treasurer,
and Messrs. Drake and Higgs, M.L.A., delivered
speeches. The proceedings were very enthusiastic.
A branch of the Queensland Federation League
was formed at Dalby yesterday at a public meeting.
BOMBALA, Thursday,
The Federal League committee decided that a
deputation should wait on the Premier to announce
officially that Bombala, Southern Monaro, was a
claimant for the federal capital.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
NO. 2 JURY COURT.
(Before Mr. Justice Stephen and juries of four.)
CLAIM AGAINST AN INTESTATE ESTATE.
Walsh v. Orrock.
Mr. A. Thomson, instructed by Mr. F. Norrie,
appeared for the plaintiff, and Mr. T. Rolin,
instructed by Mr. L. Levy, for, the defendant.
This was a suit by Michael Walsh to recover
£342 14s from Charles Orrock, defendant, as
administrator of the estate of Alexander Orrock,
deceased, intestate. The money was alleged
to be due for work done, materials provided, funeral
expenses, board, lodging, washing, and attendance
for deceased, goods sold and delivered, &c., and
money lent and paid, and interest thereon. The
defence was that defendant and the deceased Alex¬
ander Orrock were never indebted as alleged.
The jury gave a verdict for the defendant, adding
a rider that it was of opinion that the undertaker's
account should be immediately paid out of the
estate, and if the plaintiff had paid anything to the
undertaker for the funeral the undertaker should
refund him.
Mr. Rolin said the defendant had already asked to
have the undertaker's account sent to him for settle¬
ment.
ALLEGED SLANDER OF A CONSTABLE.
Winter v. Sadleir.
Mr. Gannon, with him Mr. Mason, instructed by
Mr. Maddocks Cohen, for plaintiff ; Mr. Loxton,
instructed by Mr. M. J. Brown for defendant.
This was an action brought by Constable Alfred
George Winter, to recover £500 from Mrs. B.
T. Lysaght Sadleir of the Revolving Battery
Hotel, Woolloomooloo, for alleged slander conveyed
to plaintiff's wife, to whom, according to the declara¬
tion, defendant had said words to the effect that
plaintiff had introduced to the defendant another
woman as plaintiff's wife, and that the other woman
had a baby with her, of which baby plaintiff had
said, " Don't you think it is like me?" It was also

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
SYDNEY RAGGED SCHOOLS.
¬
The annual picnic given to the children attending
the Sydney Ragged Schools by Mrs. E. C. Mere¬
wether and family was held at Bondi Beach on
Friday last. The scholars from the Harrington¬
street, Glebe, Waterloo, Brisbane-street, and Wool¬
loomooloo branches were conveyed by special tram to
their destination. In addition to the teachers and
other helpers there were present :- The Revs. R.
S. Paterson, Dr. Clouston, J. G. Southby, James
Buchan, Messrs. T. P. Reeve, and John Marshall
(city missionaries), Frealand, Cooper, Mills, Mathers,
and Mercer. A bountiful dinner and tea were pro¬
vided. The intervals between meals were filled up
with races and various amusements. The weather
was all that could be desired, and the children (many
of whom were very ragged, and most with bare
feet) thoroughly enjoyed themselves. The order
prevailing and the behaviour of the children were
very favourably commented upon by the visitors.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ANTARCTIC EXPLORATION.
THE BRITISH EXPEDITION.
LONDON, May 18.
" Nature," in commenting upon the
differences that arose in regard to the com¬
mand of the projected expedition to the An¬
tarctic regions, blames the council of the
Royal Geographical Society for refusing to
endorse the compromise which Mr. R. A.
Gregory had accepted.
Professor E. B. Poulton accuses some of
the committee-men of the Royal Society of
having sacrificed an exceptionally competent
Antarctic scientific leader for the sake of
peace.
In February last a discussion arose between the
Royal Society and the Royal Geographical Society.
The Royal Society insisted that the command of the
Antarctic Expedition should be entrusted to the
Director of the Civilian Staff of the expedition. The
Royal Geographical Society held that a naval officer
ought to command. Lieutenant R. F. Scott, R.N., is
the naval commander of the expedition.

THURSDAY ISLAND FORTS.
Yesterday Major-General French, C.M.G., the
New South Wales Commandant, left by the steamer
Kasuga Maru on a visit to Thursday Island. He
will be joined by the Queensland Commandant,
Colonel Finn, who will leave Brisbane on Saturday
on his annual visit of inspection of the defences at
Thursday Island. Major Owen and Major Bridge
will accompany the General. During the absence of
Major-General French from the State Colonel H. D.
Mackenzie, A.A.G. and C.S.O., will act as Com¬
mandant of the New South Wales military forces,
and Captain J. H. R. King, of the Engineer Staff,
will act as S.O.E.S. for Major Owen.

(Before the Registrar, Mr. A. Henry.)
CERTIFICATE APPLICATIONS.
Re Charles Henry Coombe. This matter was ad¬
journed until October 9, as the official assignee had
reported that he had received information of a debt
omitted from the statement of affairs of which an
explanation was required.
Re William Robert Tulloch. This matter was
adjourned until October 9.
Re James Kellick. By consent this application
was adjourned until October 9.
MOTION TO VARY ORDER.
Re Robert Henry Warmoll. On the motion of the
solicitor for the bankrupt this matter was adjourned
until October 9.
MEETINGS AND EXAMINATIONS.
Re Charles Dobson. The bankrupt was examined
by the official assignee. He stated that his bank¬
ruptcy was caused by a land speculation, a decrease
in the value of the land, and by borrowing money at
a high rate of interest. The meeting was closed, and
the public examination declared concluded.
Re Joseph Garner the meeting was closed, and
the public examination declared concluded.
Re William Robert Pope. The bankrupt was
further examined by the official assignee, and, as the
bankrupt had not filed accounts, the public examina¬
tion was adjourned until October 16.
Re Robert Henry de Winton M'Kie, trading as
Robert Henry Bryant. Accounts not having been
filed the matter was adjourned until October 9.
Re Margaret Isabel James. A witness was ex¬
amined under section 30, and the matter was ad¬
journed until October 9.

James Stevenson miner of Adelong Mr W. H.
Palmer, official assignee.

Redfern. Petition to be heard on September 22.

Mungo Scott, and Co., of Sydney, v. Thomas Wil¬
son Kidd, of Granville. Petition to be heard on
September 22.
