ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE RECENT EXTENSIVE
ROBBERIES.
RECOVERY OF THIS STOLEN PROPERTY
In connection with the recent robbery of copper,
tin, and other metal, valued at £55, from the pre¬
mises of Davis, Shepherd, and Co., of Petersham,
and also of the finished and unfinished boots and
dressed leather which was stolen last Friday night
from Mr F.J. Walters' boot factory, Parramatta¬
road, Forrest Lodge, the police have been able to re¬
cover the whole of the stolen property, and also to
effect the arrest of several persons who are
alleged to be connected with the thefts.
It seems that on Tuesday last, acting on
certain information, Constable Hind, of the Red¬
fern police, proceeded to a certain smelting works,
and after remaining there for some hours a man
made his appearance with a load of copper and tin,
which has since been identified as portion of
the property stolen from the premises of Davis,
Shepherd, and Co., at Petersham. Hind arrested
this man, and, after locking him up, proceeded to
his residence, where he secured a quantity of books.
From statements that were made to Hind, the latter
telephoned to the Redfern police station for assistance,
and Constables Moore, Whitehair, and Urquhart
arrived. Together the police went to a cottage in the
district of Waterloo, and there they found the whole
of the goods which had been stolen from the boot
factory at Forest Lodge, and also portion of the
goods stolen at Petersham. They then made several
other arrests. The persons charged, who included
a woman, were brought before the Redfern Police
Court yesterday, and were remanded for a week.
Last evening Detective Bannan made a further
arrest in connection with the matter.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MURDER NEAR MARY¬
BOROUGH.
A HALF-CASTE'S CONFESSION.
SHE SHOOTS HER FATHER.
BRISBANE, Monday.
Some sensation was caused at Maryborough to-day
when it became known that a half-caste Girl named
Clara Wheeler had given herself into custody, stating
that she had shot her father dead with a gun. The
girl, who is about 23 years of age, made a state¬
ment at the inspector's office, the effect of which was
that her father had ill-treated her, and that she shot
him in self-defence. Wheeler, who was an old
identity, carried on dugong fishing about Fraser
Island. The tragedy occurred at Poonah, near Boo¬
nooroo. A constable and the girl have gone to the
scene of the tragedy.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PHOENIX WHARF RESUMPTION.
--¬
THE ARBITRATOR'S AWARD.
Yesterday the Commissioners of the Land Appeal
Court, Messrs A. Oliver (president), C. Brandis, and
W. Houston, defended their decision in the case in
which they acted as arbitrators, viz., the Phoenix
Wharf resumption case.
The terms of agreement between the Sydney
Harbour Trust Commissioners and the Illawarra
Steam Navigation Company were de¬
tailed. From these it appeared that the wharf
property which was the property of the company
was resumed by proclamation on May 3, 1900, and
vested in the Minister for Works. The company
had since that date remain in occupation of portion
of the property. On December 1 1900, the Minister
for Works and the company mutually decided that
until an agreement as to the rent to he paid by the
company, or until the determination of their
occupancy they should pay to the Crown £1 per
month on account, and that in the event
of the parties not agreeing, the rent
was to be determined by the arbitrators
(the Land Appeal Court). The arbitrators
were therefore asked to determine the amount to be
paid by the company for rent or occupation from
the date of resumption until the determination of
the occupancy of the premises by the company. The
parties agreed to consider the Court's decision abso¬
lutely final and binding upon them, and to bear their
own costs in the matter of the agreement. The
Court said: ''We award, order, and determine
that the amount to be paid by the Illawarra
S. N. Company to the Crown for rent or
occupation of the land and premises or any
part thereof occupied by the company at Darling
Harbour shall be at the rate of £1900 sterling per
annum inclusive of rates and insurance, from the
date of the resumption to the determination of the
occupancy of the land and premises by the company,
which period we determine to be three years and
28 days."
Evidence in the arbitration case extended over
eight days, and a vast amount of evidence was ten¬
dered. The award was in favour of the company's
contentions.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
FOURTH TEST MATCH.
APPOINTMENT OF UMPIRES.
At a well-attended meeting of the New South
Wales Cricket Association last evening the
appointment of umpires in connection with
the fourth test match formed the chief busi¬
ness. Mr. Warner wrote, requesting that Mr.
Crockett be appointed one of the umpires.
After a good deal of discussion it was finally
decided, on the motion of Mr. Davis, that Mr.
Warner be informed that Mr. Crockett could
only be allowed the fees provided by the asso¬
ciation's rules, namely, one guinea a day and
travelling expenses. The selection of the re¬
maining umpire resulted in even voting for
Mr. Giltinan and Mr. Laing. On the casting
vote of the chairman, Mr. C. F. W. Lloyd, Mr.
Giltinan was appointed. The resignation of
Mr. G. H. Reid as president of the association
was received and accepted in view of the ap¬
proaching litigation with the Sydney Cricket
Ground trustees.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PERSONAL.
M. Leon Caron, the well-known musical
conductor, died at the Gresham Hotel, near
the Town Hall, at 2 o'clock yesterday
morning. Towards the end of the Gilbert
Sullivan season, which terminated at her
Majesty's Theatre last February, M. Caron
suffered from a severe attack of dropsy, and
when he courageously insisted upon embark¬
ing with the company for New Zealand, few
people thought he would reach that country
alive. After two operations, however, he re¬
covered sufficiently to resume work for a
time, but eventually it was deemed better
that he should return for rest in Sydney. He
arrived here about ten days ago, and although
a further operation was successfully per¬
formed by his medical adviser. Dr. M'Carthy,
he gradually sank. By his death Mr. J. C. Wil¬
liamson loses his principal musical conductor,
and the genial French musician will
be widely mourned. He conducted the Royal
Comic Opera Company for many years, and
must have directed a repertoire of some two
score different works. M. Leon Caron was born
in 1S50 at Boulogne-sur-Mer, where he studied
music under Alexandre Guilmant, and the first
of the world's organists corresponded with him
affectionately throughout his life. M. Caron's
talent was so remarkable that he was sent
by the Boulogne Municipal Council to the
Paris Conservatoire, where he won distinc¬
tion as a violinist and composer, but the close
of his studios was broken by the outbreak of
the war with Germany. This was an un¬
fortunate crisis for the students, as the Prix
de Rome, for which young Caron stood in with
two others for the committee's final award,
was not bestowed that year (1870). M. Caron
fled to London during the Commune, and
was engaged as first violin of Riviere's or¬
chestra at the Alhambra Theatre; and 12
months later he joined the famous orchestra
of Theodore Thomas in America, and re¬
mained with it until it was disbanded after
the Philadelphia Exhibition in 1876. At the
end of that year M. Caron made his debut
at the Melbourne Opera House, and then ac¬
cepted the post of conductor of Lyster's
Grand Italian Opera Company, which he di¬
rected until 1880. A little later M. Caron
won the prize of 100 guineas offered by the
Commissioners of the Melbourne International
Exhibition for an original cantata, and his
"Victoria" was accordingly performed under
his direction by a chorus of 1000 voices and
an orchestra of 125 players at the ceremonious
opening of the Exhibition on October 1, 1880.
After the Exhibition M. Caron, as conductor
of English opera, produced for the first time
in Australia in English, "Carmen," "Mig¬
non," "Paul et Virginie" (by Victor Masse),
"Fra Diavolo," "Rose of Castile," and many
other works. Since April, 1889, when he
directed the first performance of "The Yeo¬
men of the Guard," M. Caron's energy and
talent maintained the high musical efficiency
for which the Royal Comic Opera Company
became celebrated under his baton. During
his long career in Australia, the late M. Leon
Caron married the Tasmanian soprano, Miss
Tasma Sherwin, nearly related to Mme. Amy
Sherwin. Mme. Caron, who nursed her hus¬
band devotedly to the last, and their young
daughter Irma, who made her stage debut
this year as the midshipmite in "H.M.S.
Pinafore," survive the esteemed musician.
The Chief Secretary, Mr. Hogue, accompan¬
ied by Mr. Hugh M'Donald, M.L.A., will leave
this evening for Warren, where the former
will to-morrow officially open the annual show
of the Warren Agricultural and Pastoral So¬
ciety. Mr. Hogue will receive a number
of deputations with regard to local wants, and
returns to Sydney on Friday morning. On
tho following day the Chief Secretary, with
Mr. Dick., M.LA., honorary Minister, will
proceed to Newcastle, to attend the golden
jubilee of the Grand United Order of Oddfel¬
lows.
Mr. J. B. Labatt, an engineer representing
the South Australian Government in the mat¬
ter of the distribution of the waters of the
Murray River, returns to Adelaide by to¬
night's express. Mr. Labatt has been en¬
gaged for the past two months collating in¬
formation in this State for the guidance of
his Government.
The date of the departure of the Prime
Minister for Queensland is not yet definitely
fixed, but it will probably be early next
week. His itinerary is not yet marked out.
Mr. P. H. Rogers, B.A., of St. Andrew's Col¬
lege, who has been awarded a Rhodes
scholarship, has had a brilliant scholastic
career. He won the Coutts scholarship at
his entrance into the college, and by securing
a first-class each year at the final examina¬
tions, qualified himself for the Gordon
scholarships, which he thus gained for the
two remaining years of his course.
At a meeting held at Her Majesty's Theatre,
Melbourne, yesterday, arrangements were
made with regard to the complimentary bene¬
fit to be given to Mr. and Mrs. G. B. W. Lewis,
on June 8. Mr. J. C. Williamson, who pre¬
sided, said the theatrical profession was
splendid when one possessed youth and health,
and success was met with; but a different pic¬
ture was presented when a member of the
profession grew old and became the victim
of unmerited misfortune, as in the case of
Mr. Lewis, who had reached the age of 87
years.
The late Mr. A. M. Smidmore, of Wentworth
Falls, left an estate valued at £133,375, and
upon this stamp duty has been paid amount¬
ing to £13,334 10s.
Mr. William Henry Monk, brother of Mr.
Cyril Monk, the violinist, has been appointed
organist to Saint Jude's Church of England,
Randwick, lately vacant.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
REMEMBER THE CIVIL SER¬
VANTS.
TO THE EDITOR OF THE HERALD.
Sir — Whenever there is a so-called surplus
in the accounts of the State Treasurer or
the Railway Commissioners, there is a host of
applicants for concessions which would soon
dissipate the whole of the money. In your
issue of Thursday a correspondent, "Henry
Brooke," asks that now the State is prosper¬
ous, the salaries of Public servants should
be restored to the figures ruling before the
days of retrenchment and economy. Appar¬
ently he does not realise that 15 years ago
this country was conducting its affairs, both
industrial and governmental, on an extrava¬
gant basis, which, as the crisis of 1893 proved,
could not continue without bankruptcy. Since
then all classes have had to accept less money
for services; interest in the bank has fallen
from 5 to 3 per cent. — a fall of 40 per cent, in
income; house rents have come down 25 per
cent, while salaries, commissions, and profits
of general business have been similarly re¬
duced. So the Civil servant is not the only
person who has to put up with a smaller in¬
come, but he, like other consumers, finds his
money to-day will go further in hiring house
room, in cost of provisions, tramfares, etc.,
than it did in the extravagant times of two
decades ago.
Your correspondent, however, ignores the
fact that Mr. Carruthers intends to use the
surplus in the only way justified by the cir¬
cumstances. The £900,000 in credit really
consists of the proceeds of land sales, which
is not true revenue, but is a portion of the
State capital too long employed to bolster up
a wasteful method of government. Hence¬
forward this money is to be kept separate from
the revenue, and used like loan funds for
making reproductive works.
Civil Service rewards may be now lower
than those ruling here in years gone by but
they are very much higher than those given in
other countries abroad, or even in the neigh¬
bouring State of Victoria. I am etc.,
TAXPAYER.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE CHURCHES.
COOMA, Wednesday.
The new Church of England rectory has just
been completed. It is the most attractive
dwelling in Cooma, and one of the best on
Monaro. It is built in Gothic style of archi¬
tecture, with tiled roof (the first in the town).
Acetylene gas has been installed. The archi¬
tect was Mr. G. D. Cochran, of Cooma, and
the contractors, Messrs. G. H. Foster and
Sons, of Sydney. The building cost about
£1500.
ORANGE, Wednesday.
The Rev. H. G. Chivers and Mrs. Chivers
left Millthorpe for a new charge at Parra¬
matta yesterday morning, after seve¬
ral years' residence and ministry in
the Orange and Millthorpe districts, where
they were both highly esteemed. They
were the recipients of numerous presentations
from members of St. Mark's Church, Mill¬
thorpe, and parish churches, prior to their
departure. Mr. Chivers will be succeeded
by the Rev. Mr. Kelly, of Cobar.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ESCAPEES FROM CASTLEMAINE.
A SURRENDER.
SECRETED IN A GOODS TRAIN.
MELBOURNE, Tuesday.
Harrison Goodhind, one of the three prison¬
ers who escaped from Castlemaine Gaol on
Sunday night, surrendered himself to the Carl¬
ton police to-day. After making their escape
the men struck out into the bush, and tra¬
velled hard all night. They had some bread
having saved their rations on Sunday. The
trio boarded a goods train at Taradale. Good¬
hind got in one part of the train, his com¬
panions in another. It was arranged that
the three escapees should got out at Sun¬
shine, but this was not carried out. The train
stopped for half an hour at Woodend, and
it is supposed that Meakin and Brown left
there, and have made for the north-eastern
district. Goodhind came on to Melbourne in
the train without being discovered, and
dropped down from it unobserved a short dis¬
tance from the city. He then went to his
home at North Fitzroy. Whilst on his way
to his home he was noticed by Constable Clin¬
nick, who saw he answered to the description
of one of the wanted men, and followed him
to the house. The officer saw him enter the
back gate, and came up close behind him.
When Goodhind got inside the yard he locked
the back gate and rushed through the house.
When the officer got round to the front of the
house he saw Goodhind a couple of hundred
yards away, and started in pursuit. Goodhind
then visited Carlton police station and gave
himself up. He told his mother that he was
very sorry for what he had done, but he was
led away by others older than himself.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MR. WADE'S RETURN.
HIS EXPERIENCE AS MOTORIST.
The Premier returned to town on Friday
with his faith in motor cars rudely shaken,
and with the sympathy that comes of under¬
standing for the Government Whip, who on
one memorable occasion found it necessary to
leave a comfortable seat in the State car and
join in a ten-mile push to the nearest hotel.
Anxious to inspect the big Berembed weir,
the Premier, while on his way from Narran¬
dera to Coolamon by motor caused a detour
to be made. Berembed was safely reached,
and the big works were inspected, but when
the car was headed southward and pace got
up, soft patches of red soil, in places under
water, gave trouble. In one of these patches
the car sank to the axles, and remained there
for an hour. Pine trees for a hundred yards
round were denuded of their lower branches,
and the soft road packed with bushes, in the
hope that the wheels as they were sent re¬
volving round would get a grip. But to no
purpose. Chauffeur and passengers, politi¬
cians of high estate, and pressmen
toiled and sweated as they strained
with heavy poles to lever the car out of
the mud, and consigned to oblivion a group
of laughing jackasses which from the topmost
boughs of a neighbouring tree seemed to make
merry over man's misfortunes. Then a swag¬
gie happened along, and stood, smoking his
pipe, in solemn silence, as he watched the
work going forward to get the car running.
Once out of the difficulty, speed regulations
were set at nought by the chief lawmaker, and
the car went flying on to Coolamon, some 20
miles distant. But before many miles had
been covered the car was again axle deep
this time in a broad ditch, which stretched
across the whole width of road. The Pre¬
mier went hot afoot to a neighbouring farm
and enlisted the help of the farmer, who ar¬
rived on the scene with plough-horse and
chain gear. But no plough that horse had
ever drawn was so unyielding as the Premier's
car. Mr. Wade was already overdue at Coola¬
mon, and he had visions of injury to the Libe¬
ral cause if he failed to keep his appointment
with the electors. He tramped along towards
the town of Mattong, where he was overtaken
by the car. Again the motor flew on its way
to Coolamon, but four miles out of the town
it went down into a deep ditch. The Premier
gave it up, borrowed a bicycle from a stranger,
and put in some fast pedalling. His troubles
ended just outside Coolamon, where he met
with a reception that helped towards philo¬
sophic acceptance of his past troubles.

There is always a right and a wrong way
to go about doing things. The right way is
that which leads to the accomplishment of the
object in the most direct and workmanlike
manner. When a person suffers from some
ailment, such as rheumatism, gout, lumbago,
backache, sciatica, blood disorders anaemia,
indigestion, biliousness, jaundice, sick-head¬
ache, general debility, gravel, stone, or blad¬
der troubles, the right way to recovery is to
attack the seat of the disorder All the com¬
plaints named are symptomatic of a disordered
condition of the kidneys and liver. This
disordered condition interferes with the work
of the organs and conduces to the retention
in the system of uric and biliary poisons
which it is the function of the kidneys and

(5s) bottles and in the cheaper (2s 6d) non¬

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PUBLIC LIBRARIAN.
-»¬
OFFER TO MR. IFOULD.
MR. CARMICHAEL'S ATTITUDE.
The trustees of the Free Public Library,
preparatory to making a recommendation to
the Minister for Education, have offered the
vacant position of librarian to Mr. W. H.
Ifould, chief librarian of Adelaide, at a salary
of £630 per annum, but he has not yet de¬
cided whether he will accept or reject the
offer.
Mr. Carmichael, Minister for Education,
stated last night that no recommendation had
yet reached him from the trustees.
Correspondence took place a month ago be¬
tween the Minister for Education and the
trustees of the Free Public Library about the
appointment of a successor to Mr. F. W.
Bladen, who retired from the position of
librarian as far back as February. The
State Government passed by the nominee of
the trustees, and asked for some other nomi¬
nation. For some time Dr. Watson, who is
a trustee of the Institution, has been acting
as librarian, and it was generally understood
that his co-trustees were desirous that he
should be permanently appointed to the post.
Mr. Carmichael took the point that with the
trustees lay the nomination, but that the ap¬
pointment had to be made by the Governor
in-Council. Any nomination, therefore, had
to receive his official approval. He said that
he did not regard Dr. Watson as the best
nomination available, and he asked the trus¬
tees to send in another name.
Professor MacCallum, who is chairman of the
library trustees, regarded the action of Mr.
Carmichael, in allowing the facts of the case
at that stage to get into the press, as a grave
breach of etiquette. He thought that they
at least should have had the Minister's de¬
cision placed before them first of all.
Mr. Carmichael, in reply, said he was not
aware of having committed any breach of
etiquette, as his remarks to the press were
simply intended to reply to certain observa¬
tions which had got into print, and which
suggested that the delay in the appointment of
a librarian was due to the department.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
NOTES.
Mrs. T. A. Griffin, hon. secretary of the
Ladies' Kennel, Club, reports that the follow¬
ing judges have been appointed for the show
on October 17 and 18:- Dr. Stopford, spaniels;
Mr. L. Smith, Australian terriers; Mr. R.
Mason. Yorkshire and Sydney silky terriers;
Mr. J. Creighton, Scottish and Skye terriers;
Mr. E. Driver, sheep dogs and cattle dogs.
Judges for greyhounds, collies, and Irish ter¬
riers have not yet been selected. Mr. J.
Maude will judge foxterriers and all other
breeds for which a special judge is not ap¬
pointed. Mrs. Griffin adds that prize sche¬
dules will shortly be distributed, and that this
year's L.K.C. ball promises to be a great
success.
The Adelaide bull bitch Carmen (dam of
Champion Tommy Burns) is nearly seven
years old. She has whelped 62 puppies, which
is claimed as an Australian record tor the
breed.
Mr. R. Comley, of North Sydney, has im¬
ported a foxterrier bitch from England.
Mr. A. E. Nash, of Sydney, has presented a
handsome shield for competition at the Bull¬
dog Club's show.
Mr. Horatio Bartlett, one of the pioneers in
this State of wirehair foxterriers, has im¬
ported a brace of this breed from the old
country.
Mr. C. H. Angas, of Adelaide, has had the
bad luck to lose his imported bull bitch Kil¬
burn Caramel. This bitch was coming to
Sydney for exhibition In October. She had a
first-class show record, and her presence in
competition with the Sydney champions was
anticipated with interest.
Mr. W. H. Rix, lately of Brisbane, has
taken up his residence in Sydney. Some time
ago he bought the Yorkshire terrier Fury from
Mr. H. Ekin. Mr. Rix still owns this bitch,
also Vickey and two puppies. He intends
taking on the breeding of Yorkshire terriers
in Sydney.
Mr. W. D. M'Crea, during his year's ab¬
sence from Sydney, has transferred his Scot¬
tish terrier, champion Kirribilli Crae, with all
engagements, to Mrs. A. H. Cameron, of Wav¬
erley.
An Adelaide exchanges reports that Mr. J.
Arthur has purchased from Mr. Wilkins, of
Sydney, a red bull bitch named Guinea Gold.
Mr. Warner having resigned his position of
hon. secretary to the Bulldog Club, Mr. W.
M'Namara has consented to act during the
currency of the coming show.
Mr. M. Gardiner, of Randwick, has disposed
of a poodle dog pup, by Calderwood. Bonheur
ex Avoca Belle, to Mr. S. Broomfield, and a
bitch pup. sister to the above, to Mrs. New¬
man.
Mrs. Dorrington has transferred one of her
poodle dogs to a country fancier.
Mr. J. Perkins has secured the well-known
bulldog Burbank Ludovic, by Hever Dexter ¬
ch B. Berenice, from Mrs. Mitchell, who is
leaving Sydney to reside in England. Mr
Perkins reports that his bitch Burbank. Eu¬
nice (sister of Ludovic) died recently.
Mr. H, F. Moses, of Goulburn, has trans¬
ferred to Mr. Perkins, of Ashfield, the bulldog
Chadwell Rex, winner of several first prizes.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
RECKLESS DASH.
AUSTRALASIANS DISDAIN
COVER.
CHARGING AHEAD.
ATHENS, May 10.
An eye witness of the recent fighting
states that the heaviest losses were borne
by the Australians and New Zealanders,
whose one fault was a complete disdain for
cover. Their bravery and dash were amaz¬
ing.
In some cases men, after rushing the first
Turkish trenches for 10 minutes charged
ahead, despite the appeals of their officers,
penetrating several miles inland, and suf¬
fering heavily, when Turkish reinforce¬
ments compelled them to retreat.
Home troops pay the warmest tributes to
the Australians.
German officers endeavoured to create a
diversion by donning uniforms of English
and Australasian officers, mingling with our
men, and giving false orders.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PATRIOTIC NATIVES.
¬
Mr. K. C. Eliot, Resident Commissioner in
the Gilbert and Ellice Islands colony, who is
in Sydney on official business, states that
when the native constabulary of the Islands,
comprising about sixty men, heard of the
serious war news about six weeks ago, a
considerable number of them waited upon
him, and requested that they should be al¬
lowed to go to the war. He obtained the
necessary permission, and shortly a body of
thirty, representing half the force, would
arrive in New Zealand under Mr. Burge, who
had volunteered to accompany them to the
front, and they would proceed to the war with
the native troops from the Dominion. Since
the war commenced the residents of the
group have contributed £12,000 to patriotic
funds, and, the white community being very
small, it was a safe estimate, to say that
£10,000 of this amount had been contributed
by the natives, the total population being
about 35.000.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
DEATH OF MR. J. HERRMANN.
The death occurred on Saturday last at his
home Rossmore, Wardell-road, Dulwich Hill,
of Mr. Jacob Herrmann, late outdoor superin¬
tendent of the New South Wales Tramways.
The deceased was for many years in the
tramway service, from which illness neces¬
sitated his retirement recently. He was 56
years of age. The funeral took place on Monday, and was
very largely attended. From deceased's late
residence to Lewisham railway station the
cortege was headed by the Tramway Band
and about 200 tramway officers and employees.
At the cemetery a short service was held in
the Roman Catholic Church, conducted by
the Rev. Father E. J. M'Namara, who also
officiated at the grave.
The chief mourners were Mrs. Herrmann,
(widow), Messrs. H. and L. Herrmann (sons),
Mr. and Mrs. Naylor (son-in-law and daugh¬
ter), Miss Herrmann (daughter), Mr. and Mrs.
Digby (brother-in-law and sister), Messrs. L.
and H. Herrmann (nephews), Mrs. Haddock,
and Miss H. Herrmann (nieces), Mrs. E. Herr¬
mann (sister-in-law), and Mr. and Mrs. H.
Ross.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
DEATH OF MS. A. W. HAMBLY.
The death of Mr. Anthony Warren Hambly
took place at his residence, in Elswick-street,
Leichhardt, at the age of 80 years. In his
earlier years the late Mr. Hambly followed
mining and grazing pursuits in the Grenfell
district. He came to Australia from England
when about 20 years old. While gold-mining
at Grenfell he made a very successful find
at a place called O'Brien's Reef. He subse¬
quently went in for grazing, and owned the
station known in the Grenfell locality as
Ipswich. After having spent about a quarter
of a century at Grenfell, where he took a live
interest in all public matters, he came to
Sydney, where he has led a retired life. He
is survived by a widow, three sons, and five
daughters. One son was killed in the Boer
war. The funeral takes place this afternoon,
leaving his late residence for the Anglican
portion of the Rookwood Cemetery, where the
interment will be made.

war. The funeral takes place this afternoon,
leaving his lato residence for the Anglican
portion of the Rookwood Cemetery,-whore the
Interment will be made.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
IN DIVORCE.
(Before Mr. Justice Owen.)
ROYLE v ROYLE.
Ethel Louie Royle (formerly Kemp) sought
a divorce from Wallace Richard Royle, on
the grounds of desertion. The parties were
married at Newcastle in December, 1906, ac¬
cording to the Anglican rites. Petitioner,
who was represented by Mr. Toose (instruct¬
ed by Mr. R. W. Fraser), was granted a de¬
cree nisi, returnable in six months.
STEWART v STEWART.
Robert Henderson Stewart sued for a dis¬
solution of his marriage with Minnie Gertrude
Stewart (formerly Sullivan), on the grounds
of desertion. The marriage took place at
Coolah, in December, 1899, according to the
rites of the Church of England. A decree
nisi, returnable in six months, was granted.
Mr. R. W. Fraser appeared for the petitioner.
SHIRLAW v SHIRLAW.
In this suit Arthur Shirlaw sued for an
order for the restitution of conjugal rites
against Amy Violet Eva Shirlaw (formerly
Cotter). The parties were married in June,
1905, at Bathurst, according to the Anglican
rites. Petitioner, for whom Mr. Collins (in¬
structed by Messrs. F. B. Kenny and Son, of
Bathurst, through their Sydney agents, Messrs.
Fulton, Lowe, and Fulton) appeared, was
granted an order in the usual terms.
PETERSEN v PETERSEN.
A decree for the restitution of conjugal
rites was granted in the suit brought by Val¬
demar Emil Petersen against Jessie McGregor
Petersen (formerly Grubb). The parties were
married in Sydney in February, 1915, accord¬
ing to the Presbyterian rites. Mr. Toose (in¬
structed by Mr. J. T. Ralston) appeared for
the petitioner.
ACTON v ACTON.
On the grounds of desertion, Jennie Acton
(formerly McFarlane) was granted a decree
nisi, returnable in six months, against Robert
Acton. The marriage took place in July, 1908,
in Sydney, according to the rites of the Ro¬
man Catholic Church. Mr. Evans (instruct¬
ed by Mr. J. Birnie Jackson) appeared for the
petitioner.
McCARRY v McGARRY.
George Samuel McGarry sued for a divorce
from Ida Mary McGarry (formerly Boyle) on
the grounds of adultery with Stanley Derrett,
who was joined as co-respondent. The
marriage was solemnised in Sydney in Feb¬
ruary, 1904, according to the Roman Catholic
rites. Petitioner, for whom Mr. R. W.
Fraser appeared, was granted a decree nisi
returnable in six months.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PERSONAL.
VICE-REGAL.
His Excellency the Governor-General, at¬
tended by Captain the Hon. C. Mulholland,
CB.E., D.S.O., military secretary, and Lieuten¬
ant W. J. Seymour, A.D.C., R.N., will arrive in
Sydney this morning by the Melbourne express.
Her Excellency Lady Forster, attended by
Captain Alan Lawrence, A.D.C., was present at
the Junior Red Cross pageant at the Town Hall
yesterday evening.
Dame Margaret Davidson, attended by Miss
Henderson, opened the Junior Red Cross exhi¬
bition at the Town Hall yesterday afternoon.
Mr. and Mrs. Lionel Manchee and Miss Man¬
chee were the guests of his Excellency and
Dame Margaret Davidson to luncheon yester¬
day.
Dr. Earle Page, the Federal Treasurer, who
arrived from Melbourne yesterday, was suf¬
fering from a severe cold, and went direct to
Strathfield, where be was confined to his home
all day.
Senator Gardiner (N.S.W.) was reappointed
leader, Senator M'Dougall (N.S.W.) deputy
leader, and Senator Needham (W.A.) party
whip, at a meeting of the Labour senators in
Melbourne yesterday.
The Chief Justice (Sir Wm. Cullen) and Lady
Cullen will leave Sydney to-night for Mudgee,
where the winter holiday will be spent.
Mr. Peter Board, late Director of Education,
is at present on a visit to Adelaide, where he
is inquiring into matters relating to technical
and apprenticeship education.
Mr. W. H. Atherton, managing director of
Turner, Atherton, and Co., Ltd., hatters' en¬
gineers, of Stockport, England, and also a
director of the Stockport Ring Spinning Co.,
Ltd., is at present on a short visit to Australia
for health reasons. He Is staying in Sydney,
but will leave for England again on July 21
by the Ascanius.
Mr. Paul Marcus, who has been Vice-Consul
for France in Sydney for many years, and who
has been transferred to Shanghai, will make
his departure in a few days.
The Boyd Dunlop Morehead Exhibition for
the most meritorious student proceeding from
the Sydney Grammar School to Sydney Uni¬
versity this year, tenable for three years, has
been awarded to Victor Radcliff Webb, only
son of Mr. J. M. Webb, of Parliament House.
Mr. Reg. Powell, Australasian advertising
manager for Perdriau Rubber Co., Ltd., who
is severing his connection with the company,
was the recipient of a case of cutlery and
silver, and a complete smoker's outfit, from his
fellow employees.
Mr. A. O. Manning, M.P., has been elected
representative of New South Wales at the in¬
terstate Pastoral Conference, to be held in
Melbourne next week, regarding the British
Empire Exhibition.
Mr. M. E. Tilnay, who was formerly a Com¬
missioner of Repatriation in New South Wales,
has assumed the duties of his new office as a
member of the Victorian Repatriation Commis¬
sion.
Mr. C. H. P. Robinson has been appointed
principal Parliamentary reporter on the Fede¬
ral "Hansard" staff, to succeed Mr. B. H.
Friend, who has retired from the position. Mr.
W. Adams has been appointed second Parlia¬
mentary reporter.
Mr. Geo. H, James, district superintendent
for Messrs. Allen Taylor and Co., accom¬
panied by Mrs. James, left Sydney on Thursday
by the Niagara on a tour of inspection of
British Columbia and the Pacific Coast of
America.
Senator E. J. Russell (Vic), who has been
in ill-health for some weeks, has been granted
two months' leave of absence by the Senate.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CASUALTIES.
-----------¬
DROWNING CASES.
Believed to have taken a fit and fallen into
the water, Thomas Sterling, 23 years or age, of
Hillcote-crescent, Manly, was found drowned
near the rocks at Fairlight yesterday. He
left his home on the previous evening to go
fishing at Fairlight, and owing to his not re¬
turning that night a search was made yes¬
terday. Sergeant Dale, of the Manly police,
found portion of the young man's clothing on
the rocks and a piece of fishing line, and
hidden in seaweed nearby be found the body.
It is stated that Sterling was subject to fits,
and the police believe that he was overcome
by an attack whilst fishing near the water's
edge.
ALBURY, Sunday.
Thomas Benjamin O'Donnell a labourer,
aged 60, who was making down the river to
Moama to secure employment on the railway
construction works, was drowned in the
Murray yesterday afternoon, near the Goora¬
nadda Hotel, on the Victorian side, opposite
Howlong.
He was on the bank of the river, when he
is supposed to have taken a fit. He was
seen to fall backwards into shallow water,
but was not seen again. Dragging operations
were undertaken by Constable Bartholomew,
of Howlong, and Constables Bell and Barnett,
of Albury, but the body had not been re¬
covered at 9 p.m.
One brother of O'Donnell, John O'Donnell,
resides in Wodonga, and another in Albury.
KYOGLE. Monday.
Yesterday at Wyangarie a man named Crai¬
gie met his death by drowning in the river.
He had been working for a local farmer for
some time, and was 65 years of age.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CITRUS SHIPMENTS.
The steamer Jervis Bay, which leaves
Sydney to-day, is carrying the last consign¬
ment of fresh fruit for the British Empire
Exhibition. This consignment consists of
76 cases donated by the Central Mangrove
Citrus Packing House Ltd., and the Maroota,
Glenorie, and Kenthurst branches of the
Fruitgrowers' Association of New South
Wales.
Although this shipment is a few cases
short of the total requirements, it is the
only one which has been so. Considering
that this State has supplied nearly twice its
original quota, owing to supplies from
Queensland not having been forthcoming, the
growers who have interested themselves in
the matter must be congratulated.
The same steamer is also carrying 350
cases of citrus fruit from the New South
Wales Central Citrus Association, Ltd., made
up of consignments of Navels, Silettas, and
Parramatta, from the Wyong, Gosford, Cen¬
tral Mangrove, and Somersby citrus packing
houses, and from Mr. F. A. Nicolson, of
Glenorie.
The results of this shipment will be eagerly
looked for, particularly as to the keeping
qualities of Sllettas and Parramattas, and
their suitability for the English.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
DRIVER'S MISTAKE.
HOLIDAY PARTY ENDANGERED.
A serious accident was averted on Satur¬
day night, when, on the Camden-road, about
two miles from Cambelltown, a motor lorry,
driven by F. Hopkins, of Leichhardt, and con¬
taining a large party of excursionists, was
travelling along the road, when a portion of
road under reconstruction necessitated a
deviation. The driver inadvertently ran
across an open paddock. Suddenly the lorry
raced down a sharp decline into a deep gully,
and Hopkins, realising that he had made a
mistake, forced the brakes on. The lorry
struck the bottom and tilted over on one
side, hurtling its crowd of holiday-makers into
a heap. Luckily the vehicle did not turn
completely over.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
SCHOOL-CHURCH AT SANS SOUCI
The foundation-stone of a new Roman
Catholic school-church, to be built with a
frontage to Broughton-Street, Sans Souci, was
recently placed in position. The walls of the
building are of brickwork, the exterior wall¬
ing being cavity, and of sufficient thickness
to enable an extra story being added at a
later date. The foundations are of reinforced
concrete. The total length of the building
will be 60 feet by a width of 30 feet. This
space can be divided into three class-rooms
by the means of folding partitions. In addi¬
tion to this floor area there is a brick sanc¬
tuary, provided at one end, with a sacristy
for the clergy. Along one side of the build¬
ing, which will be roofed with tiles, a 10 feet
wide verandah is provided. The elevations
are of a neat design, and the construction
is in O.K. bricks, with cement dressings. The
work is being carried out by Messrs. S. Patrick
and Sons, builders, of Bexley, to designs
prepared by Mr. Austin McKay, architect.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
HORATIO BOTTOMLEY.
LONDON, Oct. 11.
The first public lecture given by Mr. Horatio
Bottomley since his release from prison at¬
tracted thousands to Queen's Hall. Prolonged
cheers greeted his reading of a letter from
the foreman of the Jury at his trial, saying,
"It was a cruel sentence, and it came as a
great surprise to me and other members of the
jury. We had not expected anything like it.
Our opinion was that you had no intention
of defrauding anyone."
[By special arrangement, Reuter's World
Service, in addition to other special sources
of information, is used for the compilation of
the oversea intelligence published in this
issue, and all rights therein in Australia and
New Zealand reserved.]

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
WOMBEYAN CAVES.
MITTAGONG, Thursday.
The new road to the Wombeyan Caves is
almost completed. At the caves considerable
improvements have been executed, including
the installation of electric light. The pro¬
posed road from the Jenolan to the Wombeyan
Caves has been surveyed and levels are being
taken. This road, instead of following the old
track over the Abercrombie Range, thus in¬
cluding many steep grades, keeps to the ridge
of the mountains, which offers a splendid
gravel surface and few hills. Increasing num¬
bers of tourists are utilising Mittagong as
a starting point for the caves, which are 43
miles away.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MR. ALEXANDER VEITCH.
Many of the bowlers and Freemasons of
Sydney will regret to hear of the death of
Mr. Alexander Veitch, of Wellington, New
Zealand, which took place after a brief
illness at his residence in that city on the
5th inst. Mr. Veitch has for many years
past spent the winter months of the year in
Sydney, and was a member of the Mosman and
Neutral Bay bowling clubs, also of the Masonic
Club and the Commercial Travellers' Club in
Sydney. The older members of those clubs
will miss his genial personality. He leaves
a family of one son and five daughters, two
of the latter, Mrs. H. T. Brittain and Mrs. S.
T. H. Elliott, have been residents of Neutral
Bay for many years.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
NEAR AND FAR.
Lady Street presided at a large and re¬
presentative meeting in the lounge at the
Wentworth yesterday, when arrangements
were made to hold "The Journalists' Ball" on
June 30 at the Wentworth. The proceeds of
the dance will be given to the benevolent
fund of the Australian Journalists' Associa¬
tion. Mr. T. W. Spencer explained that the
benevolent fund had been started some years
ago, and was for the purpose of providing
financial assistance to journalists who, by
reason of ill-health, old age, or unemployment,
required help. The revenue received from
money already invested in this fund was about
£240 per year. But there were increasing
demands being made upon it, and more money
was required. A supper-dance was decided
upon, and arrangements were made for com¬
petitive fancy sets. The office-bearers ap¬
pointed were as follow: - President, Lady
Street; deputy-president, Mr. T. W. Spencer;
vice-presidents, Mrs. T. R. Bavin, Lady Fair¬
fax, Lady Denison, Lady MacCallum, Mrs. V.
Bennet, Mrs. E. G. Knox, Mrs V. Molesworth,
Mrs. Q. S. Spedding, Mrs. T. W. Spencer, Mrs.
Norman Lowe, Mrs. H. S. Dettman, Mrs.
MaClurcan, Mr. W. P. Tiernan, Mr. N. Free¬
hill; hon. organiser, Mrs. M. W. Liddell; hon.
secretary, Mr. R. Harper; hon. treasurer, Mr.
C. C. Faulkner.
The "Sunshine Fair," which is the annual
united effort by women of the churches and
temperance organisations for the prohibition
campaign, will be opened by Mrs. Richard
Arthur in the basement of the Sydney Town
Hall at 2.30 p.m. to-day. Miss Edith Kil¬
minster and Mrs. George Alexander will give
musical items during the afternoon, and in
the evening there will be a programme by
girls from the Salvation Army Home. Mrs.
A. A. Kemp is the president, and Miss E. M.
Andrews the secretary.
Lady de Chair will present the cheques to
representatives of the participating charities
incorporated in the United Charities Fund on
Thursday, at 8 p.m., In the King's Hall, Hun¬
ter-street. Sir Dudley de Chair will give
an address, and Mr. B. R. Fleming, Chief
Civic Commissioner, will preside. A special
musical programme has been arranged.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
AMUNDSEN.
STILL MISSING.
LONDON, July.
The correspondent of the British United
Press at Tromsoe telegraphed to London to¬
day; "It is reported that Captain Amundsen
has been rescued near the south coast of
Spitzbergen by the British yacht Albion."
Since receipt of the foregoing message,
however, the Albion, which is north of Trom¬
soe, reports having picked up by wireless the
information broadcast from England that it
had discovered the missing explorer.
A report from Oslo says that the committee
organising the search for Amundsen declined
the offer of British light planes, owing to
the delay that would take place in them
reaching Tromsoe.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
SINGLETON QUARTER SESSIONS.
SINGLETON, Friday.
Judge White presided at the Singleton
Quarter Sessions, which concluded yesterday.
Michael Joseph Maher was charged with
breaking and entering the premises of Henry
William Bourke, mercer, and stealing a quan¬
tity of goods valued at £48, and with having
received the goods knowing them to be stolen.
He was sentenced to two years' imprisonment,
but at Maher's request his Honor made the
term one year and 11 months to allow ac¬
cused to avoid the detention usual in two¬
year cases.
William Ernest McTaggart, a grazier, of
Howes Valley, who was proceeded against on
a charge of cattle stealing, was found not
guilty.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CALL THE AMBULANCE.
DANGER OF UNSKILLED
TREATMENT.
When people are injured it is not advis¬
able to transport them to hospital in private
vehicles if ambulance facilities are available.
The secretary of the New South Wales
Ambulance Transport Board (Mr. J. L. R.
Cameron) said yesterday that in the case of
accident the proper procedure was to tele¬
phone for the ambulance. Ambulance sta¬
tions were open day and night ready to render
assistance to the injured or sick. Owing to
unskilled treatment a simple fracture might
become a compound fracture, thus endanger¬
ing a limb, or frequently the life of the
patient.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
AMAZING VOYAGE.
Schooner Adrift in
Hurricane.
SWEPT ACROSS ATLANTIC.
WOMAN PASSENGER'S ORDEAL.
(British Official Wireless.)
LONDON, Jan. 18.
The three-masted British schooner
Neptune, of St. John's, Newfoundland,
a wooden vessel of 126 tons, has been
towed into Tobermory Bay, on the west
coast of Scotland by the lighthouse
steamer Hesperus, after remarkable ad¬
ventures.
She was literally blown across the
Atlantic in the wrong direction.
The Neptune left St John's on November
29 on a coastal voyage of 100 miles to New¬
town, Bonavista Bay, Newfoundland. She
was within 30 miles of her intended port
when she was compelled by head winds t0
make for the open sea. Her complement con¬
sisted of the captain, mate, a crew of three,
and five passengers, including one woman.
Without warning came a hurricane, before
which the ship was driven helplessly. The
captain had no idea of his bearings. With
boats and bulwarks smashed, the deckhouse
washed away, and only two spokes left in the
steering wheel, the Neptune was at the mercy
of the mountainous seas for seven weeks.
The vessel was well provisioned, but there
was anxiety that the water might run out,
which eventually became a grave possibility.
At last a rocky coast was seen ahead. It was
Ardnamurchan Point. The schooner's dis¬
tress was observed from the lighthouse, which
signalled to the steamer Hesperus.
CAPTAIN'S STORY.
(Copyright .— Australian Press Association)
"After the storm I had no idea of my posi¬
tion," said Captain Job K. Barkow. "My
knowledge of navigation is limited to my usual
route and I am seldom out of sight of land.
Therefore, although a large steamer gave us
a course, I was little the wiser. We picked
up a light, which I thought was Eddystone, on
January 14, but it was Skerrymore, on the
west coast of Scotland. We lived on the
cargo of fruit, but the water was rationed to
a glass per day."
Mrs Humphreys, the wife of one of the
crew, said that for long periods she was con¬
fined to the forecastle with the men. It was
impossible to remain aft, owing to tremen¬
dous seas.
Four out of the ten men on board were
passengers bound for Newtown, Newfound¬
land.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MEMORIAL TO JOHN BAXTER.
PERTH. Thursday.
The Western Australian Historical Society
has decided to erect a tablet on a cairn be¬
tween Eucla and Esperance to the memory
of John Baxter, the explorer, a companion
of Edward John Eyre, who was killed on the
spot by a native on April 29, 1841. Mr.
John W. W. Graham, who has undertaken
to affix the tablet, was the boy of the party
which, in 1882, found Baxter's remains.
Eyre and Baxter, with two New South
Wales natives and one from Western Aus¬
tralia, left Port Eyre, South Australia, for
Albany, and, when camped about 40 miles
west of Eyre, Western Australia. Baxter was
shot by one of the New South Wales blacks.
During Eyre's absence, two blacks decamped
with two guns and most of the rations.
Baxter died soon after the return of Eyre,
who left the body covered with a blanket on
the stony ground, and, after great privations,
with the Western Australian black reached
the coast near Esperance, where he was picked
up by a French whaling boat.
The site of the tragedy is about 15 miles
south of the trans-Australian car route.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CAPTAIN COOK'S VOYAGES.
"Captain Cook and his Naturalists" was
the subject of a lantern lecture given before
the Naturalists' Society of New South Wales
by Mr. Tom Iredale, conchologist to the Aus¬
tralian Museum. Mr. Iredale declared that
Sir Joseph Banks, rather than Captain Cook,
was the father of Australia. Cook was purely
a navigator, whereas Banks, as a naturalist
and an artist, was deeply impressed with natu¬
ral phenomena, and in turn impressed others
with the subjects which he had studied.
Cook had taken down word for word in his
log book what Banks and Solander had told
him. Hence the common mistake often made
that Cook himself was a naturalist.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
IN PROBATE.
(Before Mr. Justice Harvey.)
WILL OF AMY JONES.
By order of February 3 Alice Mary Went¬
worth, caveatrix, was directed to show cause
why probate should not be granted to the
Perpetual Trustee Company of the will of
Amy Jones, made on September 6, 1928, and
it was further ordered that a document dated
January 5, 1931, purporting to be the last
will of deceased, should be propounded by
the persons named in the order.
By her will of 1928 testatrix made alter¬
native devices and bequests in favour of her
adopted son to meet the position created by
any possible judgment of the Court of Appeal
regarding the estate of her deceased husband.
The document of January, 1931, was found
near her body when on January 6 she was
discovered dead in her residence at Kogarah.
Her death was a mystery, but was generally
attributed to heart failure, injuries to the head
having been caused by falling on to the stove
in the kitchen. By the will executed on
the previous day, Mrs. Jones left all her real
and personal estate to her brothers and sisters,
except a bequest of £500 to Mrs. Wentworth,
and two small bequests to others. The docu¬
ment purported to have been witnessed by "J.
T. Scott" and "A. Carmichael." All efforts
to discover these persons had since proved
fruitless.
On the matter now being called it was stated
that the relatives of testatrix did not propose
to enter upon a suit, but would submit all
the facts to the Court. The caveatrix did
not contest probate, but would make submis¬
sions as to costs.
The reading of documents had not con¬
cluded when the Court adjourned.
Mr. J. R. Nield (instructed by Messrs. Sly
and Russell) appeared for the trustees; Mr. T.
T. Henery (instructed by Messrs. Rowley,
Roseby, and Co.) for certain relatives; and Mr.
E. R. Mann for the caveatrix.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MRS. JAMES GARTRELL.
Mrs. James Gartrell died on Tuesday last
at Marrickville, aged 92 years. She was born
in Cornwall, England, and in 1877 came to
Australia with her husband, the late Mr.
James Gartrell and 24 other members of the
family, including Mr. Frank Gartrell of
Gartrell-White Ltd. Mrs. Gartrell and family
went to Orange, where she resided for 21 years,
thence to Dubbo, and had resided in Marrick
ville for 25 years. Mrs. Gartrell was the mother
of 13 children, of whom eight sons and one
daughter survive. There are 27 grandchildren
and 17 great-grandchildren.
The funeral took place on Wednesday at
Rookwood.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
BIGAMY CHARGE.
Amazing Defence.
JUDGE DISCHARGES JURY.
MELBOURNE, Thursday.
Mr. Justice Wasley told a jury to-day that
there had not been such startling develop¬
ments in the Criminal Court for many years
as those which occurred at the trial of Wil¬
liam Leslie Edmund Penney, 31, salesman,
on a charge of bigamy.
Penney was charged under the name of
Billy Edwards, and the Crown Prosecutor
(Mr. Book) alleged that he had gone through
the ceremony of marriage with a young woman
at Trafalgar in August, 1930, while his wife
by a previous marriage, which was said to
have taken place at Drouin in April, 1930,
was living.
The trial ended by Mr. Justice Wasley dis¬
charging the jury, and remanding Edwards
to allow the Crown to investigate his defence.
The defence had evoked sharp comments
from Mr. Justice Wasley yesterday. Mr.
Stretton, who defended Edwards, had de¬
clared that the charge must fail, because
neither of the marriages referred to by the
Crown was lawful, as Edwards had had a
prior marriage in 1921.
Mr. Justice Wasley would not allow a certi¬
ficate of that marriage to be admitted as
evidence, neither would he allow Edwards's
mother to be recalled to testify that the
woman whom Edwards was said to have
married in 1921 was still alive.
When the trial was resumed to-day, how¬
ever, Mr. Justice Wasley said that he thought
that he should allow Edwards's mother to be
recalled. Mr. Stretton had made a slip
inadvertently, and he should be allowed to
correct it.
Mr. Stretton: I have better evidence now.
The real wife is here.
This woman, called as a witness, said that
she had married Edwards in September, 1921,
and they had separated after 15 months.
Mr. Justice Wasley discharged the jury,
and remanded Edwards.

at Trafalgar in August, 1930, while his wife
by a pievlous marriage, which was said to
have tal.cn place at Drouin in April, 1930,
was living.
I The trial ended by Mr. Justice Wasley dis-
charging the jury, and remanding Edwards
to allow the Crown to investigate his defence.
The defence had evoked sharp comments
from Mr. Justice Wasley yesterday. Mr.
Stretton, who defended Edwards, had de-
clared that the charge must fall, because
neither of the marriages referred to by the
Crown was lawful, as Edwards had had a
prior marriage in 1921.
Mr. Justice Wasley would not allow a certi-
ficate of that marriage to be admitted as
evidence, neither would he allow Edwards's
mother to be recalled to testify that the
woman whom Edwards was said to have
married in 1921 was still alive.
When the trial was resumed to-day, how-
ever, Mr. Justice Wasley said that he thought
that he should allow Edwards's mother to be
recalled Mr. Stretton had made a slip
Inadvertently, and he should be allowed to
correct it.
Mr. Stretton: I have better evidence now.
The real wife Is here.
This woman, called as a witness, said that
she had married Edwards in September, 1921,
and they had separated after 15 months.
Mr. Justice Wasley discharged the Jury,
and remanded Edwards._

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE UNIONS.
RUBBER WORKERS' STRIKE.
The strike at the Sydney rubber works,
Drummoyne, is to be continued. A conference
was held yesterday between the management
of the works and representatives of the Rubber
Workers' Union, but no agreement, was
reached. The works are owned by Perdriau¬
Dunlop, Ltd. The twenty men affected are
asking for higher bonus rates. No extension
of the strike is yet likely. The men receive
a minimum wage to which bonus payments
are added.
PRINTING TRADES UNION.
At a special general meeting of the Amal¬
gamated Printing Trades Employees' Union
at the Trades Hall to-morrow night, the
secretary of the Lang A.L.P. (Mr. Graves)
will be asked to explain to members why their
union was not notified of the ballots at which
delegates to the Easter conference were
elected. The business paper says: "Mr. Graves
will be present to reply to certain happenings
between this union and the A.L P."

DISTRICT COURT.
(Before Judge Thomson and a jury of four.)
TRAM DRIVER'S CLAIM.
Frederick Montgomery, tram driver, of
Undercliffe, sought to recover from the Com¬
missioner for Road Transport and Tramways
£2 alleged to be due to him as wages while
he was totally, and later partially, incapaci¬
tated, following a tram collision in Prince's
Highway, Tempe, on January 19, 1935.
Plaintiff's claim was brought under section
124 of the Transport Act, 1930, which pro¬
vides that if any officer received personal
injuries arising out of and in the course of his
employment, except where the injury was
caused by his own gross negligence or wilful
or rongful act, he was entitled, during par¬
tial or total disablement, to the salary he was
receiving it the time of the injury.
The defence was a denial of liability and
pleas that plaintiff was not an officer within
the meaning of the section, and that he had
been guilty of gross negligence.
By direction a verdict was given for plain¬
tiff, for £16/15/4.
Mr G. Amsberg and Mr W. Redapple (in¬
structed by Messrs Landa and Lamaro) ap¬
peared for plaintiff, and Mr Rex Chambers
(instructed by the Solicitor for Transport) for
defendant.
(Before Judge Thomson.)
COMPENSATION TO EMPLOYEE
The Dairy Farmers' Co-operative Milk Com¬
pany, Ltd , sued Sabina Diamond, of Bunyula¬
road, Bellevue Hill for £63/7/6, alleged to be
due as compensation paid by the plaintiff
company to J. A. Stalk, one of its employees,
for injuries received, together with medical
and nursing charges, as the result of being
struck by a car. It was alleged that the
accident was due to defendant's negligence
The defence was a denial of the allegations
and a plea that the accident was due to Stark's
negligence.
A verdict was given for plaintiff for the
amount claimed.
Mr W. B. Simpson (instructed by Messrs
Dawson, Waldron, Edwards, and Nicholls) ap¬
peared for plaintiff company, and Mr G. P.
Stuckey (instructed by Messrs Stephen,
Jaques, and Stephen) for defendant.
(Before Judge Markell.)
HORSE TRAINER'S CLAIM.
Frank Taverna, horse trainer, of Thomas-
street, Ultimo, sued J. Rosen and M. Rosen,
trading as Rosen Bros, of Anzac-parade, Ken¬
sington, for £24/7/6, alleged to be due for the
serving of defendant's mares, Eileen Wood and
Tatari, by plaintiff's stallion Valamita, and
also for certain agistment fees.
The defence was a denial of liability and a
plea that plaintiff had agreed upon a total fee
of £ 30, subject to certain conditions.
Judgment was given for plaintiff for
£24/7/6 including £10 paid into court.
Mr T P. MacMahon (instructed by Mr
L. C. Abigail) appeared for plaintiff, and Mr
R. M. Sturt (instructed by Mr John S.
Heaney) for defendants.
(Before Judge Curlewis. )
PARTNERSHIP ALLEGED
Bertha Michael, of Balmain sued the Public
Trustee, as administrator of the estate of the
late John Donnelly, of Lakemba, for £400,
alleged to be due to her as the unliquidated
balance in respect of a partnership, which,
she alleged, had existed between her and Don¬
nelly in mixed businesses at Lakemba and
Punchbowl.
The defence was a denial of the partner¬
ship.
Plaintiff was non-suited.
Mr Gordon Wallace (instructed by Messrs
Mervyn Finlay and Jennings) appeared for
plaintiff, and Mr. L. C. Badham (instructed
by Messrs Garrett, Christie and Buckley) for
defendant.
COMMISSION ON LEASE
Jack Thomson Cummings of Oxford-street,
Bondi, sued Labor Motor Funerals, Limited,
of Barrack-street, Sydney, for £10/16/, alleged
to be due as commission for securing a lease
of premises at Newtown. George Frederick
Davis of the Property Owners' Association,
was joined as a defendant.
Plaintiff alleged that he was instructed by
the company to secure the lease of premises
in Enmore-road, Newtown. Later he was
refused commission.
The defence was a denial of the instruction
or of any agreement to pay commission.
Judgment was given for defendant.
Mr Frank Hidden (instructed by Messrs
Savegh, Cotter, and Co) appeared for plain¬
tiff, and Mr. M. F. Hardy (instructed by
Cleary and Callachor) for Labor Motor
Funerals, Limited.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CASUALTIES.
EXPLOSION AT RYDE.
James Howden 44 of Cooper-Street,
Waterloo, and Eugene Colloty, 40, of Bellevue¬
street, Glebe were working in a trench at the
corner of Victoria and Monash roads, Ryde,
yesterday when there was an explosion of
gas which had leaked into the trench. They
both suffered burns to the face and arms,
and were taken to Ryde Hospital by the Cen¬
tral District Ambulance.
THROWN THROUGH CAR WINDOW.
NEWCASTLE, Thursday.
William Robert Stokes of Rocky Point-road,
Ramsgate reported to the Newcastle police to¬
night that while he was sitting in his motor
car, stationary at the corner of Tudor-street
and Gordon-avenue, Hamilton, a bicycle came
into collision with the car. The rider of the
bicycle was thrown through the window and
his head struck Stokes on the chin. Neither
man was seriously injured.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ETHEL TURNER'S ACHIEVEMENTS.
Recognition by Fellow Writers.
Ethel Turner, the famous Australian author of "Seven Little Australians,"
and more than a score of other children's books, holds the key to a chil¬
dren's paradise in which grown-ups are allowed to roam. To recognise her
literary achievement the Fellowship of Australian Writers will entertain her
at luncheon at Farmer's Blaxland Galleries on June 25.
Mrs.
H. R. CURLEWIS (ETHEL TURNER) looks at the world from the
window of her Mosman home.
IN private life, the story-teller is Mrs. H
R Curlewis, wife of Judge Curlewis. To
meet her you see a blue-eyed bustling little
person with greying hair and a pink and
white complexion many a girl would envy.
She keeps that fresh outlook on life which
has made so much of the charm of her writ¬
ing during the past 40 years. Mrs Curlewis
is a gracious woman with a fine sense of
humour and a real understanding of boys and
girls.
Her home is on the shores of Sydney Har¬
bour looking towards the sea. This children's
story-teller was born near Doncaster in York¬
shire, but since her arrival from England
when she was eight years of age her life has
become merged in the stream of Australian
life and literature.
She was educated at that famous square
brick building in Elizabeth-street Sydney, the
Girls' High School-which has nourished so
many bright hopes and efforts. Even at
school Ethel Turner wrote stories; with her
sister, Lilian, she edited "Iris," a schoolgirls'
magazine.
Busy Literary Pen.
MRS. CURLEWIS'S real literary life com¬
menced when she wrote her "Children's
Page" in the "Illustrated Sydney News." In
1894 "Seven Little Australians" was published,
and since then the busy pen has never rested.
This year she contributed one of the reddest
chapters to "Murder Pie," a collaborated effort
by several Australian writers.
More than a million copies of "Seven Little
Australians" have been sold. That it is a story
for all ages and all peoples is indicated by
the fact that it has been translated into
Danish, Norwegian, Swedish, Dutch, French,
and Czechoslovakian.
An Example for Authors.
THE quiet persistence of this small figure at
her desk writing destroying, destroying
and working again perfecting style and plot
and winning at last a well-deserved success
holds a lesson for many an ambitious author.
Mrs. Curlewis found it no trouble to write a
book a year and with one or two exceptions
all her books have been about children.
"Some days indeed," she has confessed, "the
pen will not write. You sit down and make
sketches on the blotting paper. But as a rule
I went to my room about ten o clock and
by noon or so I had done my days work. So
many days work to a chapter, so many chap¬
ters to a book—a regular literary arithmetic."
Interrupted Arithmetic
SOMETIMES the arithmetic in the early
days was not so easy for a son and daugh¬
ter intercepted the methodical dip into the ink¬
bottle. Now there are a grandson and grand¬
daughter to read her books and demand more
of the favourite characters.
In the midst of writing her thirty-odd books
Mrs. Curlewis —who was one of the first
people to write books for children without a
moral —has found time to surf, play tennis
and golf, and potter about the lovely garden
of her home. At the moment the garden wins
over the ink-pot but one suspects that Mrs.
Curlewis is still very much in love with ''Seven
Little Australians;" perhaps the three-year-
old Phillipa and the five year-old Ian will
soon persuade their grandmother to write
more about the lovable Judy.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
QUARTER SESSIONS.
(Before Acting Judge Edwards.)
Crown Prosecutor, Mr. L. J. McKean, K.C.
(instructed by the Clerk of the Peace).
RECEIVER SENTENCED.
Frederick Charles Schmelz, 59, agent, found
guilty of receiving furs knowing them to have
been stolen, was sentenced to 18 months'
imprisonment.
Mr. J. C. S. Cordell (instructed by Mr.
Irving L. Addison) appeared for the prisoner.
ROBBERY UNDER ARMS.
Leslie Clarence Guy, 21, cook, was charged
that on April ¡24, at Lillyfield, being armed with
a revolver and in company with Leslie George
Knight and Clarence Patrick Booth, he
assaulted Norman Collyer and robbed him
of £31/8/.
Accused pleaded guilty. Mr. Stan Taylor
instructed by Mr. W. C. Taylor) urged in
mitigation of sentence that accused had been
unemployed, and being easily led, had drifted
into bad company. His parents were respec¬
table, and he himself had borne a good
character up till March of this year.
His Honor said he would pass sentence this
morning.
MANLY OUTING.
William Sydney Francis Knight, 31, cleaner,
and William Raines, 31, clerk, pleaded not
guilty to a charge of assaulting Rudolph
Weber, at Manly, on July 18, with intent to
rob him.
Weber, a pastrycook, stated that two men
suddenly attacked him in a lane off the Corso,
and were going through his pockets when a
passing police car frightened them off. The
accused Knight was arrested and identified as
the man who hit him on the face, knocking
him down. The accused Raines was identified
later as the other assailant.
Knight's defence was that any trouble
with Weber was the result of a brawl, and
that no attempt was made to rob him. Raines
denied that he was there at all, and his Honor
described the evidence of identification as
very weak.
The jury acquitted both accused, and they
were discharged.
Mr. Stan Taylor (instructed by Mr. W. C.
Taylor) appeared for Raines.
(Before Judge White.)
Crown Prosecutor, Mr. T. S. Crawford. K.C.
(instructed by Mr. Hardman, of the Com¬
monwealth Crown Solicitor's Office.)
PATENTS ACT PROSECUTION.
The trial of Joseph Frederick Wirz, 34,
engineer, for alleged breaches of the Patents
Act in relation to letters patent for a spring
mattress, was concluded. It was alleged in
three counts that accused made false state¬
ments in communications to the Patents'
Office.
The jury found the accused guilty on the
second and third counts and not guilty on
the first. He was remanded till Wednesday for sentence.
Mr. Spender, K.C, and Mr. A. G. Thomas
(Instructed by Mr. Gilbert. Johnstone) con¬
ducted the defence.
DOMAIN STABBING CASE.
Salvatore Calabro, 29, Antonio Calabro, 39,
and Pasquali Calabro, 36, bootmakers, were
charged with having on June 13 maliciously
wounded Allan Lindsay Arnold Hewitt and
caused him grievous bodily harm, A second
count charged them with common assault.
The accused pleaded not guilty.
According to the Crown case, conducted by
Mr. Crawford, K.C. instructed by the Clerk
of the Peace), Hewitt, in militia uniform, was
walking through the Domain to his work,
when he met the accused. They blocked his
path and as he made to push through them a
scuffle occurred. Hewitt, struck by Salvatore,
retaliated and broke the Italian's nose. He
then stooped to gather up some parcels he
had been carrying, and was stabbed in the
back by one of the Italians. Hewitt claimed
that, the Italians were the aggressors and
that they made insulting remarks concerning
his uniform and the King.
The defence alleged that Hewitt was the
aggressor; that, he made insulting remarks
about Mussolini and the Abyssinian war, and
referred to them as dagos.
The trial will be continued this morning.
Mr. Curtis, K.C., and Mr. Sheahan (in¬
structed by Messrs. Lamaro and Magrath)
appeared for Salvatore Calabro, and Mr. Kin¬
kead and Mr. Sheahan, instructed by the same
firm) for the other two accused.

monwealth Crown Solicitor's Office.)
PATENTS ACT PROSECUTION.
The trial of Joseph Frederick Wirz, 34,
engineer, for alleged breaches of the Patents
Act in relation to letters patent for a spring
mattress, was concluded. It was alleged in
three counts that accused made false state-
ments in communications to tile Patenta'
Office.
The jury found the accused guilty on the
? second and third counts and not guilty on
' the first. He was remanded till Wednesday
Mr. Spender, K.C, and Mr. A. G. Thomas
(Instructed by Mr. Gilbert. Johnstone) con-
ducted the defence.
DOMAIN STABBING CASE.
Salvatore Calabro, 29, Antonio Calabro, 39,
and Pasquali Calabro, 30. bootmakers, were
charged with having on June 13 maliciously
wounded Allan Lindsay Arnold Hewitt and
caused him grievous bodily harm, A second
count charged them with common assault.
The accused pleaded not guilty.
According to the Crown case, conducted by
Mr. Crawford, K.C. instructed by the Clerk
of the Peace), Hewitt, in militia uniform, was
walking through the Domain to his work,
when he met the accused. They blocked his
path and as he made to push through them a
scuffle occurred. Hewitt, struck by Salvatore,
retaliated and broke the Italian's nose. He
then stooped ti gather up some parcels he
had been carrying, and was stabbed In the
back by one of the Italians. Hewitt claimed
thal, the Italians were the aggressors and
that they made insulting remarks concerning
his uniform and the King.
The defence alleged that Hewitt was the
aggressor; that, he made insulting remarks
about Mussolini and the Abyssinian war, and
referred to them as dagos.
The trial will be continued this morning.
Mr. Curtis, K.C, and Mr. Sheahan (in-
structed by Messrs. Lamaro and Magrath)
appeared for Salvatore Calabro, and Mr. Kin-
kead and Mr. Sheahan, instructed by the same
firm) for the other two accused.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MOTHERS' DAY APPEAL.
The Mother's Day appeal organised by the
Benevolent Society of New South Wales will
be held on Friday, May 6, when flannel flowers
will be sold by voluntary helpers on all met¬
ropolitan railway stations and in most muni¬
cipalities. The proceeds will go to mothers
and children in the society's Royal Hospital
for Women, Renwick Hospital for Infants,
Scarba Welfare House for Children and the
food relief depot.
Honorary helpers will be welcomed, and
volunteers should communicate with Mr. T. E.
Shonk, general organiser, Benevolent Society
of New South Wales, Thomas Street, Sydney.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
CAR FATALITY.
Finding of Accidental Death.
POLICEMAN EXONERATED.
A finding of accidental death was returned
by the City Coroner, Mr. Oram, at the con¬
clusion yesterday of an inquest on Albert
Victor Anderson, 45, electric meter fixer, of
Rose Street, North Bondi, who died in hos¬
pital on May 28, shortly after having been
struck by a car in Old South Head Road,
near Warner's Avenue, Rose Bay.
The car was driven by Constable William
Aloysius O'Rourke, of No. 2 Police Station,
Sydney, who was accompanied by Constable
Arthur William Manley, of the same sta¬
tion.
The coroner said that he was satisfied that
the tragedy was entirely an accident, and he
could not see that any blame was attachable
to the driver of the car.
The evidence showed that the locality was
badly lighted.
"This inquest has taken a long while,"
he added, "because there were many witnesses,
owing to the fact that the Police Department
is jealous of the reputation of its officers
and wants thorough investigations into mat¬
ters concerning them."

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
PRECAUTIONS OF THE
POWERS.
Bulgarian Reservists.
SOFIA, Aug. 25. (A.A.P.)
It is officially announced that Bulgarian
reservists who were called up on August 1
will remain with the colours for a further 13
days.
The Bulgarian Government has forbidden
the export of war materials.
BRUSSELS, Aug. 25. (A.A.P.)
The Belgian Cabinet has decided on im¬
mediate security measures in "order to place
the country in a condition of active defence."
The Belgian Cabinet, at an emergency
meeting yesterday, decided upon special mea¬
sures to assure a supply of provisions. Sol¬
diers have been recalled from leave, and
the frontiers have been manned.
MALTA, Aug. 25. (A.A.P.)
All defences at Malta have been manned.
The Territorials and auxiliary units have re¬
sponded magnificently to the recruiting call.
Gas masks are being distributed.
An Italian who was alleged to be photo¬
graphing the shore defences has been
arrested.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
NOOGOORA BURR
Macquarie Marshes
Infested.
WARREN, Monday.
In view of the appalling infes¬
tation of the Macquarie Marshes by
noogoora burr, an appeal has been
made to Marthaguy Shire Council
to make representations to have
the marshes declared a quarantine
area.
Nearby settlers are faced with a very
difficult problem, the seed of the burr
being carried on to their properties from
the marshes by the periodical inunda¬
tions of slow-spreading waters and the
thousands of travelling stock which leave
the marsh area annually heavily covered
with matured noogoora burr.
Writing to the shire council one of the
settlers Mr Roland Storm, of Killarney
pointed out that hired labour was out
of the question to deal with the burrs.
Very often the properties carried a cer¬
tain amount of water, and at such times
wasps and hornets were to be found in
countless millions among the heavy
growths of lignum while wild pigs and
snakes were there in considerable num¬
bers.
He quoted an instance when hired
labour beat a frantic retreat from a
continous and concerted attack of mos¬
quitoes. Mr. Storm stated that an in¬
spection of the various routes travelled
by stock from the marsh area revealed
a plain trail of flourishing noogoora burr
plants.
The council decided to refer the matter
to the Department of Agriculture.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
BOXING PROMOTER'S
DIVORCE
Charles Lucas, a well-known boxing
promoter, was granted a decree nisi
by Mr. Justice Street in the Divorce
Court yesterday from Violet Jane
Jamison Lucas, formerly Anderson, on
the ground of her desertion.
Lucas, now a warrant-officer
attached to the physical training
branch of the military forces, said
he went to England in 1921. His wife
arrived there later, but refused to live
with him. He returned to Australia
in 1929 without her, and she had been
in England ever since.
The parties were married in Western
Australia in 1911 when Lucas was 25
and his wife 22.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ADVANCES ANNOUNCED
IN COMMUNIQUE
The communique from Gene¬
ral MacArthur's headquarters
yesterday, announcing the new
advances, stated: ¬
In combined and co-ordinated opera¬
tions, the forces of the South-west
Pacific and South Pacific areas have
advanced and expanded their bases of
deployment by occupying Nassau Bay,
on the north coast of New Guinea, the
Trobriand and Woodlark island groups,
and are attacking in the New Georgia
Group.
The occupation of the Trobriands
and the Woodlark area was success¬
fully accomplished without opposition,
and apparently unbeknown to the
enemy.
In the New Georgia Group, our
forces have successfully landed on New
Georgia and Rendova Islands, and
are engaging the enemy's defences.
All elements, ground, naval, and air,
are being employed in closest syn¬
chronisation.
The forces of the two areas are
operating under their own comman¬
ders, the entire movement being
under the direction of the Comman¬
der-in-Chief, South-west Pacific Area.
NORTH-WESTERN SECTOR:
DARWIN. - Twenty-seven enemy
bombers, escorted by 21 fighters, raided
the area at midday, with some dam¬
age to ground installations and slight
casualties. Our fighters intercepted
and shot down six enemy bombers and
two fighters. Seven bombers and a
fighter were damaged. We lost six
planes in air combat.
TIMOR. - Lautem: Our medium
units bombed the aerodrome after
dark, starting fires.
Fuiloro: Our medium units, in a
night attack, bombed the aerodrome,
causing fires.
Koepang: Our medium bombers at¬
tacked Penfoei aerodrome after dark,
starting numerous large fires in dis¬
persal areas.
NORTH-EASTERN SECTOR: ¬
NEW BRITAIN. - Rabaul: Our
heavy bombers attacked Vunukanau
and Lakunai aerodromes during the
night, dropping nearly 23 tons of high
explosives, fragmentation, and incen¬
diary bombs throughout the dispersal
areas. Several explosions and numer¬
ous fires were observed, one visible for
100 miles. Heavy anti-aircraft fire
and some enemy night fighters were
encountered. One of our bombers is
missing.
Cape Gloucester: One of our heavy
units, on armed reconnaissance, under
difficult weather conditions, bombed
from mast-head height, and strafed
one of three 1,000-ton enemy cargo
ships in Dampier Strait, leaving it
sinking. Another of the vessels, after
being heavily machine-gunned, ap¬
peared damaged, and deserted when
our aircraft departed.
NEW GUINEA. - Salamaua: Our
medium bombers, in support of
ground forces, heavily attacked enemy
positions, and supply installations near
the aerodrome and at Bobdubi.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
LANCASTER ON
LOAN TOUR
Repair Completed
The Lancaster bomber, which
came to Australia last July and
was badly damaged when land¬
ing at a northern New South
Wales town last October during
a Liberty Loan tour, passed
through Sydney on Saturday
from Melbourne on its way to
Brisbane.
The mishap in October was caused
by the machine's over-running a short
runway. It was stated at the time
that repairs would take too long to en¬
able it to continue the loan tour to
Queensland.
The Minister for Air, Mr. Drakeford,
announced recently that repairs had
been nearly completed, and that the
Lancaster would begin a Victory Loan
tour in Queensland on April 17.
Flight-Lieutenant Peter Isaacson is
again in command, and most of the
original crew which brought the
bomber to Australia will be with the
machine on the tour.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
LEVEL-CROSSING
COLLISION
Narrabri Farmers
Injured
NARRABRI, Tuesday. - Two
men were injured when a lorry
struck a train at a level crossing
on the Gunnedah road to-night.
There are no gates at the crossing
and an eye-witness said that the lorry
struck the middle carriage of the train.
The train crew did not realise that
there had been a collision and the
train did not stop immediately.
The injured men were :
Walter Cox, 36, farmer, Gunnedah
Road, Narrabri, fractured jaw, a
probably fractured skull, internal in¬
juries, and shock; William Barnes, 22,
farmer, Bullawa Creek, near Narrabri,
injuries to face and shock.
They were taken by the Namoi¬
Walgett Ambulance to the Narrabri
District Hospital, where they were
admitted.
Mr. Amos Smith, a garage pro¬
prietor, and his son Reuben, who live
near the scene of the accident, heard
the noise of the collision and found
Barnes under the damaged truck.
Cox was found lying across a sleeper
of the railway track.
