ground_truth.transcription_unit:
TWO MURDER
CHARGES
10-year-old Crime
Recalled
FORBES, Tuesday. - The hear¬
ing of two charges of murder
against Ronald William Bath¬
gate, 41, farmer, of Forbes, was
continued before Mr. J. W.
Brown P.M., at Forbes Police
Court to-day.
Bathgate is charged with the
murder of Ethel May Wells 61 of
Forbes and Luigi Origlia, 41, also of
Forbes at Fitzgerald's Bridge near
Forbes on the night of October 9,
1935.
Detective-Sergeant E. J. Barnes of
the CIB Sydney cross-examined by
Mr. R. Meillon for the accused said
that accused was questioned by detec¬
tives for five hours.
Witness said that he had made
inquiries into the character of the
deceased Origlia, and had ascertained
that he was a man of violent temper
and was frequently seen carrying a
rifle.
Counsel for the defence asked
witness whether he knew that the
Chief Justice had said that "any¬
thing in the nature of cross¬
examination of a prisoner, who is un¬
willing to answer questions, is to be
avoided." Witness replied that he did
not know about it.
Replying to further questions
Detective-Sergeant Barnes said that
he had heard that accused had fired
at grasshoppers which got into his
tomato crop.
Herbert Arthur Pymont munition
worker at the Commonwealth Small
Arms Factory at Lithgow, now residing
at 3 Inner Crescent Lithgow, said
that until 1941 he had lived at Kalang
on Condobolin Road near Forbes. He
knew the deceased Wells and Origlia.
On the night of October 9, 1935, he
was riding along Condobolin Road
towards Fitzgerald's Bridge when he
heard several shots. Approaching the
bridge he noticed a horse and sulky.
He proceeded to to Fitzgerald's Bridge
and saw on the bridge the body of a
man. When he heard the shots he
heard a scream like that of a woman
Continuing, Pymont said he did not
see anyone about the bridge at that
time. "I can see the man Bathgate
in court. I know him by sight," said
Pymont.
James Mathew Kalusy, who at the
time of the shooting lived at Forbes
said that he was at the home of Frank
Sullivan near Fitzgerald's Bridge on
the night of October 9 1935. While
at Sullivan's home he heard four or
five shots. Before hearing the shots
Kalusy said he had seen a horse and
sulky travelling along Condobolin Road
towards Fitzgerald's Bridge, and recog¬
nised it as belonging to the deceased
Mrs Ethel May Wells.
The Court adjourned until to¬
morrow.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Hostels Along The Way
The Explorers Went
By A STAFF
CORRESPONDENT
Some day, if the dreams of the Youth
Hostel Association of New South Wales are
realised, bush-walkers will he able to travel in
ten to fifteen-mile stages over the routes taken
by the early explorers of Australia and spend
each night in a hostel at a cost of only 2/6 a
night.
'"THE dream has been
realised in other countries
of the world. In Britain and
on the Continent on holidays
and week-ends thousands of
young people in their late
teens or early twenties, and
many considerably older, are
to be seen on highways and
byways seeing their own
country first and on foot,
travelling the stages between
one hostel and the next.
In Australia, at present, the
number of hostels and the num¬
ber of travellers using them is
necessarily fewer, but the
lian Youth Hostel Association
(which is affiliated with the Inter¬
national Youth Hostel Federation)
has plans to build more and more
overnight resting places provided
it can win enough support from
the young people who will use
them, and their elders who can see
their value.
Already around Sydney in the
areas to which the bush-walkers
make their way there are ten of the
hostels. They include Glen Alva,
near Hazelbrook on the lower Blue
Mountains, Greendale, at North
Kurrajong, The Weir, on the Nepean
River, downstream from Penrith,
Stanwell Tops, on the South Coast,
Towler's Bay, and Apple Tree, in
Kuring-gai Chase, West Head,
Kuring-gai Chase, Juno Head, near
Patonga, on the Hawkesbury, and
Little Marley, in National Park.
The first three are "farm" camps.
They are on private farms, whose
proprietors make the facilities avail¬
able to holders of the association
cards, at the usual nominal fee.
Stanwell Tops, Towler's Bay, and
Apple Tree were furnished by the
National Fitness Council, and the
association rents and operates the
properties. The others are run
by the National Fitness Council.
Strict Rules
The Youth Hostel Association of
N.S.W. has just acquired a new place
at Kiandra in the heart of the
Alpine snowfields which will be open
for members in the forthcoming
winter season.
Members of the association hasten
to point out that these hostels are
not "just cheap boarding-houses."
The maximum stay, except at
Kiandra, is three nights, and there
are strict rules on the use of the
hostels.
At present the hostels between
them are used by about 75 people
each week - that is, about 75 bed
nights a week. In Britain and in
Europe the figures are proportion¬
ately much higher, but there is a
growing demand here by walkers
and cyclists for the overnight accom¬
modation that the hostels provide
within the purse-reach of young
people.
British migrants anxious to see
their new country cheaply and on
foot or bicycle, and who have had
experience of the hostel scheme in
the United Kingdom, are showing
interest in the New South Wales
association, while the walking clubs
number among their members Dan¬
ish and Dutch migrants.
The clubs and organisations which
foster the outdoors cult expect, too,
that as Displaced Person migrants
begin to move out of their camps
and directed employment, into the
community at large, they will pro¬
vide a large number of potential
hostel-users.
The Hostel Association is open to
anyone. Organisations may join at
sixpence per year per member. In¬
dividuals over 21 are charged 10/
a year, and under 21 5/ a year.
The international card with which
members are issued entitles them to
accommodation in any hostel any¬
where in the world.
One result of this wide privilege
is that the association receives five
or six applications for membership
each week from Australians plan¬
ning holidays in Britain and Europe
The plan to build hostels along
the explorers' trails is being carried
out first on the 1813 route of Blax¬
land, Lawson, and Wentworth from
Penrith to Hartley. A chain of over¬
night resting places is envisaged at
intervals of no more than 15 miles
- a fair day's walk. More rugged
walkers will be able to make two
stages in a day if they want to.
The planning of hostels in other
areas in the walking country around
Sydney will be adapted to the local
geography. There would be little
interest in following the explorers
of the western plains, but the trails
on the north and south coast, pass¬
ing through some of the finest
scenery in the State, will some day
be pin-pointed with the simple hos¬
tels where the young walkers can
put up for a night, cook their own
food, and set out the next day on
another leg of the journey.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
I'd Free Prisoners
Week Days—Judge
Judge Shortland said yesterday he believed
prisoners should be allowed to work at their
ordinary jobs during the week and serve their
prison terms at week-ends.
This system had been
adopted in some countries
overseas and should be
tried here, he said.
He was also a great believer
in granting bonds to offenders.
"Eighty per cent. of the people
to whom I have given bonds
have not come back to court,''
he said.
FAREWELL
Judge Shortland was speaking
at a "farewell" held in No. 7
District Court, Queen's Square,
on his retirement after over 11
years on the District Court
Bench.
He said he believed a short
sentence was the worst thing that
could be given to a man. It
should never be given except in
the most urgent cases.
"It is an awful thing for a
man to be taken from his family
and spend three or six months in
gaol—-not that I am a believer
in big sentences," Judge Short¬
land said.
SUCCESS
He had adopted a system of
calling boys and men up for
sentence in six, 12, or 18 months
and had achieved success.
Eleven District Court Judges
and scores of barristers and
solicitors attended the farewell.
The chairman of the District
Court Bench, Judge Nield, paid
tribute to the great amount of
work done by Judge Shortland
and to his speedy and accurate
judgments.
REMARKABLE MAN
The Attorney - General, Mr.
C. E. Martin, M.L.A. (on behalf
of members of the Bar), said
Judge Shortland was a remark¬
able example of a man who had
succeeded in two professions.
He first became headmaster
of Mudgee High School, then a
barrister, and finally a Judge of
the District Court.
"We say farewell to a scholar,
a good Judge, and a wise and
kindly gentleman," said Mr.
Martin.
Mr. H. H. Small, senior mem¬
ber of the Bar present, and Mr.
R. Z. de Ferranti, on behalf of
the Incorporated Law Institute,
also paid tributes to the Judge.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Tests Allay
Fears Of
Myxomatosis
MELBOURNE, Wednesday.
Three tests had shown there
was no connection between
encephalitis and the rabbit-kill¬
ing virus, myxomatosis, Sir
Macfarlane Burnet, director of
the Walter and Eliza Hall In¬
stitute, said to-day.
These tests would be accepted
by scientists anywhere as prov¬
ing there was no relationship be¬
tween the two diseases, he said.
(1) Blood tests had shown that
there was no immunological link
between the encephalitis virus
and the myxomatosis virus.
(2) Encephalitis virus injected
into rabbits had failed to infect
them.
3) Myxomatosis did not affect
mice, but the encephalitis virus
killed them.
RABBIT SALES DROP
Rabbit sales in Melbourne
have dropped by more than half,
and retailers say this is because
of fear of myxomatosis.
Even a drop in prices had
failed to tempt housewives, they
said.
Rabbits were offered at 4d to
6d a lb at the wholesale markets
to-day without attracting many
buyers.
But rabbit exporters are buy¬
ing at what would normally be
regarded as bargain prices.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Pilot Gets
Clear As
Plane Burns
_____
The pilot jumped unhurt
from the blazing cockpit of
a Moth Minor aircraft which
crashed in the front garden
of a house in Beatty Parade,
George's Hall, near Banks¬
town, yesterday.
The pilot was James Wil¬
liam Prince, 21, single, of
"The Bluff" station, Cobar.
A few minutes after
Prince's escape the petrol tank
exploded, and the fire
destroyed the plane.
The plane crashed into the
front garden of Mr. Claude
Ernest Godfrey's home.
Mr. Godfrey, a plumber,
had just left the front of his
house, where he had been
working.
Prince had held his pilots
licence for three months.
He bought the plane three
weeks ago for £600. It was
not insured.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
COLUMN
8
SHADES of Mr. Ded¬
man's Pink Icing.
The Federal Budget in¬
creases the sales-tax on
Christmas stockings from
8 1-3 per cent, to 33 1-3
per cent.
* * *
THE Navy is getting a
bit beyond itself.
Once it was content with
the ocean, but to-day it's
invading the inland.
Six Fireflys, representing
H.M.A.S. Albatross, are to
show the flag at Alice
Springs - and when the
town called Alice has fin¬
ished with them they'll be
more at sea than they've
ever been.
* * *
STUNNED by the Budget,
everybody seems to
have overlooked that on
the day after Sir Arthur
knocked us down, Western
Germany was accorded
Most Favoured Nation
tariff treatment — second
only to British Preferen¬
tial.
What interests me is the
wording — Most Favoured
Nation.
* * *
PROFESSOR N. F. Ast¬
bury has resigned from
the Chair of Applied Phy¬
sics.
I understand that in¬
ability to swallow the re¬
quired amount of red tape
was one of the reasons.
One instance was the
fact that he had to take the
train to attend a science
congress in Queensland be¬
cause it was against regu¬
lations for him to fly.
As a scientist he found
this restriction rather funny.
* * *
WITHIN a month of
completion, a £300,000
contract for building the
migrant centre at Ka¬
pooka, in the south west,
has been halted.
The theory is that the
camp is to be taken over
by the Army.
But you'd have thought
that the authorities would
have made up their minds
before £300,000 was spent,
wouldn't you?
* * *
THIRTY years ago Mr.
H. R. Lacy, Invercar¬
gill (N.Z.) manager for
Dalgety's, said goodbye to
his brother Mr. F. B.
Lacy, who was on his way
to start cattle raising in the
Kimberleys, W.A.
Yesterday, Mr. H. R.
Lacy arrived in Sydney by
TEAL flying-boat.
"Believe it or not," he
said, "but my firm has sent
me on business to the
Kimberleys—which means
that I'll be able to see my
brother after all these
years."
* * *
SOMEONE has bored a
small rat hole in the
door of the Communist
shop in William Street.
Over the hole they've
written, "Enter here."
* * *
AT Moss Vale the offices
of the Department of
Agriculture are in the
School of Arts building.
The other day a visitor,
seeking historical trivia,
poked his head in the door
and said: "I believe there's
an old picture here — a
relic."
"Goodness gracious,"
said the very presentable
secretary, "I'm surely not
THAT old."
Granny

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Magistrate
Bars
Mr. Evatt
-¬
Mr Beavers, S.M.,
yesterday refused to
allow the Chief Secre¬
tary (Mr. Clive Evatt)
to be represented at
Central Court during
the hearing of the Sun¬
day films case.
Mr. Alan Bagot asked
Mr. Beavers for permission
to intervene on behalf of
the Chief Secretary's De¬
partment.
He said the whole question
hinged on the discretion of the
Minister.
Yesterday William Fred¬
erick Farrer Crabtree, private
secretary to Mr. Evatt, pro¬
duced documents which had
been subpoenaed at an earlier
hearing but not produced.
The hearing was adjourned
to enable the Chief Secretary
to produce the documents.
Before the Court were
Kenneth Frank Easton Cook,
of the King's Cross Theatrette,
and Natan Scheinwald of
Spring Street, Sydney.
They are being prosecuted
at the instance of William
Robert Harrop, secretary of
the N.S.W. branch of the
Australian theatrical and
Amusements Association.
The prosecutions were laid
under the Theatres and Public
Halls Act, 1908-46.
Mr. Harrop is also honorary
business manager of the
Motion Picture Exhibitors'
Association.
NEW SUMMONSES
The two men are accused
of contravening Section 19 of
the N.S.W. Theatres and
Public Halls Act by screening
films at the King's Cross
Theatrette on Sunday, August
12, for which an admission
charge was made.
Further summonses have
been laid against Hilda
Blanche Hattam, the Austra
lian Biograph Company Pty
Ltd., and the Premier Theatre
(Surry Hills) Pty. Ltd., for
screening films on Sunday.
August 19.
Asking for leave to appear
for Mr. Evatt, Mr. Bagot said
yesterday that the Minister,
in his discretion, had approved
the Sunday screenings and it
was not a matter for the Court
to grant such approval. It
was up to the Minister, who
could regulate or prohibit
the conduct of any Sunday
entertainment if, in his
opinion, it offended against
good behaviour.
MINISTER'S RIGHT
Mr. C. Begg (for Schein¬
wald) said Mr. Harrop was
asking the Court to sit in
judgment on a Minister of the
Crown.
He said: "The Minister is
not responsible to this Court.
He is responsible to the
Crown and Parliament.
"It is not for the Court to
interfere.
"The Minister is in a most
unusual position under the
Act. He has the sole right
to say whether a theatre or
a public house is in fact a
theatre or a public house. '
The hearing was further
adjourned to November 2.
Mr. J. W. Smythe (by
Bartier and Purcell) for
Harrop; Mr. W. J. Bradley,
K.C., and Mr. H. Maguire,
K.C. (by Murphy and
Moloney), for Cook; Mr. C.
Begg (by Whitehead and
Ferranti) for Scheinwald.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Dismissed
Men In
Protest March
The 150 men dismissed
from the Railway Depart¬
ment's White Bay power¬
house decided at a lunch¬
time meeting yesterday to
march to Parliament House
to-day and ask the Premier,
Mr. J. J. Cahill, to give them
their jobs back.
Sixty of the men, who were
dismissed on Friday, turned up
for work at 7.30 a.m. yester¬
day, staged a stay-in strike at
the works, and demanded that
they be reinstated.
The powerhouse manage¬
ment did not eject them.
At a lunch-time meeting the
men decided to turn up for as
long as necessary to get their
jobs back.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ARTISTS
IN
FORCE
JUST about all of
Sydney's art world
turned up for a private
view of David Strachan's
exhibition of paintings at
the Macquarie Galleries
yesterday, when the
artist dispensed sherry to
guests and the galleries
were solidly packed by
transient crowds between
noon and two o'clock.
In a charcoal grey
flannel suit, plus a pale
grey silk shirt, the artist
- whose tiny, attractive
house in Montparnasse
in Paris where he's been
living for the last six
years is a stop-over to
many Australian visitors
- confounded those who
expected a "French-style
bohemian" and won
top satorial honours of
the day.
Runners-up numbered
Dick Foote, also in im¬
maculate grey; Michael
Kmit in tweeds and a
yellow tie; Desiderius
Orban in a yellow shirt
and Adrian Feint featur¬
ing his bow tie.
Women artists made
fashion news with hair¬
styles — Margaret Olley
(who herself painted in
Paris for a time) had her
hair caught up into the
largest "bun" there;
Dahl Collings wore a
wide gold ring holding
her "pony-tail"; Mar¬
garet Lord had a new
"Medici" fringe; and .
English artist Eileen
Mayo wore black velvet
ribbons tying up her long
blonde hair.
Fresh flowers - tawny
chrysanthemums for
Mrs. Douglas Dundas,
a single red rose for
Elaine Haxton, pinned to
a pink check shirt
complemented the still
life bowls in some of the
pictures.
One of Miss Haxton's
paintings - "Children
with Pigeons" - is pre¬
sumably among the lug¬
gage standing ready to
be shipped out of Mos¬
cow with members of
the Australian Embassy,
as it was bought in Mel¬
bourne by the Third
Secretary, Richard
Woolcott, and has
travelled both to Eng¬
land and Russia with
him.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Earth and Home
"Speak to the Earth," by Elyne
Mitchell. - Angus and Robertson.
THERE is a delightful story
concerning a certain elderly
"viveur" well known in Paris at
the beginning of the present
century, whose proud boast it
was that for the past fifty years
he had never set foot beyond the
boulevardes.
When someone once naively in¬
quired why he did not go to the coun¬
try for a change, his disdain was
almost sublime. "The country?
Bah!" he retorted contemptuously.
"What is it then, the country? Merely
a damp place where raw birds
scream!" '
"ELYNE MITCHELL might admire
the speaker's indubitable mastery
of epithet, but she would be far from
agreeing with his sentiments. So
materialist a heresy as that a bird in
the oven is worth two in the bush
would never win her adherence. More¬
over, she is a born country-lover, the
descendant of generations of squatters,
and the wife of Captain T. Mitchell,
owner of Towong Hill station on the
Upper Murray.
Captain Mitchell - now a free man
once more - became a prisoner of the
Japanese, along with the rest of the
8th Division, when Singapore fell; and
thenceforward to the end of the war
the winning of the battle of the land
at Towong became for his wife a trust
to be fulfilled at all costs. The pre¬
sent volume is a record of that battle.
Readers of Mrs. Mitchell's former
book, "Australia's Alps." will need no
assurance as to her ability to write
well and interestingly of the Austra¬
lian scene.
IF such assurance were needed, her
new book would certainly supply it.
Even so incorrigible a town-dweller as
this reviewer, for whom a life spent
almost exclusively in the society of
cows and "silly sheep" would rank as
an exile comparable only to that of
honest Ovid among the Goths, found
himself murmuring, "Almost thou per¬
suadest me" under the spell of her
quietly rambling prose, so deceptive in
its smooth facility.
Season by season, from autumn to
summer, she tells of her daily life and
labours on the station in a narrative
that, for all its inevitable undercurrent
of anxiety and yearning, is invariably
cheerful and courageous, and, indeed,
rises in many places to heights of
great beauty.
As an authoritative expression of
one of the best and most genuine
aspects of the Australian spirit,
"Speak to the Earth" deserves to take
a high place.
The book is illustrated by a number
of excellent photographs.
- F. S. B.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
EARTHQUAKE NEAR
ALICE SPRINGS.
RECORDED IN SYDNEY.
The main shock of an earthquake
in Central Australia on Friday and
Saturday was the most violent in Aus¬
tralia during the 32 years of the exis¬
tence of the Riverview Observatory.
Scientists believe that the happen¬
ing establishes that the earthquake
area which is known to exist in the
Great Australian Bight extends
through Central Australia.
The Rev. Father D. O'Connell, direc¬
tor of the Observatory, last night
confirmed the centre of the earth¬
quake as about 1,000 miles north-
west of Sydney, which would be near
Finke, a railway station on the Alice
Springs line, from where personal ex¬
periences of the earthquake have been
reported.
A telegram from Finke to the Syd¬
ney Weather Bureau, which has been
sent to the Acting Government As¬
tronomer, Mr. H. Wood, stated that
the earthquake was accompanied by
a very loud rumble, and that all build¬
ings in the town were shaken severely.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE RIVERINA.
OFFICER'S STORY.
The first officer of the Riverina's comple¬
ment to reach Sydney, Mr. S. Oyston, fourth
engineer, yesterday told a graphic story of
the long struggle against a sandstorm which
members of the crew had to make, on their
way from the stranded steamer.
"I was the last to leave the ship." said
Mr, Oyston, "nearly two and a half-hours be¬
hind the main party. Later I joined four
others, and we had gone about half-way along
the beach when we ran into the sandstorm.
The sand seemed to go right through us - it
was in our eyes, in our ears, and in our
mouths, and the heavy packs we were carry¬
ing only made matters worse. We were con¬
tinually stumbling over boots, clothing, ropes,
sticks, and other articles which those ahead
of us had been forced to drop. Also, we
were hungry, and five of us had to make a
meal of three apples and two pears. Fortu¬
nately a man with a packhorse, who had been
collecting baggage, caught up to us about a
mile from Mallacoota Lake, and took some of
our packs, though even then we each had
about 40lb to carry. When we finally reached
the lake, we were practically exhausted, and
were only too thankful to be able to eat some
plain bread which someone in the launch wait¬
ing for us happened to have."

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MR. DAVID MORRICE.
The death occurred recently of Mr. David
Morrice, of Ealing, Sutton Forest, who passed
away unexpectedly in his sleep. Born at
Sutton Forest, he was a son of the late Mr.
John Morrice, M.L.A., and was 83 years of
age.
Mr. Morrice took an active interest in the
public life of the district. He was elected
a councillor in the first Wingecarribee Shire
Council in 1905, and served on the council
till 1912, occupying the presidential chair for
four years of that period. He was also a
member of the Berrima District Agricultural
and Horticultural Society, and the Bong Bong
Picnic Race Club, and served on the P.P.
Board and the Local Land Board. Mr. Mor¬
rice, who followed grazing pursuits, is sur¬
vived by his widow and five sons and five
daughters. The funeral, which took place
at the Church of England Cemetery at Sut¬
ton Forest, was largely attended. The Rev.
Canon Carver, assisted by the Rev. A. C.
Corlette, conducted the services at the church
and at the graveside.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
QUARTER SESSIONS.
(Before Judge Cohen.)
Crown Prosecutor, Mr. L. J. McKean.
APPEAL.
Leo Gerald Gorman appealed against a sen¬
tence of one month's imprisonment with hard
labour pronounced upon him by Mr. W. J.
Camplin, S.M., at the Central Police Court
on October 1, for assaulting a constable. His
Honor varied the sentence to a fine of £5 and costs.
ASSAULT AND ROBBERY.
William Thompson and Joseph New were
charged with having, on October 15, at Sydney,
assaulted Ernest George Slavin and robbed
him of a pocket knife and 11/. The accused
was sentenced to two years' imprisonment with
hard labour in Bathurst Gaol.
FALSE PRETENCES.
John William Dunn pleaded guilty to having
on June 30, at Sydney, falsely pretended to
Charles Patrick McCoo that he was authorised
by Farmer and Company, Limited, to deliver
to him a suit of clothes and to receive the sum
of £7 in payment.
The accused, who was defended by Mr. Brad¬
ley, was bound over in recognisance of self
and one surety in £20 each to come up for
sentence if called upon, and to be of good
behaviour for 12 months.
HOUSEBREAKING.
Albert George Dangerfield pleaded guilty
to having broken and entered the dwelling
house of John O'Grady at Liverpool on Sep¬
tember 29, and stealing £7/13/2. He was re¬
manded for sentence.
MALICIOUS DAMAGE.
Stanley William Folkes, who pleaded guilty
on February 20 to damaging a plate glass
window, the property of William Curtis, and
who failed to pay the amount of £13/13/ as
ordered, was bound over in his own recog¬
nisance in £20, to come up for sentence if
called upon within three months, and to pay
£2 forthwith to the Clerk of the Peace, and
ordered to pay instalments of £2 until the
amount owing is paid.
Mr. W. D. McMahon appeared for the ac¬
cused.
LARCENY.
Gladys James, who was convicted of stealing
the sum of £35, the property of Donald
McIlree, was bound over in recognisance of
self and one surety in £25, to come up for
sentence if called upon, and to be of good
behaviour for 12 months.
Mr. E. R. Abigail appeared on behalf of Mr.
J. W. Abigail for the accused.
ASSAULT.
Louis Clifford Wilkinson, who pleaded guilty
on Thursday to committing an indecent as¬
sault on a male person on board the British
ship, Esperance Bay, on September 13, then on
the high seas, was bound over in recognisance
of self and one surety in the sum of £20
each to come up for sentence if called upon
within two years. Mr. E. R. Abigail appeared
for the accused.
NO. 2 COURT.
(Before Judge Rolin.)
Crown Prosecutor, Mr. E. R. Barton (in¬
structed by Commonwealth Crown Solicitor).
ACQUITTED.
Evidence was continued regarding the charge
against John Everistus O'Regan of conspiracy
and having unlawfully removed taxation
papers.
The accused, in the witness box, denied that
he had ever interfered with files in the Taxa¬
tion Department. In reply to his counsel (Mr.
Windeyer, K.C.) the defendant said he was
one of the first income tax officers to be ap¬
pointed in New South Wales, and had remained
in the department for several years. He had
been an alderman of Randwick, a Justice of
the Peace, and had held other public positions.
Witness stated that he had never been em¬
ployed in the Federal Taxation Department.
He had known Mr. Coveny for several years,
and Detective-Sergeant Stuart Robson for
about three years. Coveny had spoken to him
about income tax matters, and had remarked
that he was in trouble about his income tax
returns, and asked witness to help, which he
agreed to do, and certain papers were handed
to him for the purpose of compiling returns.
Sometime after, Coveny told him that he had
made arrangements with someone else to do
the work.
On returning the papers to Coveny witness
said: "What about the £25 I put on Precious
Dust for you?" Coveny replied that he was
short of cash, and gave witness a post-dated
cheque for £25. At a later date Coveny tele¬
phoned him not to put the cheque through, as
things were not too good.
Mr. Windeyer: Was there any other reason
why that cheque should not have been pre¬
sented that morning? - No.
Did you have anything to do with Mr.
Coveny's Income tax file? - No.
Do you know anything about interference
with files at either the Federal or State Taxa¬
tion Departments? - No.
Witness also denied having left taxation
papers at the bottle department of the Sup¬
reme Court Hotel, stating that he had re¬
turned them personally to Mr. Coveny.
Witness emphatically denied ever receiving
a cheque from Coveny in connection with in¬
come tax matters. The cheque found in the
wallet, he said, was the one given to him by
Mr. Coveny in payment for the bet of £25 on
Precious Dust.
Patrick McGrath, a bookmaker and contrac¬
tor, stated that O'Regan had asked him to put
£25 on Precious Dust for him on June 13
last. Witness had remarked: "It must be a
good thing, Mr. O'Regan, for you to place
£25 on it," and O'Regan had answered that
it was not his, but Coveny's money. On that
occasion Precious Dust ran third in the race.
Mr. Windeyer, K.C., with Mr Sherwood (in¬
structed by Mr. W. Niland and Mr. C. L.
Sheehey)appeared for the defendant.
The Jury brought in n verdict of not guilty,
and the accused was discharged.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MR. D. RYRIE, M.L.A., AT BOMBALA.
[BY TELEGRAPH.]
(FROM OUR OWN CORRESPONDENT.)
BOMBALA, FRIDAY.
Mr. David Ryrie, M.L.A., addressed the electors in the
School of Arts yesterday evening. Mr. John Cruickshank
was voted to the chair, and there was a good attendance.
Mr. Ryrie was listened to throughout his address with
marked attention. He expressed his regret at not being
able to visit Cathcart and Delegate to address the electors at
those portions of the electorate, owing to the opening
of Parliament on Tuesday next, it being the urgent
request of a great number of the selectors in this
and in the Cooma district that he should be
present at the opening of Parliament, to bring their
claims fully before the Government for consideration. He
promised, however, on his return after the present session to
visit those places. He spoke freely on the present Land
Bill, and strongly condemned the action of the local land
board at Cooma,who, in contravention ot the 13th section of
the Land Act, had recommended for forfeiture selections
for which certificates of conformity had already been
issued by the Government to the holders thereof, and
quoted in support of his opinion from the proviso to
clause 3 of the 13th section of the Crown Lands Act. He
also spoke on the railway policy of the Government. The
claims of the district were laid fully before Mr. Ryrie by
several gentlemen present at the meeting, and he promised
to give the same his best attention on his return to Sydney.
A unanimous vote of confidence in Mr. Ryrie was carried
at the conclusion of the meeting. He left here to-day, on
route for Cooma.

POLICE.
CENTRAL POLICE COURT.
Mr. Clarke, S.M., presided in the Charge Court yester¬
day.
George Silvester, a Madrasee, was charged with bigamy.
The prisoner followed the profession of a corn doctor, and,
according to the evidence, it appeared that he was married
in 1875, at St. Mary's Cathedral, Sydney, to one Ellen
O'Brien. He lived with her for a little while, and then
went away for about 12 months. On his return to Sydney
he again lived with his wife, but he afterwards left
Sydney again and was married in Wagga Wagga, in the
month of October last to one Susannah Saville, his former
wife being then alive. The prisoner contended that he
was not legally married to Ellen O'Brien, as he was under
the age of 14 years when the marriage took place,
and he had not received any consent to marry
her. He also stated that he was of opinion
that Ellen O'Brien was dead when he married again. The
prisoner was committed for trial at the next Court of
Quarter Sessions.
Eliza Cleland was sent to gaol for one month, for having
a coat and two hats in her possession, reasonably supposed
to have been stolen.

WATER POLICE COURT.
THURSDAY.
BEFORE the Water Police Magistrate and Messrs. Hill,
Tucker, Spence, and Raphael.
Five drunkards were punished.
Jane Ryan, charged with attempting to commit suicide
by throwing herself into the water at Queen's Wharf, was
found to be of unsound mind, and ordered to be sent to
Tarban Creek.
Thomas Barry was charged with assaulting and attempt¬
ing to rob William Marks. Constable Kilpatrick deposed
that at 1 o'clock that morning he heard cries of police in
Cumberland-street, and upon running to Prince-street saw
prisoner walking away, followed by prosecutor, who was
still crying out. Witness stopped prisoner and prosecutor
then gave him in charge for assaulting and attempting to
rob him. William Marks deposed that he resided at the
corner of Cumberland and Essex streets, and met prisoner
about 12 o'clock last night in York-street, he asked witness
where he was going, and he said home. After some con¬
versation they went back as far as the corner of Margaret
and Clarence streets, and prisoner asked him to come
and have a drink. They went into a public-house
and witness paid for the drinks with half-a
sovereign and received the change. Prisoner wanted to
carry his parcel, but witness refused to let him, and he then
caught hold of the parcel and put his other hand into pro¬
secutor's waistcoat pocket. He wanted witness to give him
the change of the half-sovereign, but upon prosecutor
refusing, prisoner struck him in the face. Witness imme¬
diately screamed and caught hold of prisoner's coat, and
tore a piece off. The constable shortly afterwards came up,
and he gave prisoner in charge. Prisoner, after receiving
the usual caution, said that he had two or three drinks
with prosecutor, and afterwards came out, and prosecutor
then wanted him to stand treat, or " flat," as he called it,
but he (prisoner) refused, and asked prosecutor where his
home was, and upon being told took him up to the door of
his house, where prosecutor said, " Now we will go and
have these drinks, and "mauled hold" of prisoner and
tore his coat. The man was foolishly drunk and tearing
away at prisoner's coat. The Bench committed the accused
to take his trial at the ensuing Quarter Sessions to be held
in April.

where ho was going, and ho said home. After somo con-
versation they went back as far ag the corner of Margaret
and : Clarence streets, and prisonor asked him to come
and have ' a drink. They went into a public-house
and - . witness paid for. the drinks '. with half-a
sovereign and received the change. Prisoner wanted to
curry .his parcel, but witness refused to let him, and he thea

secutor's waistcoat pocket. Ho wanted witness tb give nim
tho .chango of tho half-sovereign, but upon .proseoutor
refusing, prisoner struck him in tho face. Witness imme-
diately screamed and caught hold of prisoner's coat, and
tore,a pioce off. The constable shortly afterwards came up,
and ho gave prisoner in charge. Prisoner, after receiving
tho usual caution, said that ho had two or. three drinks
with prosecutor, and; afterwards came out, and prosecutor

his hbuso, whero prosecutor said, " Now wo will go and

away at prisoner's coat. Tho Bench committed the acensad
to take his trial at tho ensuing Quarter Sessions to bo hold
in April.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
ENGLAND DEFERS
CHOICE OF
TEAM FOR TEST
By TOM GOODMAN
Final selection of England's team for
the first Rugby League Test on Monday
will not be considered before Sunday, the
team manager, Mr. Walter Popplewell,
said last night.
It is doubtful if Harry Murphy and Bob Nichol¬
son, two of England's youngest forwards, will be able
to play again on the tour.
At least four of England's Test
forwards will have to play against
Newcastle at Newcastle to-morrow.
Ted Ward, a centre, will play in
the pack to allow star lock-forward,
Ike Owens, to have a well-earned rest.
Fit players who will not appear at
Newcastle are Gus Risman, Albert
Johnson, Eric Batten, Ernest Ward,
Willie Horne, and Tom McCue
(backs), Ike Owens, and probably Les
White (forwards).
It is a pity that Joe Jones's knee
trouble will prevent him from play¬
ing at full-back.
Otherwise, either Martin Ryan or
Jack Kitching would have been rested
A more thorough deduction as to
Test team possibilities could then have
been made.
Jones is the only back on the in¬
jured list.
Jenkins Back
Dai Jenkins, Leeds half-back, will
have his first game since he injured
a knee at Canberra.
He has been training during the
past few days.
Jenkins, however, is below best
condition.
It does not seem likely that he can
force his way into the Test Team.
It would not be a great surprise if
Martin Ryan were to be played in the
Test at half-back, as in the match
against New South Wales last Satur¬
day, with Gus Risman full-back and
Jack Kitching partnering Ernest Ward
in the centre.
But the chances are that experienced
rugged, Tom McCue, despite that he
was below his best at Wollongong and
Orange, will retain his place as Eng¬
land's first half-back.
Barring accidents, the teams in the
Test on Monday may line up as fol¬
lows:
ENGLAND. AUSTRALIA.
Full-back.
M Ryan. D. Parkinson.
Three-quarters.
E. Batten. L. Cooper.
E. Ward, R. Bailey.
A. J. Risman (c). I. Jorgenson (c).
A Johnson. E. Newham.
Five-eighth.
W. Horne. P. Devery.
Half-back.
T. McCue. J Grice.
Forwards.
L Owens. N. Mulligan.
L. White. A. Clues
D. Phillips. R. Kay.
K. Gee. F. Farrell.
J. Egan. G. Watt.
F. Whitcombe. H. Westaway.
Only Seven Fit
Of England's 11 forwards, only
seven were fit to be considered for
the Newcastle match.
The Englishmen hoped that Fred
Hughes would be available to take a
place in the front row.
Hughes found at training yesterday
that his ankle was still troublesome.
Trevor Foster's knee injury robs
him of any chance of qualifying for a
place in the Test pack.
Harry Murphy, with a displaced
collarbone, still has his left arm at
the horizontal, supported by a splint.
Bob Nicholson, with a recurrence
of pleurisy, is in bed again, and it is
unlikely that he, will play again in
Australia.
Mr. Popplewell announced the fol¬
lowing team to play against New¬
castle:
Full-back: M. Ryan.
Three-quarters: J. Lewthwaite. J.
Kitching, B. Knowelden, A. Bassett.
Five-eighth: W. Davies.
Half-back: D. Jenkins.
Forwards: K. Gee, J. Egan. F.
Whitcombe, G. Curran, D. Phillips,
Ted Ward, L. White (one to be
omitted).
It is a pity the New South Wales
selection committee was not represen¬
ted at the Orange match.
This will probably rob Bathurst
full-back, O. Kennerson, of any
chance of being chosen in the New
South Wales team to play Queensland
in Brisbane.
His tackling and long kicking were
outstanding at Orange.
BATHURST, Thursday. - Oriel
Kennerson, Bathurst full-back, has
had two offers to transfer to Sydney
clubs, but has not made any decision.
One offer was from Eastern Suburbs.

WATER POLICE COURT.
BEFORE the Water Police Magistrate, with Messrs. Road-ing and E. Smith.
Several drunkards were brought before the Court and
fined.
Samuel Jobson, for being drunk and disorderly in the
public streets, was ordered to pay a fine of 20s., with the
alternative of seven days imprisonment
Joseph Brotherton, a deserter from the ship British
Consul was sent to gaol for four weeks.
SUMMONS SHEET. - The summons sheet contained nine
cases which were disposed of as follows:- Annie Row¬
land, for using insulting language to Ellen Hallinan, was
found to be guilty, and was ordered to pay a fine of 10s.
to deliver up to James Russell certain premises illegally
detained by him. George Petty, summoned by Isaac
Marshall for committing wilful and corrupt perjury, was
committed for trial. Bail allowed himself in £40, and
two sureties in £20 each. The other cases were either
LICENSING BUSINESS.- The license of the Criterion
Hotel, George-street, was transferred from Charles Ware
to George Murray. Billiard licenses were granted to
Joband Gorman and Charles Armit.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
A CONGRESSMAN-AT-LARGE is to be
elected in Illinois this fall to fill the
vacancy occasioned by the elevation of
Mr. Logan to the United States senate.
The democrats should soon take up the
subject and determine who shall lead
them in the contest for that important
position. To say that the democratic
party is in the minority and that effort
to elect a democrat as Logan's successor
would be futile, is to admit that the dem¬
ocrats of Illinois are cowards or have
no hope of success. If they are cow¬
ards they should surrender at discre¬
tion; if they have no hope of success
they should either throw down their
arms in despair or resolve to die fighting
in the last ditch. But the demo¬
crats are not cowards and they are not
hopeless. They have braved the force
of radical power too often to fear it, and
there has not been for many years a
time when their prospect for success
were as bright as they now no. What
is wanted among the democrats of Illi¬
nois is organization - organization that
will instruct the party in its duty and
bring its electors to the polls. With
such organizations we can defeat the
radicals and redeem the state ; without
it our efforts are love's labor lost. To
effect Such organization, we must, in the
first place, have a platform that is sound
one that will meet the issues of the
day and rally in its defense and support
men who are enthusiastic and deter¬
mined to succeed in its vindication be¬
fore the people. In the next place, we
must have a candidate that appreciates
the situation - a live, earnest, fearless
man, who can and will carry the war
into Africa. And in the next place, we
must have a state central committee that
understands its business, with a chair¬
man not taken from among those old
politicians who lag superfluous on the
stage. If, too, the headquarters of the
committee could be located elsewhere
than at Springfield, the party would be
greatly benefitted. There are other
necessary policies that should be inaug¬
urated, which will be suggested to any
person who has observed the bungling
manner in which the affairs of the dem¬
ocratic party have been managed of
late years in this state, but the chiefest
necessity of the party is the clearing
away of the dead-beat leaders who have
no interest in the way that rises above
their own personal ambitions - the po¬
litic men who look wise and are so non-
committal. These men ought to be
made to take a back seat, and the bold,
earnest, plucky, fighting men - men of
the Vallandigham stamp - permitted to
go to the front.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
FROM SPRINGFIELD.
THE HOBBLING LEGISLATURE
JOGGING ALONG.
NO QUOROM IN THE SENATE - BUSINESS
NEARLY COMPLETED - BILLS THROUGH
AND SQUEEZING THROUGH - "SCULLDUG¬
GERY." SPRINGFIELD, June 13, 1871.
To the Editor of the Bulletin:
This is Saturday, and, a usual, the sen¬
ate has no quorum. The present house of
representatives is so much larger than the
senate that the latter body is able to keep
up with the former without work¬
ing on Saturday, and so, without adjourn¬
ing the members quietly slip home for
Sunday and return some time during
Monday.
The work for which the extra session
was called is nearly accomplished. All
the subjects embraced in the proclamation
have been considered, and where it was
thought necessary bills have been intro¬
duced and have either passed both houses
or are well along on the regular road.
THE PARK BILLS
are all through; the state house bill and
that for the pay of members are through
and signed by the governor ; the bills for
the investigating of the affairs of the pen¬
itentiary and of the soldiers' orphans'
home, for the appropriation of an addi¬
tional $25,000 for the insane asylum at
Jacksonville and for the payment of
school-fund and school-fund interest or¬
ders, hav epassed both houses.
AN EMINENT DOMAIN BILL
has passed the senate, but is being fought
in the house. The opponents of the senate
bill hold that no benefits to property in es¬
timating the value of property taken or
damaged for public use, while the senate
bill provides that such benefits may be
taken into the account so far as they are
special and are not shared in common
with the property not taken.
THE GENERAL APPROPRIATION BILL
providing for the current expenses of the
state government has passed the house but
is meeting with opposition in the senate,
especially among the democrats. The
democrats in the house mostly opposed
the bill on account of some extravagant
appropriation for the office expenses
of the various staff officers but they were
voted down at all points. Yesterday, in
the senate, a number of amendments were
offered, but every one of them was voted
down, and the bill was ordered to a third
reading. When it is put upon its passage,
however, the republicans will find that
they will have to submit to some amend¬
ments or they cannot command votes
enough to carry it through.
IT IS NOW CONCEDED
that nothing will be done with the sal¬
aries of circuit judges, the bill fixing them
at $3000 having been lost in the senate by
a vote of 25 to 10, it requiring 26 to make
a constitutional majority.
THE ROAD BILL
has been laid aside as too long a job to
take up during the hot weather. Some of
the judges and justicse in the state have
decided that juries may be summoned in
accordance with the requirements of the
new constitution without any new pro¬
visions of law, and that land may be con¬
demned for highways or other public uses
as heretofore, and a jury, summoned as in
other civil cases, at the request of either
party, may assess the damages. If this is
a correct view of the case it is not so
very important that a road law or even an
animal domain bill be passed this session.
The appropriation of $60,000 for the In¬
sane Asylum, at Anna, which passed both
houses at the last session, failed to become
a law. It passed the house on the last day
of the session, and was not reported en¬
rolled before both houses were adjourned.
It did not, therefore, reach the governor
in the regular order of business. Since
this session convened, the bill has
been signed by the speakers of both houses
and presented to the governor, but his
present impression is that it does
not come by proper legislative author¬
ity, and that he cannot sign it. There
are some whispering of scullduggery con¬
cerning it, but I am inclined to think that
it has happened as the natural result of the
pernicious habit of setting the time of ad¬
journment a week or two in advance,
when it is impossible to tell in what con¬
dition the business will be in when that
time arrives.
The house has asked to adjourn finally
on Wednesday next, but the senate has
not yet concurred. ELI NOYES.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE GREAT DEAD MAN.
A BRIEF SKETCH OF HIS LIFE.
He came of a Huguenot family, and was
born in New Lisbon, Columbiana county,
Ohio, in 1822. He received a good educa¬
tion; spent one year in Jefferson College,
in Ohio; spent two years as principal of
an academy at Snow Hill, Maryland; re¬
turned to Ohio in 1840; studied law, and
was admitted to the bar in 1842; was
elected to the state of legislature in 1845 and
1846; was editor of the Dayton Empire
from 1847 to 1849; for some years subse¬
quent to that date he devoted himself
wholly to his profession and polities; was
a member of the "national democratic
convention" held at Cincinnati in 1856;
ran for the thirty-fifth congress against
L. D. Campbell, whose seat he successfully
contested; and he was re-elected to the
thirty-sixth congress. At the commence¬
ment of the second session of the thirty-
fifth congress, and during the thirty-sixth,
he was placed on the committee on terri¬
tories. Re-elected to the thirty-seventh
congress. In 1863 he was arrested by mil¬
itary authority for expressing his opinions
against the war, was banished to the south¬
ern states, and by way of Bermuda went
to Canada. During his exile he was nom¬
inated for governor of Ohio, and defeated.
He subsequently returned, and was a del¬
egate to the "Chicago convention" of 1864.
Since then, with the exception of the part
he took in the New-York convention, and
the subsequent canvass, he has devoted
himself assiduously and quietly to the
practice of the law, until the recent Ohio
democratic convention, where he success¬
fully advocated an advanced position and
a froward policy for the democratic party.

THE Missouri Democrat has discov¬
ered that Mr. Trumbull has several rel¬
atives holding offices under the govern¬
ment and that paper accordingly de¬
clares against nepotism. The consist¬
ency of some radical papers is beautiful
to contemplate.
THE Chicago Post, like the boy that
whistled to keep his courage up, calls
loudly for an investigation of the frauds
charged against the city council. The
Post is charged with being as deep in
the mud as the aldermen are in the
mire.
BEN. BUTLER has introduced a bill
in congress of the nature of a declara¬
tory act that all females of age have a
right to vote under the fourteenth and
fifteenth amendments. This is the ob¬
ject Mrs. Woodhull has had in view
and it is probable she and Butler are
working in concert, and are to share
the honors if sucessful, which is im¬
probable, the bill having been referred
to the judiciary committee, which is
known to be opposed to it.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MRS. LIVERMORE says it is a pity
that Horace Greeley was not on hand
when the world was created. The Chi¬
cago Republican says "it is a much
greater pity that Mrs. Livermore
wasn't on hand herself, for if she had
been we wouldn't have her now."
Such remarks as these are a little se¬
vere on Mrs. Livermore, but no doubt
they are a satisfaction to that lady
since they are a sign that she has
acquired the right to be abused like
a man.
AN exchange, remarking on Governor
Palmer's position on the military occu¬
pation of Chicago, thinks "it is a little
funny that the state which was the
first to decry the doctrine of states'
rights, bids fair to be the first to re¬
assert the doctrine in a substantial
manner." It is not so funny after all,
since it is fast being demonstrated that
the seed of disregard of states' rights
fell in ground where it has taken deep
root, and like a bad weed, promises now
to overtop the doctrine of states'
rights.

ner) means, when he speaks of Senators

posed to the most searching and thorough

WOMEN are very scarce in Wyoming
Territory, there being in some sections
five men to one women. Why don't the
Woodhulls, Livermores, Anthonys and
the rest of the dissatisfied brood emigrate
to Wyoming?
"THE national convention of colored
people, shortly to assemble in this city,"
says the New Orleans 'Times,' "is attract¬
ing, it is said, unusual attention from the
leading politicians of the radical party.
Mr. Sumner will have some friends in
attendance to watch its proceedings, and
the Grantites think it is highly important
that it should endorse the administration
of the great gift-receiver."
SOMETIME since Gen. Howard, of
freedman's bureau notoriety was sent to
the frontier to find out "the precise temper
of the Apache toward the government."
Meanwhile by order of the government,
the soldiers are idle in the defense of the
white settlers and the Indians are plun¬
dering and murdering in all directions.
The temper of the Indians of Arizona to¬
ward the people of that section, is ap¬
parently, desire to exterminate them as
speedily as possibly, and the national au¬
thorities are affording every facility for
the carrying out by the Indians, of their
murderous intentions.
THE NEW YORK 'World,' which some
time ago, was classed among the strongest
advocates of the "passive policy," reads
the liberal republicans a lesson for their
neglect to take advantage of the oppor¬
tunity offered by the Connecticut election
to make a good fight and show their real
strength in that state, which, in the result
of a victory, could not have failed to have
had a telling effect on the political com¬
plexion of affairs all over the country. The
'World' says: "The democratic party will
waste no more breath on the liberal repub¬
lican leaders, but will hereafter appeal
directly to the people.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
SAMUEL FINLEY BRESE MORSE died
in the city of New York at seventeen
minutes before eight o'clock on the even¬
ing of the second instant. Prof. Morse
was born at Charleston, Massachusetts,
April 27, 1791, and was at the time of his
death, in his eighty-fourth year. He
graduated at Yale college in 1810, after
which he went to England where he stud¬
ied painting with Washington Allston
and Benjamin West. In 1813, he received
the gold medal of the Adelphi Society of
Art for his first effort in sculpture, "the
Dying Hercules. He returned to New
York in 1815 and was elected first president
of the National Academy of Design, and
was appointed professor of the art of design
in the university of the city of New York.
He did not give his entire attention to art,
but was interested in chemistry and especi¬
ally in electrical and galvanic experi¬
ments; and on a voyage from Havre to
New York in 1832, he conceived the idea
of the magnetic telegraph, the perfection
of which made him celebrated while living
and will carry his name down to after gen¬
erations. Prof. Morse had scanty means
with which to develope his idea, and was al¬
most in despair when congress, in 1843,
at midnight, and in the last moments of
the session appropriated thirty thousand
dollars for the erection of an experimental
line between Washington and Baltimore.
This was the beginning of that system of
telegraphy which is now in use all over
our country and Europe, and in parts of
Asia and Africa. Success showered upon
Prof. Morse wealth, honor, and world¬
wide distinction. He was at one time
the recipient of four hundred thousand
francs, the joint gift of several European
states; and a statue of him was erected in
New York city a year before his death.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
A BRILLIANT AFFAIR
The Masonic Ball at Independence
Hall a Grand Success.
Never before has Independence
Hall appeared in such brilliant array
as it did last evening when the mem¬
bers of Hawaiian Lodge No. 21, F.
& A. M., entertained their friends
and brethren at a most elegant ball.
The decorations were unique, and
the display of electric light made
the hall look like a dream of fairy
land. The floral decorations were
superb, making the hall resemble a
tropical bower. The banquet hall
was in charge of Honolulu's leading
caterer, Mr. T. Krouse, and the
beautiful decorations as well as the
select refreshments enhanced the
charm of the evening. Many of the
decorations were emblematic of
masonry and the taste with which
they were prepared drew the ad¬
miration of all present. The Quin¬
tette Club furnished the music for
the dancers, and it was past 2 o'clock
before the last guests departed. A
special feature of this interesting
affair was the total absence of any¬
thing stronger than lemonade.
Among those present were Mr. and
Mrs. Dole, British Commissioner
Hawes, Acting French Consul Gif¬
fard and Mrs. Giffard, Mrs Neumann

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
MAUI NOTES.
The patrons of the INDEPENDENT
are sorry to hear that it has gone
out of existence, but will do all they
can to keep the new issue.
The last day of the court session
was spent in hearing the argument
for a new trial in the Young Hee
bribery case. The motion was
denied and will now be brought
before etc., supreme court.
Mr. G. E. Simpson and wife gave
a farewell dance at their Paia resi¬
dence at which was present a large
number of friends. A very enjoy¬
able time was had by all.
Mr. Henry Long has been admit¬
ted to practice before the lower
courts. He passed a very creditable
examination before Deputy Attor¬
ney-General Brown and attorneys
John Richardson and Kaulukou.
The gentleman acting as post¬
master in Mr. Wilder's absence is a
very obliging person. Last Wednes¬
day people had to wait for over an
hour for their mail while the gentle¬
man would talk with any person of
influence that would happen along
and would not allow the clerks to
handle the mail.
The Executive Committee of the
Maui Racing Association has
changed the programme for the
Fourth. Full particulars by next
mail.
It is to be hoped that the Legisla¬
ture will appropriate money to im¬
prove the highways between Kula
and Kahului.
The S. S. Kahului is to come here
again. She will touch at Mahukona
first and arrive here about the
Fourth.
Spreckelsville Plantation finished
grinding yesterday.
Dr. Eiger who has been the
chemist at Spreckelsville for the
past year severed his connection
with that Plantation today and will
depart for his former fields of labor
the cane fields of Louisiana.
La Grippe is catching on. A good
many people are laid up.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
The Ladies of the Cannibal Isles.
BY JOHN C. BOWKER, M. D.
As a society study, using the term
in its American acceptance, the Ha¬
waiian woman is not to be consider¬
ed. But, viewed from another van¬
tage-ground, she possesses certain
marked and unique characteristics.
Primarily, she is natural and a
stranger to most of the arts and de¬
vices so frequent among her Cau¬
casian Sisters.
She is far from beautiful, judged
by our standards, yet in her youth
she is not repulsive. Her skin is
chocolate-colored, eyes a sable black
and teeth as brilliant and white as
those found elsewhere in advertise¬
ments of popular dentifrices and
tooth-washes.
Her features are tolerably regular
and her hair a glossy black.
Dame fashion is practically an
unknown quantity to her as she
habitually wears a Mother Hubbard
dress and sailor hat of straw perch¬
ed jauntily on one side of her head.
Around her neck she wears the
lai or national wreath of fresh
flowers made frequently of frangi¬
panni blossoms, in which luxury she
can indulge at small cost.
Of intellectual pursuits she has
few or none and loves to pass her
time in gadding about and gossiping
of her neighbors' affairs. She is in¬
variably a good horsewoman, or
shall we say horseman, since she
rides astride the animal. She has
a sort of bifurcated skirt, with long,
flowing ends of gay-colored calico,
which are useful in covering the
stirrup and preventing bushes and
strong ferns catching the feet when
riding rapidly through the brush.
Men's stirrups are provided with a
long, sweeping cover of leather for
the same purpose. Both men and
women ride their horses at the top
of their speed.
The Hawaiian woman is a good
swimmer and formerly spent much
of her time in the water, the tem¬
feature of which is most delightful
and alluring to those fond of Nep¬
tune's domains. When other re¬
sources fail she hies away to some
purling rill, and where its cascade
tumbles into the sea she seats her¬
self for a cooling bath.
The hula-hula dance and other
sports in which she once engaged
are now forbidden by the laws of this
republic, where men are supposed
to be free to do as they like.
As the woman grows older - for
one remarks the absence of very old
people in the islands - she increases
in adiposity until she reaches a
tremendous size, which is increased
in appearance by the loose Hubbard
gowns with long trains. And when
a woman of size comes sweeping and
puffing into church small people
must flee the aisle or meet disaster
before this elephantine craft, which
seems to float around in a half-im¬
becile fashion, as though devoid of
steering gear.
It would be ungallant to call the
Hawaiian womsn lazy; yet, when
she sits on the floor to cook her
meal and prepare her provender,
and even to do what little ironing
of clothes she accomplishes, she cer¬
tainly cannot be called sprightly or
active. Her tongue, however, never
fails, and rattles on endlessly on the
most trivial and inane subjects.
If the future of a race depends on
its women, the doom of the Sand¬
wich Islands is rung already, for the
present woman is strongly averse to
caring for a family.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Three hundred letters by Andrea
Doria, High Admiral of Emperor
Charles V., have been discovered in
the Este archives at Modena by Sig.
Carboni, and will be published soon.
They form part of a collection of
100,000 letters bequeathed by the
Marchese Campori to the town of
Modena, and placed in the Este
library.

The Velocity in Port.
Our old friend Capt. Martin has
again brought the Velocity safely
into port. She brings no Chinese
passengers but a full consignment
of freight for Wing Wo Tai & Co.
She made the passage in 54 days,
and will be berthed at the Pacific
Mail wharf, for a change, as soon as
the Lillie Starbuck sails this after¬
noon. The vessel is undergoing
fumigation.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Labby's Jubilee Lay.
(From the London Truth)
On this great day of fetes and feasts
All men rejoice, then why not beasts?
Why should not they their feelings
show,
Fired by a patriotic glow,
And gladly half the glorious day
With roar, with yelp, with scream,
with bray ?
And prove how animals can cheer
The advent of this famous year.
The noble horse, beyond a doubt,
With zeal will draw his loads about;
The bull will bellow at his best,
While donkeys all will bray with
zest;
Terrific roarings from the Zoo,
And shrieks from many a cockatoo,
Shall show the royalty's the same
In savage beasts as well as tame.
Nor shall the strains of rapture cease
From flocks of foxes and of geese;
Insects shall hum from flowers and
trees,
Homage shall come from wasps
and bees,
The fish shall flap their tails about,
And, if 'tis possible, get out;
While eager just to have a glimpse,
Up will pop heads of loyal shrimps
Beasts, birds and fishes we shall see
United for the Jubilee;
Horn, hide and shell, hoof, fur and
feather,
For once with man shall join to¬
gether;
Union of hearts at length appears,
Because the Queen's reigned sixty
years!

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
Great Britain of the East.
The Japanese may in many senses
be compared to the people of this
country (Great Britain). To begin
with they number 40,000,000; then
they live in an island country of
about the same area as Great Bri¬
tain and they are becoming depend¬
ent upon foreign countries for a
large proportion of their food. With
the lapse of each year, and especially
since the war with China, this de¬
pendence on outside sources is in¬
creasing. The Japanese authorities
have been much concerned to know
why their people are smaller than
the European races. They believe
they have discovered the reason.
They have concluded that it is ow¬
ing to their dieting almost exclusive¬
ly on rice and fish. Consequently
the Japanese are now becoming
meat eaters, and the movement in
this direction is a natural one - al¬
most a patriotic one. The heads of
families make a point of giving their
children meat once a day, if they
can afford to do so, and when they
are drilling or working hard Japa¬
nese men have meat twice a day.
The older generation do not take
to meat very rapidly. The taste for
it has to be acquired; but when the
Japanese does acquire the taste for
European food he cannot do with¬
out it. - London People.
Briton's Iron Industry.
The output of pig iron in Great
Britain in 1896 was 8,750,000 tons,
which beats the record, the nearest
approach to it having been 8,495,800
tons in 1883 and 8,325,000 tons in
1889. It is evident, however, that
the old continuous progress of Bri¬
tish metallurgy has been checked.
British ironmasters have, in fact,
now to struggle for business to a
far greater extent than hitherto.
The vast amount of British capital
invested in foreign and colonial
railway and other enterprises, and
the unrivalled transport facilities
afforded by the splendid British
mercantile marine have all helped
to maintain British supremacy not
only in iron-making but also in
other branches. Still, British iron¬
masters cannot afford to underrate
the formidable character of the Am¬
erican and German competition
which they have now to confront
from day to day. - The People.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
THE FAREWELL REWARD.
The Formal Endorsement of Pastor
Birnie's Labors Here.
The Ecclesiastical Council of
Central Union Church have adopted
the following resolutions in re¬
ference to the resignation of Rev
Douglas P. Birnie, their Pastor:
Resolved: That while endorsing
the action of the church and Pastor
we desire say for ourselves that it
is with sincere regret that we part
with one whom we have learned to
esteem very highly. Rev. D. P.
Birnie's pastorate in Honolulu,
though brief has been fruitful.
Through the leadership by the bles¬
sing of God, large additions have
been made to the Church roll, while
as pastor and preacher he has en¬
deared himself to the hearts of this
people. He has proved himself well
adapted to work for the young, espe¬
cially young men. In organizing and
carrying out plans for the moral and
intellectual improvement of the
youth of this city, we believe he has
had no superior. We feel assured
that this historic field of religious
effort has been greatly blessed by
his ministry, and that the best
wishes and earnest prayers of God's
people will follow him into other
parts of the Master's vineyard,
where he may be called to labor.
We affectionately commend him to
the churches, as a man of lofty
Christain character, a wise pastor,
and an effective preacher of the
vital truths of the Gospel. We con¬
gratulate the members of this
church on the service which has
been rendered them, and pray that
upon them the divine blessing may
still rest; that the Great Head of
the Church may send them another
leader of like wisdom and devotion."

The first quarter of the century saw his

FATHER WYMAN AT
ST. MARY'S CHURCH
The Paulist Fathers at St. Mary's
Church made a special feature of their
usual vesper service last night. At the
close of the benediction the German
choral "Te Deum" was sung by the choir,
composed of sopranos, Miss Paula, Miss
Higgins and Miss Johnson; contraltos.
Miss Josie Murphy and Miss Nettie John¬
son; tenors, W. Moore and Dr. J. F.
Smith; basso, W. G. O'Brien: organist.
Miss Giorgiani. The sermon was preached
by the Rev. Father Wyman. He said in
part:
"This is a special year in the Catholic
church. The holy Father in Rome has
ordered that the opening of the new cen¬
tury shall be recognized by a midnight
mass or the day is to be marked with
some special form of praise. The ser¬
vices to-night will be followed by the
singing of the "Te Deum" as a sign of
our thankfulness to God for his kindly
guardianship over us in the past. God
has given great blessings to the Christian
people in the past and the great blessings
that we may hope God will send will be
ours if we are only faithful.
"God works, as we know, in hidden yet
most effective ways, for the benefit of his
people. The benefits of God are known
and most deeply appreciated by those who
think serously and most desire those
things which are of lasting value. The
superficial mind does not always see what
is most real and substantial and as a rule
has little appreciation of what it receives.
On the other hand, those who are guid¬
ed by divine wisdom find in the acts of
providence infinitely more blessings than
it is possible to suppose God would give
to such weak creatures as we are."
IMPRESSIVE MUSIC
AT ST. IGNATIUS
Services in St. Ignatius Church last
night were solemn and beautiful. The
sacred edifice was brilliantly lighted and
was crowded with worshipers who offered
up thanks for blessings received during
the past year, and prayed for a con¬
tinuance of them during the next
twelve months. Rev. Father Prelato,
S. J., recited the Rosary and Litany
and then vespers, the responses being
made by Rev. Angelo Cotelli, S. J. The
choir augmented for the occasion by the
ladies' sodality choir rendered sweet
music during the services. Rev. Father
Frieden, S. J., delivered the sermon. It
was an eloquent effort and touched the
auditors. In part he said:
"We have assembled before God's altar
to-night to thank the Almighty for the
good things that have come to us in the
course of the year which is now drawing
to its close. Yes, the year is well nigh
passed-out a few hours to remain. It is
for the last time in the year '99 that the
preacher speaks to you from this pulpit;
the last time you adore our Lord in the
Blessed Sacrament and receive his bene¬
diction. And what has become of the
year? The days which it was made of
are no more — they have passed to eter¬
nity. And with the passing time, we our¬
selves have passed; and at the close of
the year we are one year nearer to eter¬
nity.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
GOV. TAYLOR
ADJOURNS THE
LEGISLATURE
FRANKFORT, Ky., Jan. 31. - Gov¬
ernor Taylor at 10 o'clock this morn¬
ing issued the following proclama¬
tion:
To the General Assembly of the Com¬
monwealth of Kentucky:
Whereas, a state of insurrection now
prevails in the State of Kentucky, and
especially in Frankfort, the capital
thereof, by virtue of the authority vested
in me by the constitution of Kentucky,
I do hereby, by this proclamation, ad¬
journ at once the General Assembly of
the State of Kentucky, to meet at Lon¬
don, Laurel County, Kentucky. Tuesday,
the sixth day of February, 1900, at 12
o'clock.
Given under my hand at Frankfort,
Ky., this 30th day of January, 1900, at 9
o'clock p. m. W. S. TAYLOR,
Governor of Kentucky.
By Caleb Powers, Secretary of State.

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
STRANGE STORY OF
IS GOING INSANE.
The world went mad one day. It
had been a sanely sunny world
back in the old country. There was
good cheer in the way the thistles
sprouted when the year grew green
and there was a laugh in the voice
of the bustling little river beyond, and
healthy Irish ruddiness bloomed in all
the faces that Maggie Fogarty, now Mrs.
Dunton, looked upon. These faces always
smiled at her and she smiled back, or
else it was the other way around. She
gave a good-day to the people she met,
and she was not afraid. That was be¬
fore the hand came in the dark.
"It's a fair pink that shines in your
cheeks, Margaret mine." said John Wood¬
lock. "There's roses on many a bush
would hide their heads at the sight of
yours for the shame they were put to."
Then he married her.
They came to liveiIn California in 1874,
and found an eager, hurrying young city
perched on a ragged point of the land's
edge, and going at the pace set by its
high-strung climate. John and Margaret
Woodlock were young, and they took hold
of hands and fell in with the merry hus¬
tle, for there was the tang of wine in the
air, and it was good to be alive. Here in
San Francisco, too, she could look at the
world eye to eye and fear nothing. There
was no hand then to fear.
The first time she felt the touch of the
hand was after Josephine went away - but
wait.
"John Woodlock was a good man in
those days," she says, "and I loved him.
He loved me, too. 'Maggie, it seems that
I can't tell the sun twinkles from your
hair when I look at you around the cor¬
ner of my eye,' John Woodlock said. That
was thirty years ago he said it. Thirty
years ago - he said my hair was not to be
told from the sun - he said - he was my
husband, you remember - you remember,
don't you?"
The words came slowly in the timid,
wondering tone of a little child who
whimpers in the dark. The old woman's
hand crept to her forehead and seemed
trying to brush away something. Her
eyes, the same blue eyes that John Wood¬
lock, the lover, had likened in the poetry
of his Irish tongue to the nemophilia blos¬
soms of California hillsides - these same
eyes grew fixed and fearing.
"Whenever I talk about old times and
think - and think - my head — the trouble -"
Then the words went back and said
themselves again, and I broke in briskly
with a town topic of the now, and she
met me on safe ground and the face grew
sure once more.
For the morning sun was deluging her
room, and there were those who meant
well, like the sunshine, and had come to
chat through a friendly hour with her,
and the day side of nature was upper¬
most. I believe it is Robert Owen who
writes of the night side.
Mrs. Margaret Dunton has seen the
night side. She has been afraid of some¬
thing that she could not see, something
that she could not hear, something that
she could not touch. It comes when the
sun has slipped away. Then the sudden
dark strides in, gulping houses and trees
and people as it comes. Then a lonely old
woman, who looks for no more from life,
sits in her room in an upper flat and
reads over letters which their writers
have forgotten, and caresses the painted
ribbon nonsenses that Josephine treas¬
ured, and then -¬
All at once she is on the edge of a
cruel precipice, and below it are the
crashing and whirling of madness. She
cannot see clearly what lies below, but
she feels that it is terrible - it is destroy¬
ing. And while she stands on the edge,
peering down, she feels the hand.
It comes behind her and pushes, pushes
- she struggles and pits her strength
against it and it gives way. She turns, to
see no one there. The precipice is gone
and she is at home once more.
But every time the hand comes it
pushes harder and after every struggle
she is weaker.
"Some day - the time will come when -"
she says.
After a few years of married life in
California the poetry died out of the Irish
love story. Just why I do not know. Mar¬
garet was the same as ever - good to look
upon. Her cheeks would still have
shamed roses and her hair still played at
being sunshine, and her eyes were still
like the flowers of the nemophilia. The

Handy Hints for Housewives.
Vinegar will clean the mica in the
stove doors.
Tepid milk and water will clean oilcloth
without soap.
Save your cold tea; it is excellent for
cleaning grained wood.
If you scorch clothes in ironing, place
in the sun to draw the marks out.
Glaze the bottom crust of fruit pies
with white of egg and they will not be
soggy.
Use white oilcloth back of the kitchen
table and sink and under books that hold
ware.
It is said that the application of a piece
of ice or even cold water to the lobe of
the ear will stop hic-coughing.
Put a small piece of charcoal in the pot
when boiling cabbage to prevent it filling
the house with the smell.
If when blackening the stove a small
lump of sugar and some soft soap be add¬
ed, it will improve it very much.
Bran or oatmeal should be used when
bathing children suffering with scarlet
fever. - Hall's Journal of Health.
The most effectual remedy for slimy
and greasy drain pipes is copperas dis¬
solved and left to work gradually through
the pipe.
A good cement to fasten on lamp tops
is melted alum; use as soon as melted
and the lamp is ready for use an soon as
the cement is cold.
To cement cracks in stoves mix stove
blacking, flour and cold water. Another
way is, take equal parts of sifted ashes,
clay and salt, and a little water

ground_truth.transcription_unit:
The World's Gold Supply.
The most eminent living writers on the
precious metals. Suess and Soetbeer,
have recently published what the New
York Telegram calls a very alarming
statement. It is to the effect that the
total amount of gold dug out of the
earth annually suffice only to supply
the present demand for that valuable
substance for use in the arts. Not a bit
of the new product of the mines is avail¬
able for coinage. Trinket use and waste
in manufacture exhaust this whole yield.
If this is correct, then gold must vanish
of jewelry often disappear, but are seldom
lost. When through accident they pass
out of the possesion of the well-to-do
they go to the poor and sharp-eyed, who
sell or pawn them. Some jewelry is lost
by fire and some in the sea, and these
losses are absolute and hopeless; but
jewelry otherwise is certain, practically,
all of it, to find its way, sooner or later,
to the pawn-shops or into hands of deal¬
ers in old gold. Thus it is melted up
eventually and reappears again in other
shapes. This is what is termed the "in¬
variable supply" of that metal.
There are a number of unavoidable
causes of loss of gold. The first and most
important of these is by abrasion.
Jewelry loses much weight in that way,
especially rings, which are usually eigh¬
teen karat, and are worn rapidly. Coins
suffer much less but still considerably
from wear. All gold leaf is a total loss
to the gold stock of the world. Where
used for decorative purposes it is never
recovered. It is not employed for filling
teeth nearly as much as formerly, "por¬
ous gold" being substituted. But, of
course, the gold utilized for filling teeth
is a total loss, and in the aggregate it is
enormous in quantity. If it is supposed
that the average dweller in cities of this
country has 50 cents worth of gold in
his or her mouth, which is placing the
figure very low, it will be seen how
great is the waste in this form. Each
succeeding generation takes so many
millions of dollars' worth of the metal
from the world's stock in this way. Some
gold is lost in remelting, though all pos¬
sible means be taken to reduce it to the
lowest possible figure. Not only are the
floors swept and the dirt treated for the
recovery of the yellow substance, but the
wooden planks are burned eventually
with the same object. Even the shoes
of each man who works with the metal
are subjected to the chemistry of fire,
yielding a small "button" of the precious
metal. - Denver Mining Record.
Senator Teller is not disappointed be¬
cause the Berlin silver commission did
not make any recommendations looking
to the restoration of the white metal nor
arrange for another international con¬
ference. "The commission was" says
the Senator, "the outgrowth of the dis¬
content among the agrarian classes and
was appointed by the government in re¬
sponse to the strong pro-silver sentiment
in the agricultural section of Germany.
I have never understood that it was ex¬
pected to do more than collect informa¬
tion in regard to the situation or if it was,
the original purpose that the commission
should make a recommendation. I think
that was abandoned some time since.
The proceedings of the commission are
public, I understand, and of course, will
be of benefit to the silver cause to the
extent that they educate the masses of
the German people. I should have been
gratified if the commission had led to an
international meeting, but it was hardly
to be hoped, in view of the declarations
side of the educating effect of the publi¬
cation of the proceedings, I don't think
the work of the commission will have
any effect, unless it be to increase the
conviction, which is already growing in
this country, that the United States will
have to take this question in hand and
act independently of other nations."
The silver traders in the house say
that the adjournment of the Berlin con¬
ference without definite results is another
evidence that the United States must
execute her own financial policy without
waiting for foreign nations. Some of the
republican leaders of the house who
have recently become identified with the
restoration of silver ideas, regret that
more was not accomplished at Berlin
Mr. Borrows takes this view of the failure
from circulation before long, because the and says that it shows that Germany
output of the gold mines of the world is
diminishing rather than increasing, and
there are few fields left to explore. But
is similarly situated to the United States
in that she cannot act alone. This being
recognized here and in Germany, Mr.
